> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT521 - CANCER BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fundamentals of Cancer Biology - Role of Biomolecules in Cancer - Carbohydrates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🍬 Role of Biomolecules in Cancer — Carbohydrates

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Carbohydrates in cancer biology** refer to monosaccharides, oligosaccharides, polysaccharides, and their conjugates (glycoproteins, glycolipids, proteoglycans) whose structure, expression, and metabolism are aberrantly regulated in malignant transformation, tumor progression, and metastasis.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **PEBBT521 – Cancer Biology**, this topic sits at the intersection of **cellular biochemistry, glycobiology, and oncology**, focusing on how sugar molecules and their conjugates act as **onco-metabolites, signaling ligands, structural ECM components, and clinical biomarkers**.

### Key Glycobiology Terminology (KTU Syllabus Anchors)

| Term | Formal Definition |
|---|---|
| **Glycan** | A polysaccharide or oligosaccharide chain attached to a protein or lipid, forming a glycoconjugate. |
| **Glycosylation** | The enzyme-directed, site-specific covalent attachment of a glycan to a biomolecule. |
| **Glycocalyx** | A dense, sugar-rich coating on the outer leaflet of the plasma membrane composed of glycoproteins, glycolipids, and proteoglycans. |
| **Warburg Effect** | The preferential reliance of cancer cells on aerobic glycolysis for ATP generation, even in the presence of oxygen. |
| **Lectin** | A carbohydrate-binding protein of non-immune origin that decodes glycan information. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** Under the KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 1 of Cancer Biology requires the student to "describe the molecular and cellular basis of cancer, with emphasis on biomolecular players (nucleic acids, proteins, carbohydrates, lipids) involved in initiation and progression." Carbohydrates form the **third biomolecular pillar** after proteins and nucleic acids.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Fingerprint" Analogy

Imagine every human cell is a **citizen in a city**, and carbohydrates on its surface are its **uniform badge** — the colors, patterns, and insignia change based on the cell's identity, role, and state.

- **Normal cell:** Wears a precise, well-defined uniform with the correct badge colors (defined glycan structures).
- **Cancer cell:** Wears a **distorted, irregular uniform** with extra fringes, missing badges, or unusual color patterns (truncated, sialylated, or fucosylated aberrant glycans).

Now imagine the city has a **food supply network** (glucose metabolism). Healthy cells run a "balanced canteen" — they use oxygen to efficiently cook their food (oxidative phosphorylation). Cancer cells, however, **abandon the efficient kitchen** and instead set up **street food stalls** that consume glucose rapidly, even when the main kitchen (mitochondria) is available. This is the **Warburg effect** — and glucose becomes the primary fuel of the tumor.

> [!TIP]
> **Why does this matter clinically?** Because if cancer cells "eat" glucose greedily, we can **feed them a labeled (radioactive) sugar** (FDG) and watch them light up on a PET scan. The carbohydrate story of cancer is, quite literally, what makes modern diagnostic imaging work.

## 1.3 Classification of Carbohydrates Relevant to Cancer

$$
\text{Carbohydrates in Cancer} \longrightarrow 
\begin{cases}
\text{Monosaccharides} \;\; (\text{D-Glucose, D-Mannose, D-Galactose, L-Fucose, Sialic Acid}) \\
\text{Disaccharides} \;\; (\text{Lactose, etc.}) \\
\text{Polysaccharides} \;\; (\text{Hyaluronic Acid, Heparan Sulfate}) \\
\text{Glycoconjugates} 
\begin{cases}
\text{Glycoproteins} \\
\text{Glycolipids (Gangliosides)} \\
\text{Proteoglycans}
\end{cases}
\end{cases}
$$

### 1.3.1 The Five Key Monosaccharides in Tumor Glycobiology

- **D-Glucose** — primary metabolic fuel (Warburg effect substrate)
- **D-Mannose** — anti-tumor metabolite; interferes with glycoprotein synthesis
- **D-Galactose** — component of ABO blood-group antigens; altered in carcinomas
- **L-Fucose** — fucosylated glycans (e.g., sialyl Lewis-a) drive metastasis
- **Sialic Acid (Neu5Ac)** — hypersialylation masks tumor cells from immune detection

---

## 1.4 Why Carbohydrates Are Central to Cancer: The Five Biological Roles

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme module outcomes (CO1: *Understand the molecular basis of cancer*), students must identify the **five mechanistic roles** carbohydrates play in malignancy.

1. **Metabolic Substrate (Bioenergetic role)** — Glucose fuels the Warburg effect.
2. **Cell-Surface Identity (Recognition role)** — Aberrant glycans drive immune evasion and adhesion.
3. **Signaling Ligand (Receptor role)** — Glycan–lectin axes (e.g., Galectin-3) trigger oncogenic cascades.
4. **Structural ECM Component (Architectural role)** — Hyaluronan remodels the tumor microenvironment.
5. **Clinical Biomarker (Diagnostic role)** — CA 19-9, CA 125, CA 15-3, AFP-L3 (fucosylated).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis

## 2.1 Aberrant Glycosylation in Cancer — The "Hallmark of Cancer" Addendum

In 2022, **Hanahan and Weinberg** added *"deregulating cellular metabolism'* and *'enabling replicative immortality'* to the original hallmarks. Aberrant glycosylation is now considered an **emerging enabling characteristic**, as it influences nearly every established hallmark.

### 2.1.1 Types of Glycosylation in Mammalian Cells

$$
\text{Glycosylation} \longrightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{N-Linked} \;\; (\text{Asn-X-Ser/Thr motif, ER-Golgi}) \\
\text{O-Linked} \;\; (\text{Ser/Thr, GalNAc-initiated, Golgi}) \\
\text{C-Mannosylation} \;\; (\text{Trp-C2, ER}) \\
\text{GPI-Anchored} \;\; (\text{C-terminal amide linkage}) \\
\text{O-GlcNAcylation} \;\; (\text{Cytoplasmic, Ser/Thr, single GlcNAc})
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.1.2 Cancer-Associated Glycan Alterations (The "Glycan Code")

| Aberrant Glycan | Biochemical Change | Functional Consequence in Cancer |
|---|---|---|
| **β1,6-branched N-glycans** | Up-regulated by GnT-V (MGAT5) | Promotes growth factor signaling, metastasis |
| **Sialyl Lewis-a (sLe^a / CA 19-9)** | Increased sialylation + fucosylation | E-selectin binding → extravasation |
| **Sialyl Lewis-x (sLe^x)** | Up-regulated on selectin ligands | Hematogenous metastasis |
| **Truncated O-Glycans (Tn, sialyl-Tn)** | Loss of core-1 β1,3-GalT (T-synthase) | Immune evasion, biomarker (e.g., breast cancer) |
| **Hypersialylation** | Over-expression of sialyltransferases (ST3GAL1, ST6GAL1) | Inhibits NK cell, complement attack |
| **Fucosylated AFP (AFP-L3)** | α-1,6-fucosyltransferase (FUT8) up-regulation | Diagnostic of hepatocellular carcinoma |
| **High-mannose N-glycans** | Impaired N-glycan trimming | Recognized by MGL lectin on dendritic cells |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do these changes occur?** Two principal causes: (1) **altered expression of glycosyltransferases/glycosidases** in the ER-Golgi, and (2) **substrate (nucleotide-sugar) flux changes** driven by the hexosamine biosynthetic pathway (HBP).

## 2.2 The Warburg Effect — Glucose as Onco-Metabolite

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** First described by **Otto Warburg (1924, 1956)**, the Warburg effect is the observation that cancer cells metabolize glucose predominantly via **aerobic glycolysis**, generating **lactate** rather than fully oxidizing pyruvate in mitochondria, even when $\text{O}_2$ is abundant.

### 2.2.1 Quantitative Comparison

| Parameter | Normal Differentiated Cell | Cancer Cell (Warburg) |
|---|---|---|
| ATP per glucose | ~**32** | **2** |
| Glucose uptake | Basal | 10–20× increased |
| Lactate output | Low | High |
| Mitochondrial OXPHOS | Dominant | Suppressed |
| Primary pathway | TCA + ETC | Glycolysis → Lactate |

### 2.2.2 Molecular Drivers of the Warburg Effect

$$
\text{Warburg Effect Drivers} \longrightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Oncogenic} \;\; (\text{c-Myc, HIF-1}\alpha\text{, Ras, PI3K/Akt}) \\
\text{Tumor Suppressor Loss} \;\; (\text{p53, PTEN}) \\
\text{Enzymatic} \;\; (\uparrow\text{HK2, }\uparrow\text{PFK1, }\uparrow\text{PKM2, }\downarrow\text{PDH}) \\
\text{Transport} \;\; (\uparrow\text{GLUT1, GLUT3, }\uparrow\text{MCT1/4})
\end{cases}
$$

### 2.2.3 The Hexosamine Biosynthetic Pathway (HBP) — Glucose's "Side Door"

A small fraction (2–5%) of intracellular glucose enters the **HBP**, producing **UDP-GlcNAc**, the substrate for **O-GlcNAcylation** and **N-glycan synthesis**.

$$
\text{Glucose} \to \text{Glucose-6-P} \to \text{Fructose-6-P} \xrightarrow{\text{GFAT}} \text{Glucosamine-6-P} \to \text{UDP-GlcNAc}
$$

> In cancer, **hyperactive HBP** drives hyper-O-GlcNAcylation of oncoproteins (c-Myc, β-catenin) and supports aberrant glycan synthesis.

## 2.3 Glucose Transporters (GLUTs) in Cancer

| Transporter | Tissue (Normal) | Cancer Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **GLUT1** | RBC, brain endothelium | Over-expressed in many carcinomas; PET imaging target |
| **GLUT3** | Neurons | High-affinity; up-regulated in brain tumors |
| **GLUT4** | Muscle, adipose | Insulin-responsive; some breast cancers |
| **GLUT12** | Heart, prostate | Breast cancer marker |

## 2.4 Tumor Microenvironment (TME) Carbohydrates

### 2.4.1 Hyaluronic Acid (Hyaluronan, HA)

- A **non-sulfated GAG** composed of repeating D-glucuronic acid + N-acetyl-D-glucosamine.
- Synthesized by **HAS1, HAS2, HAS3** (Hyaluronan Synthases); degraded by **HYAL1, HYAL2**.
- In cancer, HA **remodels the TME**, promotes **tissue stiffness, angiogenesis, and immune exclusion**.
- Clinical drug: **PEGPH20 (pegvorhyaluronidase alfa)** — degrades HA in pancreatic cancer.

### 2.4.2 Proteoglycans

- **Syndecan-1, Glypican-1, Versican, Perlecan** — all aberrantly expressed.
- **Glypican-1** on exosomes — emerging biomarker for pancreatic cancer liquid biopsy.

## 2.5 Galectins — Lectins Decoding Tumor Glycans

$$
\text{Galectin Family} \longrightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Prototype} \;\; (\text{Galectin-1, -2, -5, -7, -10, -11, -13, -14}) \\
\text{Tandem-repeat} \;\; (\text{Galectin-4, -6, -8, -9, -12}) \\
\text{Chimera} \;\; (\text{Galectin-3})
\end{cases}
$$

**Galectin-3** is a master regulator in cancer:
- Binds β-galactosides on glycoproteins.
- Promotes **tumor cell aggregation, angiogenesis, immune escape**.
- Serum Galectin-3 is a **prognostic marker** in head & neck, breast, and colorectal cancers.

## 2.6 Carbohydrates as Clinical Tumor Markers (KTU Exam Favorite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme examiner frequently asks for **examples of carbohydrate/glycoprotein tumor markers** and their associated cancers.

| Tumor Marker | Carbohydrate Epitope | Associated Cancer |
|---|---|---|
| **CA 19-9** | Sialyl Lewis-a (sLe^a) | Pancreatic, gastric, colorectal |
| **CA 125** | MUC16 glycoprotein | Ovarian |
| **CA 15-3** | MUC1 glycoprotein | Breast |
| **CEA** | GPI-anchored glycoprotein | Colorectal |
| **AFP-L3** | Fucosylated AFP | Hepatocellular carcinoma |
| **PSA** | Glycoprotein | Prostate |
| **β-hCG** | Glycoprotein | Trophoblastic / germ cell |

## 2.7 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are essential for solving numerical/computational questions in the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

| Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|
| $\text{ATP yield}_{\text{anaerobic}} = 2 \text{ ATP/glucose}$ | Glycolysis only | mol ATP/mol glucose |
| $\text{ATP yield}_{\text{aerobic}} = 30-32 \text{ ATP/glucose}$ | Glycolysis + TCA + ETC | mol ATP/mol glucose |
| $v = \dfrac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | Michaelis–Menten (glycolytic enzyme kinetics) | $\mu$mol/min |
| $\Delta G^{\circ\prime} = -n F \Delta E^{\circ\prime}$ | Redox potential of glucose oxidation | kJ/mol |
| $R_f = \dfrac{\text{distance migrated by compound}}{\text{distance migrated by solvent}}$ | TLC / paper chromatography of sugars | dimensionless |
| $[\alpha] = \dfrac{\alpha}{l \cdot c}$ | Specific rotation (sugar identification) | deg·mL·g$^{-1}$·dm$^{-1}$ |
| $\text{PAS} = \text{Periodic Acid–Schiff reaction}$ | Histochemical stain for carbohydrates | qualitative |
| $\text{Lectin} + \text{Glycan} \rightleftharpoons \text{Complex},\; K_d$ | Glycan–lectin binding affinity | M (molar) |

## 2.8 Real-World / Engineering Utility

- **Medical imaging:** $^{18}\text{F-FDG PET}$ (fluorodeoxyglucose positron emission tomography) exploits the Warburg effect for **non-invasive tumor detection**. The "cold" sugar analog is taken up by GLUT1 and trapped in cancer cells.
- **Glycan microarrays:** Used in pharmaceutical R&D to screen anti-glycan antibodies.
- **Biomaterials:** Hyaluronan-based hydrogels are used in **3D tumor spheroid models** for drug screening.
- **Biosensors:** Lectin-based electrochemical sensors detect aberrant glycans on circulating tumor cells (CTCs).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Why Cancer Cells Choose Inefficient Glycolysis

> **A frequent 7-mark KTU question:** *"Despite lower ATP yield, why do cancer cells prefer glycolysis?"* — We derive the bioenergetic and biosynthetic argument.

### Step 1: ATP Production Comparison

Glycolysis (per glucose):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucose} + 2\,\text{NAD}^+ + 2\,\text{ADP} + 2\,\text{P}_i \longrightarrow \\
2\,\text{Pyruvate} + 2\,\text{NADH} + 2\,\text{H}^+ + 2\,\text{ATP} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

Aerobic respiration (per glucose), accounting for shuttle losses and $\text{P/O}$ ratios:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net ATP} &= \underbrace{2}_{\text{substrate-level (glycolysis)}} + \underbrace{2}_{\text{substrate-level (TCA)}} + \underbrace{(2.5 \times 2)}_{\text{NADH from glycolysis (mitochondrial shuttle)}} \\
&\quad + \underbrace{(2.5 \times 6)}_{\text{NADH from TCA}} + \underbrace{(1.5 \times 2)}_{\text{FADH}_2 \text{ from TCA}} \\
&= 2 + 2 + 5 + 15 + 3 \\
&= 30 \text{ ATP (per glucose)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Each $\text{NADH}$ yields ~2.5 ATP; each $\text{FADH}_2$ yields ~1.5 ATP via the electron transport chain.

### Step 2: Warburg-Logic Decision Tree

> Cancer cells "choose" glycolysis **not for ATP**, but for **biosynthetic precursors and rapid turnover**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Glycolytic intermediates} \longrightarrow
\begin{cases}
\text{Glucose-6-P} \to \text{Ribose-5-P} &\text{(Nucleotide synthesis)} \\
\text{3-Phosphoglycerate} \to \text{Serine, Glycine} &\text{(One-carbon metabolism)} \\
\text{Pyruvate} \to \text{Alanine} &\text{(Anaplerosis)} \\
\text{Acetyl-CoA} \to \text{Fatty acids, Cholesterol} &\text{(Membrane biogenesis)} \\
\text{Fructose-6-P} \to \text{UDP-GlcNAc} &\text{(Glycosylation, HBP)}
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion (write in KTU exam):** The Warburg effect provides **faster ATP generation per unit time** (even if less efficient per molecule), supports **macromolecular biosynthesis** required for proliferation, and creates an **acidic microenvironment** (via lactate) that favors invasion and immune suppression.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Michaelis–Menten Kinetics of Hexokinase in Cancer

**Hexokinase (HK)** catalyzes the **committed step** of glycolysis: Glucose $\to$ Glucose-6-P. Cancer cells over-express the **HK2 isoform**, which binds the outer mitochondrial membrane.

$$
v = \frac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU-style)

Given: $V_{\max} = 100\,\mu\text{mol/min}$, $K_m = 0.1\,\text{mM}$, $[S] = 0.5\,\text{mM}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v &= \frac{100 \times 0.5}{0.1 + 0.5} \\
  &= \frac{50}{0.6} \\
  &= 83.33\,\mu\text{mol/min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** At physiological $[S]$ in cancer (high GLUT1-mediated influx), HK2 operates near saturation, ensuring **maximal glycolytic flux** — a hallmark of tumor metabolism.

### Inhibition Constant (Lineweaver–Burk)

For competitive inhibition (e.g., by **2-deoxyglucose, 2-DG**):
$$
v = \frac{V_{\max}[S]}{K_m\!\left(1 + \dfrac{[I]}{K_i}\right) + [S]}
$$

**KTU takeaway:** 2-DG is a **glycolytic inhibitor** under clinical investigation; it is a glucose analog that traps in HK2 reaction, depleting ATP and inducing **cancer cell death**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Lactate–Acidosis Mediated Immune Evasion

Tumor-derived **lactate** acidifies the TME. The **Henderson–Hasselbalch equation** governs the buffering:

$$
\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\!\left(\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)
$$

For lactic acid ($\text{p}K_a = 3.86$):
$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If total lactate} &= 10\,\text{mM, pH} = 6.8 \;\;(\text{typical TME}) \\
\log\!\left(\frac{[\text{Lactate}^-]}{[\text{Lactic acid}]}\right) &= 6.8 - 3.86 = 2.94 \\
\frac{[\text{Lactate}^-]}{[\text{Lactic acid}]} &= 10^{2.94} \approx 871
\end{aligned}
$$

> **95%+ of TME lactate is in the dissociated (acidic) form**, contributing to acidosis, T-cell dysfunction, and M2 macrophage polarization.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Normal vs. Cancer Glycophenotype

| Parameter | Normal Cell | Cancer Cell | KTU 1-line Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glucose uptake | Regulated | ↑↑ via GLUT1/3 | Basis of FDG-PET |
| Lactate production | Low | High (Warburg) | Acidic TME |
| N-glycan branching | Trim, biantennary | β1,6-branched (GnT-V) | Drives metastasis |
| O-glycan truncation | Extended core-1/2 | Tn, sialyl-Tn antigens | Diagnostic marker |
| Sialylation | Controlled | Hypersialylated | Immune evasion |
| Fucosylation | Restricted | ↑ sLe^a, sLe^x | E-selectin binding |
| Hyaluronan (HA) | Tissue homeostasis | ↑ HAS2 synthesis | ECM stiffness, EMT |
| Galectin-3 | Low | ↑ in cytoplasm + secreted | Anti-apoptotic |
| O-GlcNAcylation | Regulated | Hyper-O-GlcNAc of oncoproteins | HBP overdrive |
| Serum tumor markers | Absent | CA 19-9, CA 125, AFP-L3 | Clinical diagnosis |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: Glycan–Lectin Binding Affinity

A research problem: A **fluorescently labeled Galectin-3** binds a **Tn antigen** with dissociation constant $K_d = 1.2 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{M}$ at 25°C. Calculate the **standard free energy change**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta G^{\circ} &= RT \ln K_d \\
R &= 8.314\,\text{J·mol}^{-1}\text{·K}^{-1} \\
T &= 298\,\text{K} \\
\Delta G^{\circ} &= (8.314)(298) \ln(1.2 \times 10^{-6}) \\
&= 2477.6 \times (-13.633) \\
&= -33{,}777\,\text{J/mol} \\
&\approx -33.8\,\text{kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Negative $\Delta G^{\circ}$** indicates a thermodynamically favorable binding — such interactions drive **tumor cell aggregation and metastasis**.

```mermaid
    F1 --> G1[Altered N-Glycans beta 1-6 branching]
    F1 --> G2[High mannose glycans]
    F1 --> G3[Hypersialylated glycans]
    G1 --> H1[Cell surface glycoproteins]
    G2 --> H1
    G3 --> H1
    H1 --> I[Tumor Glycocalyx]
    I --> J1[Immune evasion]
    I --> J2[Selectin mediated extravasation]
    I --> J3[Galectin signaling]

    L --> M2[Invasion and metastasis]
    F2 --> N[Hyper O-GlcNAcylation of oncoproteins]
```


## 4.4 Diagram 4: Metastatic Cascade — Glycan–Selectin Axis

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Primary tumor cell] --> B[Hypersialylated surface]
    B --> C[sLe-a and sLe-x epitopes]
    C --> D[Rolling on endothelium E-selectin and P-selectin]
    D --> E[Firm adhesion via integrins]
    E --> F[Extravasation]
    F --> G[Colonization at distant organ]
    G --> H[Metastatic outgrowth]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "draw the role of carbohydrates in metastasis," always include **E-/P-selectin ligands, sialylated glycans, and hyaluronan-mediated migration** as the three core components.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
**Define the Warburg effect and mention two mechanisms by which cancer cells achieve this metabolic switch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** The Warburg effect, described by **Otto Warburg (1924)**, is the phenomenon wherein cancer cells preferentially metabolize glucose to **lactate via glycolysis**, even in the presence of adequate oxygen (aerobic glycolysis), rather than fully oxidizing pyruvate in mitochondria.

> **Two mechanisms (1 mark):**
> 1. **Up-regulation of GLUT1/HK2/PKM2** to increase glycolytic flux.
> 2. **Loss of p53/PTEN** function, releasing HIF-1α and c-Myc drive on glycolytic genes.
> 3. (Alternative) Mitochondrial dysfunction / suppression of PDH.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**
**List any three carbohydrate or glycoprotein-based serum tumor markers with their associated cancers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Marker | Cancer |
|---|---|
| **CA 19-9** (Sialyl Lewis-a) | Pancreatic adenocarcinoma |
| **CA 125** (MUC16) | Epithelial ovarian carcinoma |
| **AFP-L3** (Lens culinaris-reactive fucosylated AFP) | Hepatocellular carcinoma |

> **Mnemonic:** *CA 19-9 → Pancreas*; *CA 125 → Ovary*; *CA 15-3 → Breast*; *AFP-L3 → Liver*.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, ESE Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

> **"Describe in detail the role of altered glycosylation in cancer. Add a note on the clinical relevance of carbohydrate tumor markers."**

#### **(a) Altered Glycosylation in Cancer (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Concept of Glycosylation (2 marks):**
Glycosylation is the enzymatic attachment of glycans to proteins/lipids in the ER–Golgi. The two principal types are **N-linked** (Asn-X-Ser/Thr, started by oligosaccharyltransferase) and **O-linked** (Ser/Thr, initiated by GalNAc-T).

**Step 2 — Specific Aberrant Glycans in Cancer (3 marks):**
- **β1,6-branched N-glycans:** Over-expression of GnT-V (MGAT5) increases branching, promoting growth factor receptor (EGFR, FGFR) signaling and EMT.
- **Truncated O-glycans (Tn, sialyl-Tn):** Loss of T-synthase (Cosmc chaperone mutation) leads to accumulation of Tn antigens; recognized as **tumor-associated antigens**.
- **Sialyl Lewis-a / -x (sLe^a, sLe^x):** Hypersialylated + fucosylated glycans on cancer cells bind E-/P-selectin on endothelium, enabling **hematogenous metastasis**.

**Step 3 — Functional Consequences (2 marks):**
- Immune evasion (sialic acid inhibits NK cell, complement)
- Increased invasion and metastasis
- Modulation of growth factor signaling
- Resistance to apoptosis

#### **(b) Carbohydrate Tumor Markers — Clinical Relevance (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Define Tumor Markers (1 mark):**
Biochemical substances (often glycoproteins or glycan antigens) produced by tumor cells, detectable in blood, used for **screening, diagnosis, prognosis, and monitoring therapy**.

**Step 2 — List and Elaborate (4 marks):**
- **CA 19-9 (sialyl Lewis-a):** Most widely used marker for **pancreatic adenocarcinoma**; sensitivity ~80%, specificity ~90%. Also elevated in gastric, colorectal, and obstructive jaundice.
- **CA 125 (MUC16):** Gold standard for **epithelial ovarian cancer** monitoring; aids in detecting recurrence post-treatment.
- **CA 15-3 / CA 27.29 (MUC1):** Used in **breast cancer** monitoring, especially metastatic disease.
- **AFP-L3:** Fucosylated alpha-fetoprotein; specific for **hepatocellular carcinoma** (cutoff >10% L3 fraction).
- **CEA (carcinoembryonic antigen):** GPI-anchored glycoprotein used in **colorectal cancer** monitoring.

**Step 3 — Clinical Applications & Limitations (2 marks):**
- **Applications:** Diagnosis adjunct, monitoring therapeutic response, detecting recurrence.
- **Limitations:** Lack of specificity (elevated in benign conditions), not suitable for population screening, should always be correlated with imaging and histology.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **"Explain the Warburg effect in cancer. Discuss the role of glucose transporters and the hexosamine biosynthetic pathway (HBP) in tumor metabolism."**

#### **(a) Warburg Effect (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Definition and Historical Context (2 marks):**
The Warburg effect (Otto Warburg, 1924) describes the **metabolic re-programming** in cancer cells where, despite adequate oxygen, glucose is preferentially converted to **lactate via glycolysis**, generating only 2 ATP/glucose (vs. 30–32 via OXPHOS).

**Step 2 — Molecular Drivers (3 marks):**
- **Oncogenes:** c-Myc, HIF-1α, PI3K/Akt/mTOR, Ras — induce glycolytic enzymes.
- **Tumor suppressor loss:** p53 (↓TIGAR, ↓SCO2), PTEN loss → Akt hyperactivation.
- **Enzymatic shifts:** ↑HK2 (mitochondrial-bound), ↑PFK1, ↑PKM2 (dimeric, low-activity), ↓PDH (via PDK1 phosphorylation).

**Step 3 — Why Inefficient Glycolysis? (2 marks):**
- **Speed:** Faster ATP generation per unit time.
- **Biosynthesis:** Provides intermediates (ribose-5-P, serine, acetyl-CoA) for **nucleotide, lipid, amino acid synthesis**.
- **Acidosis:** Lactate export acidifies TME → invasion, immune suppression.
- **Redox balance:** Regenerates NAD⁺ for sustained glycolysis.

#### **(b) Glucose Transporters (GLUTs) and HBP (7 marks)**

**Step 1 — GLUT Family in Cancer (3 marks):**
- **GLUT1:** Universally up-regulated; rate-limiting for glucose entry; **target of FDG-PET imaging**.
- **GLUT3:** High affinity ($K_m \approx 1.4\,\text{mM}$), up-regulated in gliomas and other aggressive tumors.
- **GLUT4:** Insulin-responsive; expressed in some breast and colon cancers.
- **GLUT12:** Emerging role in breast and prostate cancer; insulin-sensitive.

**Step 2 — Hexosamine Biosynthetic Pathway (3 marks):**
- ~2–5% of glucose enters HBP, producing **UDP-GlcNAc**, the key sugar donor.
- Pathway: Glucose → G6P → F6P → **Glucosamine-6P (via GFAT, the rate-limiting enzyme)** → UDP-GlcNAc.
- **In cancer:** Hyperactive HBP drives **O-GlcNAcylation of oncoproteins** (c-Myc, β-catenin, FoxM1) and supports **aberrant N-/O-glycosylation**.
- Targeting GFAT is a potential anti-cancer strategy.

**Step 3 — Integration (1 mark):**
Glucose entry (GLUT1) → glycolysis (Warburg ATP) + HBP branch (UDP-GlcNAc → O-GlcNAc + glycans) forms a **unified onco-metabolic network**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing CA 19-9 and CA 125** — they are *not* the same. CA 19-9 is a glycan (sLe^a); CA 125 is a glycoprotein (MUC16). (–1 mark)
> 2. **Writing "Warburg = anaerobic glycolysis"** — this is wrong; it is *aerobic* glycolysis. (–2 marks)
> 3. **Forgetting units or cofactors** in glycolysis equations (NAD⁺, ADP, Pᵢ). (–1 mark)
> 4. **Omitting examples** of aberrant glycans — merely defining glycosylation fetches only 2/7 marks. Examiner expects **at least three** specific cancer glycans. (–3 marks)
> 5. **Confusing GLUT isoforms** — GLUT1 is basal, GLUT4 is insulin-sensitive. (–1 mark)
> 6. **Skipping the HBP in Warburg-effect answers** — full credit demands the biosynthetic + glycosylation link. (–2 marks)
> 7. **Writing "Warburg = low ATP"** without explaining *why cancer prefers it* (biosynthesis, TME acidosis). (–1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Fire Revision — Carbohydrates in Cancer (KTU 2024 Scheme)**

- ⭐ **Warburg effect** = *aerobic* glycolysis; cancer cells ferment glucose to lactate even with $\text{O}_2$.
- ⭐ **Glycolysis ATP yield** = **2 ATP/glucose**; aerobic respiration = **30–32 ATP/glucose**.
- ⭐ **GLUT1** is the principal **PET imaging target**; binds $^{18}\text{F-FDG}$.
- ⭐ **Hexosamine Biosynthetic Pathway (HBP)** branches from glycolysis at fructose-6-P → produces **UDP-GlcNAc**.
- ⭐ **O-GlcNAcylation** of oncoproteins (c-Myc, β-catenin) is a hallmark of cancer metabolism.
- ⭐ **GnT-V (MGAT5)** up-regulation → **β1,6-branched N-glycans** → metastasis.
- ⭐ **Tn / sialyl-Tn antigens** = truncated O-glycans; appear due to **T-synthase / Cosmc** loss.
- ⭐ **sialyl Lewis-a (sLe^a)** = **CA 19-9** → pancreatic cancer.
- ⭐ **sialyl Lewis-x (sLe^x)** = E-/P-selectin ligand → **hematogenous metastasis**.
- ⭐ **Hypersialylation** → immune evasion (inhibits NK cells, complement).
- ⭐ **Hyaluronan (HA)** = non-sulfated GAG; **HAS2** over-expression in tumors → ECM stiffness, EMT.
- ⭐ **Galectin-3** = master tumor lectin; binds β-galactosides; pro-survival, pro-angiogenic.
- ⭐ **CA 125** = MUC16 glycoprotein → **ovarian cancer**.
- ⭐ **CA 15-3** = MUC1 glycoprotein → **breast cancer**.
- ⭐ **AFP-L3** = fucosylated AFP → **hepatocellular carcinoma**.
- ⭐ **CEA** = GPI-anchored glycoprotein → **colorectal cancer**.
- ⭐ **Glypican-1** on exosomes → emerging **pancreatic cancer** liquid biopsy marker.
- ⭐ **2-Deoxyglucose (2-DG)** = glycolysis inhibitor; **PEGPH20** = hyaluronidase; both are **anti-glycan therapies** under clinical trials.
- ⭐ **HIF-1α** (stabilized in hypoxia) drives GLUT1, HK2, LDHA → reinforces Warburg.
- ⭐ **p53 loss** removes brake on glycolysis (↓TIGAR, ↓SCO2).
- ⭐ **Glycocalyx** = sugar-rich membrane coat; altered in cancer → recognition, signaling, adhesion.
- ⭐ KTU-mandated keywords to use: **glycosylation, glycocalyx, lectin, glycan, Galectin-3, UDP-GlcNAc, GFAT, GLUT1, Warburg, hypoxia, HIF-1α, metastasis, biomarker.**

> ✅ **Mnemonic to remember the 5 roles of carbohydrates in cancer:**
> **"M-S-R-A-B"** → **M**etabolic, **S**urface identity, **R**eceptor/signaling, **A**rchitectural (ECM), **B**iomarker.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Proteins and Lipids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT521 - CANCER BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Proteins and Lipids

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Proteins — The Molecular Workhorses of the Cell

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> Proteins are **high-molecular-weight biological macromolecules** (typically 5 kDa to >500 kDa) composed of one or more **long, unbranched chains of amino acid residues** linked together by **peptide bonds** (a specific type of amide bond formed by a condensation/dehydration reaction between the α-carboxyl group of one amino acid and the α-amino group of another, releasing one molecule of water).

The **general empirical formula** of an amino acid residue incorporated into a polypeptide backbone is:

$$\text{Residue Formula} = \text{C}_{n}\text{H}_{2n-1}\text{NO}$$

where $n$ represents the number of carbons in the original amino acid. Since each peptide bond formation releases one $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, this compact formula helps in quick stoichiometric checks in biochemistry.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Beaded Necklace of Life

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a **train** where each **coach (carriage)** is an **amino acid**. The couplers linking the coaches are the **peptide bonds**. Just as a train can travel long distances only when coaches are properly linked and the engine (the 3D folded shape) is functional, proteins perform their jobs — catalysis, signaling, transport, structural support — only when amino acids are properly linked **AND** folded into the correct 3D shape. A single misfolded "coach" can derail the entire system — this is the molecular basis of diseases like cancer, Alzheimer's, and cystic fibrosis.

### The 20 Standard α-Amino Acids — Building Blocks

All 20 standard amino acids share a **common backbone**:

$$\text{H}_2\text{N} - \overset{\alpha}{\text{C}}\text{H}(\text{R}) - \text{COOH}$$

- **α-carbon** = central carbon
- **R group (side chain)** = the variable part that defines each amino acid
- **α-amino group** ($-\text{NH}_2$) and **α-carboxyl group** ($-\text{COOH}$) are common to all

### Classification of Amino Acids (by R-group polarity at physiological pH 7.4)

| Category | Examples | Property |
|----------|----------|----------|
| Nonpolar (Hydrophobic) | Alanine, Valine, Leucine, Isoleucine, Methionine, Phenylalanine, Tryptophan, Proline | R groups avoid water |
| Polar Uncharged | Serine, Threonine, Cysteine, Tyrosine, Asparagine, Glutamine | R groups H-bond with water |
| Positively Charged (Basic) | Lysine, Arginine, Histidine | R group carries **+1** charge at pH 7.4 |
| Negatively Charged (Acidic) | Aspartate, Glutamate | R group carries **−1** charge at pH 7.4 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cancer Biology Connection:**
> **Oncogenes** (e.g., *RAS*, *MYC*, *EGFR*) and **tumor suppressor genes** (e.g., *p53*, *Rb*, *BRCA1*) all encode proteins. The **p53 protein** is often called the "guardian of the genome" because it triggers cell cycle arrest and apoptosis in response to DNA damage — a critical anti-cancer defense. Mutations in the *TP53* gene occur in **>50%** of all human cancers.

## 1.2 Lipids — The Versatile Hydrophobic Biomolecules

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> Lipids are a **structurally diverse group of naturally occurring hydrophobic or amphipathic (containing both hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions) organic compounds** that are **insoluble in water** but soluble in **nonpolar organic solvents** such as chloroform, ether, and benzene. They serve as **energy storage molecules**, **structural components of biological membranes**, **signaling molecules**, and **insulating agents**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Cell's "Walls, Wires, and Warehouses"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Think of a city:
> - **Phospholipids** are the **bricks and mortar** of the cell's walls (the plasma membrane).
> - **Triglycerides** are the city's **oil/fuel reserves** stored in underground tanks (adipocytes).
> - **Steroid hormones** (e.g., estrogen, testosterone) are the **city's communication cables** — chemical messengers traveling through the bloodstream.
> - **Cholesterol** is the **rebar** embedded in the membrane bricks, controlling fluidity and rigidity.

### Major Classes of Lipids

1. **Simple Lipids**: Fatty acids, triglycerides (triacylglycerols), waxes
2. **Compound Lipids**: Phospholipids, glycolipids, lipoproteins
3. **Derived Lipids**: Steroids (cholesterol, steroid hormones), terpenes, fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K)

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Distinction:**
> Unlike proteins, nucleic acids, and carbohydrates, lipids are **NOT polymers** in the classical sense. They are **not built from a uniform set of repeating monomeric units** linked by a single type of covalent bond. The only polymeric lipid-like molecule is **polyprenol** (found in bacterial membranes).

### Cancer Biology Connection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Lipid metabolism is **reprogrammed in cancer cells** (a recognized hallmark of cancer). Tumor cells exhibit:
> - **Increased de novo fatty acid synthesis** (lipogenesis), even in the presence of exogenous fatty acids
> - **Overexpression of lipogenic enzymes**: ATP-citrate lyase (ACLY), acetyl-CoA carboxylase (ACC), fatty acid synthase (FASN)
> - **Altered phospholipid composition** affecting membrane fluidity, signaling, and drug resistance
> - **Cholesterol-rich lipid rafts** that cluster receptor tyrosine kinases (e.g., EGFR) and amplify oncogenic signaling

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of Proteins (4 Levels)

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **HIGH-YIELD topic** for KTU 2024 examinations. Memorize all four levels.

### Level 1 — Primary Structure
- **Definition:** The **linear sequence of amino acids** linked by **peptide bonds** in a polypeptide chain.
- **Bond type:** **Covalent peptide (amide) bond** — formed by a **dehydration (condensation) reaction**.
- **Stabilized by:** Covalent bonds only.
- **Cancer relevance:** A single amino acid substitution (e.g., **Val → Glu at position 600** in BRAF) creates the constitutively active **BRAF V600E** mutant, found in ~60% of melanomas.

### Level 2 — Secondary Structure
- **Definition:** Local, regular folding patterns of the polypeptide backbone stabilized by **hydrogen bonds** between the **backbone amide H** and **backbone carbonyl O**.
- **Two main types:**
  - **α-helix:** Right-handed, **3.6 amino acid residues per turn**, pitch = **5.4 Å**, H-bonds between residue *i* and *i+4*.
  - **β-pleated sheet:** Strands run parallel or antiparallel; H-bonds form between adjacent strands.

### Level 3 — Tertiary Structure
- **Definition:** The **overall 3D folding** of a single polypeptide chain, determined by interactions among the **R groups (side chains)**.
- **Stabilizing forces:** Hydrophobic interactions, H-bonds, ionic (salt bridges), disulfide bonds (between two cysteine residues), van der Waals forces.

### Level 4 — Quaternary Structure
- **Definition:** The **assembly of multiple folded polypeptide subunits** into a functional protein complex.
- **Examples:** Hemoglobin (4 subunits: 2α + 2β), DNA polymerase, ribosome, p53 tetramer.

### Summary Table — Forces Stabilizing Protein Structure

| Level | Bonds/Forces Involved | Strength |
|-------|----------------------|----------|
| Primary | Covalent **peptide bonds** | Very strong (~200 kJ/mol) |
| Secondary | **Hydrogen bonds** (backbone) | Weak (~5 kJ/mol) each, but cumulative |
| Tertiary | H-bonds, **hydrophobic interactions**, ionic, **disulfide bridges**, van der Waals | Mixed |
| Quaternary | Same as tertiary, plus subunit-subunit interfaces | Mixed |

## 2.2 Hierarchical Organization of Lipid Architecture

### Fatty Acids — The Fundamental Lipid Building Blocks

**General formula of a saturated fatty acid:**

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}\text{COOH} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \text{CH}_3-(\text{CH}_2)_{n-2}-\text{COOH}$$

**General formula of an unsaturated fatty acid (one double bond):**

$$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-1}\text{COOH}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Relationship:** Each **double bond** removes **2 hydrogen atoms** from the saturated formula. For $k$ double bonds, the formula becomes $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1-2k}\text{COOH}$.

### Saponification Number (SN) — KTU Important

> [!NOTE]
> **Saponification** is the alkaline hydrolysis of a fat/oil (triglyceride) using a strong base (NaOH or KOH) to produce **soap** (the sodium/potassium salt of fatty acids) and **glycerol**.

**Reaction:**

$$\text{Triglyceride} + 3\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow 3\,\text{RCOONa} + \text{Glycerol}$$

$$\text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OOCR})_3 + 3\,\text{NaOH} \longrightarrow 3\,\text{RCOONa} + \text{C}_3\text{H}_5(\text{OH})_3$$

**Definition of Saponification Number:**

$$\text{SN} = \frac{\text{Mass of KOH (in mg) required to saponify 1 g of fat}}{\text{Mass of fat sample (1 g)}}$$

$$\boxed{\text{SN} = \frac{(V_{\text{KOH}})(N_{\text{KOH}})(56.1)}{W}}$$

where:
- $V_{\text{KOH}}$ = volume of KOH in mL
- $N_{\text{KOH}}$ = normality of KOH
- **56.1** = equivalent weight of KOH (g/eq)
- $W$ = weight of fat sample in grams

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inverse Relationship Rule:** The **higher the saponification number, the lower the average molecular weight** of the fatty acids in the fat. This is because shorter-chain fatty acids require more moles of KOH per gram to saponify.

### Acid Number (AN) — KTU Important

$$\boxed{\text{Acid Number} = \frac{(V_{\text{KOH}})(N_{\text{KOH}})(56.1)}{W}}$$

where $V_{\text{KOH}}$ is now the volume of KOH used to **neutralize the free fatty acids** (NOT to saponify the triglyceride). The acid number measures **lipid degradation/oxidation** — relevant to food quality and cancer risk (oxidized lipids can be carcinogenic).

### Iodine Number (IN) — KTU Important

**Definition:** The **grams of iodine (I₂) absorbed by 100 g of fat/oil**. It is a direct measure of the **degree of unsaturation** (number of C=C double bonds).

$$\boxed{\text{IN} = \frac{(V_{\text{blank}} - V_{\text{sample}})(N_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3})(126.9)}{W} \times 100}$$

where **126.9** is the atomic weight of iodine.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cancer Biology Note:** **Polyunsaturated fats** (high iodine number) are susceptible to **lipid peroxidation**, generating reactive aldehydes (e.g., **4-hydroxynonenal, malondialdehyde**) that form DNA adducts and promote carcinogenesis. Conversely, **monounsaturated fats** (e.g., oleic acid in olive oil) are associated with reduced cancer risk.

### Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation for Amino Acids

This is **critical** for KTU exams — used to determine the **charge state of amino acids, peptides, and proteins** at any given pH.

For the dissociation of an acid HA ⇌ H⁺ + A⁻:

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)}$$

For an amino acid with both acidic and basic groups (zwitterion equilibria):

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}I \quad \text{at the isoelectric point, where } [\text{cation}] = [\text{anion}]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Result:** At pH = pI, the **net charge of the amino acid/protein is zero**, and it will **NOT migrate in an electric field**. This principle is exploited in **isoelectric focusing**, a high-resolution protein separation technique used in cancer biomarker discovery.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical / Chemical Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------------------------------|--------------|
| 1 | Peptide bond formation | $\text{R}_1\text{-NH}_2 + \text{HOOC-R}_2 \rightarrow \text{R}_1\text{-NH-CO-R}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ | Loss of **1 H₂O per peptide bond** |
| 2 | Amino acid residue formula | $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-1}\text{NO}$ | For $n$-carbon amino acid |
| 3 | Average residue weight in protein | $\approx \textbf{110 Da}$ | Used for spectrophotometric quantitation |
| 4 | Beer–Lambert Law (protein quantitation) | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$ | $\varepsilon_{280} \approx 1$ for 1 mg/mL IgG |
| 5 | Henderson–Hasselbalch | $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log \frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$ | Defines buffering, ionization |
| 6 | Saponification Number | $\text{SN} = \frac{V \cdot N \cdot 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH per g fat |
| 7 | Acid Number | $\text{AN} = \frac{V \cdot N \cdot 56.1}{W}$ | mg KOH per g fat (free FA only) |
| 8 | Iodine Number | $\text{IN} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \cdot N \cdot 126.9 \times 100}{W}$ | g I₂ per 100 g fat |
| 9 | Saturated fatty acid formula | $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}\text{COOH}$ | $n \geq 1$ |
| 10 | Unsaturated (k double bonds) | $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1-2k}\text{COOH}$ | Each C=C removes 2H |
| 11 | Phospholipid amphipathy | Hydrophilic head + 2 hydrophobic tails | Forms bilayers in water |
| 12 | Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC) | Concentration above which micelles form | Detergents, bile salts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No "|" pipes inside any table cell** — all absolute values and vertical bars are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to preserve markdown table integrity (KTU 2024 compliance).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering & Medical Utility

- **Biopharmaceutical industry:** Recombinant protein drugs (insulin, monoclonal antibodies like trastuzumab for HER2+ breast cancer) are produced by exploiting primary structure knowledge.
- **Drug design:** Knowledge of **lipid rafts** (cholesterol/sphingolipid microdomains) helps design drugs that disrupt oncogenic receptor clustering.
- **Food & oil industry:** Saponification, acid, and iodine numbers are **routine QC parameters** for edible oils.
- **Diagnostics:** Protein isoelectric points and lipid profiles are biomarkers in clinical cancer screening.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation

The Henderson–Hasselbalch equation is derived directly from the **acid dissociation constant** definition. This is a **frequently asked derivation** in KTU exams.

### Starting Point — Acid Dissociation Constant

For a weak acid $\text{HA}$ dissociating in water:

$$\text{HA} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+ + \text{A}^-$$

The acid dissociation constant is defined as:

$$K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

### Step 1 — Take the negative logarithm (base 10) of both sides

$$-\log K_a = -\log\left(\frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}\right)$$

### Step 2 — Apply log rules

$$-\log K_a = -\log[\text{H}^+] - \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

### Step 3 — Recognize the standard definitions

Since $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$ and $\text{p}K_a = -\log K_a$:

$$\text{p}K_a = \text{pH} - \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange to solve for pH

$$\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log\frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$$

$$\boxed{\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[\text{conjugate base}]}{[\text{conjugate acid}]}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** In a KTU exam, you may be asked to derive this in 3–4 steps. Memorize the starting equation $K_a = \frac{[\text{H}^+][\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$ and then apply $-\log$ to both sides — that's the full derivation.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Isoelectric Point of Glycine

Glycine has:
- $\text{p}K_a(\text{COOH}) = 2.34$
- $\text{p}K_a(\text{NH}_3^+) = 9.60$

The **isoelectric point (pI)** is the average of the two $\text{p}K_a$ values flanking the **zwitterion (net zero charge) species**:

$$\boxed{\text{p}I = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}}$$

### Step-by-Step Calculation

$$\text{p}I = \frac{2.34 + 9.60}{2} = \frac{11.94}{2} = 5.97$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule:** For a **neutral** amino acid (no ionizable R group), $\text{p}I = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}$. For an **acidic** amino acid (e.g., aspartate), $\text{p}I = \frac{\text{p}K_{a1} + \text{p}K_{a(R)}}{2}$. For a **basic** amino acid (e.g., lysine), $\text{p}I = \frac{\text{p}K_{a(R)} + \text{p}K_{a2}}{2}$.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Saponification Number

A 2.5 g sample of a fat is saponified with 25 mL of 0.5 N KOH. The excess KOH is back-titrated with 0.5 N HCl, requiring 10 mL. Calculate the **saponification number**.

### Step 1 — Determine the KOH actually consumed in saponification

The total KOH added = $V \cdot N = 25 \times 0.5 = 12.5$ meq.
The excess KOH back-titrated = $10 \times 0.5 = 5.0$ meq.

$$V_{\text{used}} = 12.5 - 5.0 = 7.5 \text{ meq (or mL of 0.5 N)}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the saponification number formula

$$\text{SN} = \frac{V_{\text{used}} \cdot N_{\text{KOH}} \cdot 56.1}{W}$$

$$\text{SN} = \frac{7.5 \times 0.5 \times 56.1}{2.5}$$

$$\text{SN} = \frac{210.375}{2.5} = 84.15 \text{ mg KOH/g fat}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** An SN of ~85 indicates a fat with **medium-chain to long-chain fatty acids** (typical of coconut oil: 250–260; butter: 210–230; soybean oil: 190–200). Higher SN → shorter average FA chain length.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Protein Structural Hierarchy vs. Lipid Organization

| Feature | Proteins | Lipids |
|---------|----------|--------|
| Monomer | **Amino acids** (20 standard) | **Fatty acids / glycerol / sterols** (no uniform monomer) |
| Polymer? | **YES** (polypeptide) | **NO** (not classical polymers) |
| Primary bond | **Peptide (amide) bond** (covalent) | **Ester bond** in triglycerides/glycerophospholipids |
| Solubility in water | Variable (depends on R groups) | **Insoluble** (hydrophobic) |
| Solubility in ether/chloroform | Generally insoluble | **Soluble** |
| Functional role | Catalysis, structure, signaling, transport, defense | Energy storage, membranes, signaling, insulation |
| Cancer link | Oncogenes/tumor suppressors encode proteins | Lipid metabolism reprogrammed in tumors |
| Structural hierarchy | 4 levels (1°, 2°, 3°, 4°) | No strict hierarchy; classified by chemical nature |
| Structural diversity driver | 20 different R groups | Chain length, saturation, head group chemistry |

## 3.5 Number of Peptide Bonds in a Polypeptide — Worked Formula

If a protein has $N$ amino acid residues:

$$\boxed{\text{Number of peptide bonds} = N - 1}$$

$$\boxed{\text{Number of water molecules released} = N - 1}$$

**Example:** Insulin (mature, active form) has 51 amino acid residues organized as two chains (A chain: 21 aa; B chain: 30 aa) connected by **2 interchain disulfide bonds** and **1 intrachain disulfide bond** in chain A.

$$\text{Peptide bonds} = 51 - 2 = 49$$

(since there are 2 polypeptide chains, total peptide bonds = $(21-1) + (30-1) = 20 + 29 = 49$.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cancer Connection:** Insulin and IGF-1 (insulin-like growth factor 1) signaling **promotes tumor growth**. Epidemiological studies link **hyperinsulinemia** (Type 2 diabetes, obesity) with increased risk of **breast, colorectal, and pancreatic cancers**.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Define the following with one example each:**
**(a)** Peptide bond  
**(b)** Amphipathic lipid  
**(c)** Denaturation of a protein

### Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per sub-part)

**(a) Peptide Bond (1 mark):** A peptide bond is the **covalent amide bond (–CO–NH–)** formed between the **α-carboxyl group of one amino acid** and the **α-amino group of another** amino acid, with the **elimination of one water molecule** (condensation reaction).
*Example:* The bond between glycine and alanine in the dipeptide glycylalanine.

**(b) Amphipathic Lipid (1 mark):** A lipid molecule that contains **both a hydrophilic (polar) region and a hydrophobic (nonpolar) region** within the same molecule.
*Example:* **Phosphatidylcholine** — hydrophilic choline head group + two hydrophobic fatty acid tails. It is the major phospholipid of cell membranes.

**(c) Denaturation (1 mark):** The process by which a **protein loses its native 3D tertiary and/or secondary structure** (and thus its biological function) due to disruption of **non-covalent interactions** (H-bonds, hydrophobic, ionic), while the **primary structure (peptide bonds) remains intact**.
*Example:* Egg white albumin denatures and turns opaque upon boiling. Heat, pH extremes, urea, and heavy metals can cause denaturation.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between:**
**(a)** Saturated and unsaturated fatty acids (2 marks)  
**(b)** Primary and secondary structure of proteins (1 mark)

### Model Answer (3 marks)

**(a) Saturated vs Unsaturated Fatty Acids (2 marks):**

| Feature | Saturated | Unsaturated |
|---------|-----------|-------------|
| C=C double bonds | **Absent** | **Present** (one = mono; more = poly) |
| Hydrogen count (for same n) | **Maximum** (2n+1) | **Reduced by 2 per C=C** |
| State at room temperature | Usually **solid** (e.g., butter, lard) | Usually **liquid** (e.g., olive oil, sunflower oil) |
| Source (typical) | Animal fats | Plant and fish oils |
| Iodine number | **Low** | **High** (more I₂ absorbed) |
| Health/cancer effect | Excess linked to **cardiovascular risk** | Polyunsaturated (PUFA ω-3) may reduce inflammation |

**(b) Primary vs Secondary Structure (1 mark):**

- **Primary structure:** The **linear sequence** of amino acids in a polypeptide chain, held by **covalent peptide bonds**. Determines the identity and ultimate 3D fold of the protein.
- **Secondary structure:** The **local, regular folding** of the polypeptide backbone (α-helix, β-sheet) stabilized by **hydrogen bonds** between **backbone amide H and carbonyl O atoms**.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions offer **internal choice** between two alternatives from the same module. We provide **both** Question A and Question B with complete sub-parts and model solutions.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Adapted]

**(a) Describe in detail the four levels of protein structure with suitable diagrams and examples. Discuss the forces stabilizing each level.** (7 marks)

**(b) Explain the structure of a phospholipid. How does the amphipathic nature of phospholipids lead to the formation of a lipid bilayer in aqueous environments? Discuss the role of cholesterol in membrane fluidity.** (7 marks)

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Four Levels of Protein Structure (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):** Proteins are polypeptide polymers of amino acids linked by peptide bonds. They exhibit four hierarchical levels of structural organization — primary, secondary, tertiary, and quaternary — each stabilized by specific chemical forces.

**Primary Structure (1.5 marks):**
- The **linear sequence** of amino acids in a polypeptide chain.
- Stabilized solely by **covalent peptide bonds** (~200 kJ/mol).
- Determined by the gene (DNA → mRNA → protein via translation).
- *Example:* The primary structure of insulin (51 aa in two chains) was the first protein sequenced (Sanger, 1955; Nobel Prize). A single amino acid substitution in the β-globin chain (Glu→Val at position 6) causes **sickle cell anemia**.

**Secondary Structure (1.5 marks):**
- Local folding patterns of the polypeptide backbone.
- Two principal types: **α-helix** (3.6 residues/turn, 5.4 Å pitch, H-bonds between residues *i* and *i+4*) and **β-pleated sheet** (H-bonds between adjacent strands, parallel or antiparallel).
- Stabilized by **hydrogen bonds** between backbone C=O and N–H groups.
- *Example:* α-keratin (rich in α-helix) is found in hair and nails; fibroin (β-sheet) makes up silk.

**Tertiary Structure (1.5 marks):**
- The **3D fold** of a single polypeptide chain, determined by R-group interactions.
- Stabilizing forces: **hydrophobic interactions** (major driving force), H-bonds, ionic (salt bridges), van der Waals, and **disulfide bonds (–S–S–)** between two cysteine residues.
- *Example:* **Lysozyme, myoglobin, p53** — all are single-chain, globular, tertiary-structure proteins.

**Quaternary Structure (1.5 marks):**
- The association of **two or more folded polypeptide subunits** into a functional complex.
- Same forces as tertiary, plus **subunit-subunit interface** interactions.
- *Examples:* **Hemoglobin** (2α + 2β subunits, 4 heme groups), **DNA polymerase** (multi-subunit), **p53** (active as a **tetramer**), immunoglobulins (2H + 2L chains).

> [!NOTE]
> **Cancer Link (Bonus Point):** Many oncogenic mutations (e.g., *RAS*, *p53*) disrupt the tertiary or quaternary structure of the encoded protein, leading to loss of normal function or gain of function.

#### Part (b) — Phospholipid Structure and Bilayer (7 marks)

**Phospholipid Structure (2.5 marks):**
- A phospholipid consists of:
  - A **glycerol backbone** (or sphingosine in sphingolipids)
  - **Two fatty acid tails** (hydrophobic, nonpolar) — usually one saturated and one unsaturated
  - A **phosphate group** (polar) esterified to the third glycerol carbon
  - An **alcohol head group** attached to the phosphate (e.g., choline, ethanolamine, serine, inositol)
- *Example:* **Phosphatidylcholine** (PC), also called lecithin.

**Amphipathic Nature and Bilayer Formation (3 marks):**
- The **polar head** (hydrophilic) interacts favorably with water; the **nonpolar tails** (hydrophobic) avoid water.
- In aqueous environments, phospholipids self-assemble into a **bilayer**:
  - Tails face **inward** (toward each other) to minimize water contact.
  - Heads face **outward**, contacting water on both the extracellular and cytoplasmic sides.
- This self-assembly is **thermodynamically driven** by the **hydrophobic effect** (entropy increase as water molecules are released from ordering around nonpolar tails).
- The bilayer is the structural basis of the **fluid mosaic model** of biological membranes (Singer–Nicolson, 1972).

**Cholesterol and Membrane Fluidity (1.5 marks):**
- Cholesterol inserts into the bilayer with its **hydroxyl (–OH) group** near the phospholipid head and its **rigid steroid ring + hydrocarbon tail** among the fatty acyl chains.
- **At high temperatures:** Cholesterol **restricts** the movement of phospholipid tails, **decreasing** fluidity and preventing the membrane from becoming too permeable.
- **At low temperatures:** Cholesterol **disrupts** the tight packing of fatty acid tails, **increasing** fluidity and preventing the membrane from solidifying.
- Therefore, cholesterol acts as a **membrane fluidity buffer**.
- **Cancer relevance:** Cholesterol-rich **lipid rafts** cluster receptor tyrosine kinases (EGFR, HER2) and amplify mitogenic signaling.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Adapted]

**(a) Define saponification number, acid number, and iodine number. How are they determined experimentally? Discuss the relationship between saponification number and average molecular weight of fatty acids in a fat.** (7 marks)

**(b) Write short notes on:**
**(i) Essential fatty acids and their biological importance** (3.5 marks)  
**(ii) Oncogenic and tumor suppressor proteins with examples** (3.5 marks)

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Lipid Quality Parameters (7 marks)

**Saponification Number (SN) (2.5 marks):**

**Definition:** The saponification number is defined as the **number of milligrams of KOH required to completely saponify (hydrolyze) 1 gram of fat or oil**.

**Experimental determination:**
1. A known weight ($W$ g) of the fat is refluxed with a known **excess volume** ($V_1$ mL) of standardized **KOH** (normality $N$).
2. After saponification, the **excess KOH** is back-titrated with standardized **HCl** ($N_2$); volume used = $V_2$ mL.
3. Volume of KOH consumed by the fat = $V_1 - (V_2 \cdot N_2/N)$.

**Formula:**

$$\text{SN} = \frac{V_{\text{used}} \cdot N_{\text{KOH}} \cdot 56.1}{W}$$

**Relationship with Molecular Weight (1.5 marks):**

- The saponification reaction has a **1:3 stoichiometry** between the triglyceride and KOH (3 ester bonds per glycerol).
- One mole of KOH (56.1 g = 56,100 mg) reacts with one mole of ester bonds.
- Therefore, the **average molecular weight of the fatty acids** in the fat is **inversely proportional to the saponification number**:

$$M_{\text{FA, avg}} \approx \frac{3 \times 56{,}100}{\text{SN}}$$

- **High SN → Low molecular weight fatty acids** (e.g., coconut oil, SN ≈ 255 → short-chain FAs).
- **Low SN → High molecular weight fatty acids** (e.g., rapeseed oil, SN ≈ 175 → long-chain FAs).

**Acid Number (AN) (1 mark):**

- Defined as the **mg of KOH required to neutralize the free fatty acids in 1 g of fat**.
- Determined by **direct titration** of the fat dissolved in a neutral solvent (ethanol/ether) with standard KOH, using **phenolphthalein** as indicator.
- Indicates **lipid degradation** (rancidity); high AN = poor quality oil.
- **Cancer relevance:** Oxidized/rancid fats contain **lipid peroxidation products** (e.g., 4-HNE) that are **carcinogenic** and form mutagenic DNA adducts.

**Iodine Number (IN) (2 marks):**

- Defined as the **grams of I₂ absorbed by 100 g of fat or oil**.
- Measured by **Wijs method** (or Hanus method): the fat is treated with a known excess of ICl (or IBr) solution; the unreacted halogen is back-titrated with **standard sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃)**, using starch indicator.
- **Higher IN → more C=C double bonds → more unsaturated fat**.
- Examples: Linseed oil (IN ≈ 175–200, highly unsaturated); olive oil (IN ≈ 80, monounsaturated); coconut oil (IN ≈ 10, highly saturated).

$$\text{IN} = \frac{(V_b - V_s) \cdot N_{\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3} \cdot 126.9 \times 100}{W}$$

#### Part (b) — Short Notes (7 marks)

**(i) Essential Fatty Acids and Biological Importance (3.5 marks):**

- **Definition:** Fatty acids that the human body **cannot synthesize de novo** (because mammals lack the necessary desaturase enzymes) and **must be obtained from the diet** — hence "essential."
- **Two families:**
  - **ω-3 (n-3) family:** α-linolenic acid (ALA, 18:3) → EPA, DHA. Sources: flaxseed, walnuts, fatty fish.
  - **ω-6 (n-6) family:** Linoleic acid (LA, 18:2) → arachidonic acid. Sources: vegetable oils, nuts.
- **Biological roles:**
  - **Membrane structure** (phospholipid composition, fluidity).
  - **Precursors of eicosanoids** (prostaglandins, leukotrienes, thromboxanes) that regulate inflammation, immunity, and vascular tone.
  - **Brain and retinal development** (DHA is critical for neural tissue).
  - **Anti-inflammatory ω-3 eicosanoids** (PGE3, LTB5) may reduce cancer-related chronic inflammation.
- **Cancer link:** Arachidonic acid–derived **PGE2** promotes tumor growth, angiogenesis, and immune suppression; **COX-2 inhibitors** (aspirin, celecoxib) reduce colorectal cancer risk.

**(ii) Oncogenic and Tumor Suppressor Proteins (3.5 marks):**

- **Oncogenes** encode **oncoproteins** that, when mutated/overexpressed, **drive uncontrolled cell proliferation**. They are dominant at the cellular level (a single mutant allele is sufficient).
  - *Examples:*
    - **RAS family** (H-RAS, K-RAS, N-RAS) — small GTPase, mutated in ~30% of all cancers; **K-RAS G12D** is common in pancreatic cancer.
    - **MYC** — transcription factor, amplified in Burkitt lymphoma (t(8;14) translocation).
    - **EGFR / HER2** — receptor tyrosine kinases, overexpressed in glioblastoma and breast cancer.
    - **BCR-ABL** — fusion oncoprotein from t(9;22) in chronic myeloid leukemia (CML); targeted by **imatinib (Gleevec)**.
    - **Cyclin D1** — cell cycle G1/S driver, overexpressed in mantle cell lymphoma and breast cancer.

- **Tumor suppressor genes** encode proteins that **normally restrain cell division, promote DNA repair, or trigger apoptosis**. They are **recessive** at the cellular level (both alleles usually must be inactivated — Knudson's "two-hit hypothesis").
  - *Examples:*
    - **p53** — "guardian of the genome"; transcription factor that arrests the cell cycle (via p21) and triggers apoptosis (via BAX, PUMA) in response to DNA damage. **Most frequently mutated gene in human cancer (~50%).**
    - **Rb (retinoblastoma protein)** — binds and inhibits E2F transcription factors; loss leads to retinoblastoma, osteosarcoma, small cell lung cancer.
    - **BRCA1/BRCA2** — DNA double-strand break repair; mutations predispose to breast and ovarian cancers.
    - **PTEN** — lipid phosphatase that opposes PI3K/AKT signaling; frequently deleted in prostate, endometrial, and brain cancers.
    - **APC** — negative regulator of Wnt/β-catenin pathway; mutated in familial adenomatous polyposis and sporadic colorectal cancer.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing "peptide bond" with "hydrogen bond":** Peptide bond is **covalent** and forms the **primary structure**. Hydrogen bonds stabilize the **secondary** (and sometimes tertiary) structure.
> 2. **Mixing up the lipid numbers:** Many students incorrectly equate saponification number with iodine number. Remember: **SN = average chain length**; **IN = degree of unsaturation**; **AN = free fatty acid content**.
> 3. **Quaternary structure trap:** Not all proteins have quaternary structure. Myoglobin (single chain) has **no quaternary** structure; hemoglobin (4 chains) does.
> 4. **Forgetting stoichiometry in saponification:** One triglyceride requires **3 moles of KOH** to fully saponify. Skipping this in derivations costs full marks.
> 5. **pKa vs pI confusion:** $\text{p}K_a$ is for a **single ionizable group**; $\text{p}I$ is for the **whole molecule** at which the net charge = 0.
> 6. **Drawing arrows or signs inside Mermaid node labels** breaks the diagram renderer. Use clean alphanumeric labels only.

---

## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Proteins and Lipids**

### Proteins
- ☐ **Monomers:** 20 standard α-amino acids; general structure $\text{H}_2\text{N-CH(R)-COOH}$.
- ☐ **Peptide bond:** Covalent amide bond; formed by **condensation** (loss of $\text{H}_2\text{O}$).
- ☐ **Residue formula:** $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n-1}\text{NO}$ for an $n$-carbon amino acid in a chain.
- ☐ **4 structural levels:** 1° (sequence, peptide bonds), 2° (α-helix/β-sheet, H-bonds in backbone), 3° (3D fold, R-group interactions), 4° (multisubunit assembly).
- ☐ **Stabilizing forces:** Covalent (peptide, disulfide) < ionic, H-bonds, hydrophobic, van der Waals.
- ☐ **Henderson–Hasselbalch:** $\text{pH} = \text{p}K_a + \log \frac{[\text{A}^-]}{[\text{HA}]}$; at pH = pI, net charge = 0.
- ☐ **pI rules:** Neutral AA → avg of two pKa; Acidic AA → avg of $\text{p}K_{a1}$ and $\text{p}K_{aR}$; Basic AA → avg of $\text{p}K_{aR}$ and $\text{p}K_{a2}$.
- ☐ **Cancer link:** **p53** (guardian of the genome, mutated in >50% cancers); **RAS, MYC, EGFR, HER2** (oncoproteins); **Rb, BRCA1, PTEN, APC** (tumor suppressors).

### Lipids
- ☐ **Definition:** Hydrophobic/amphipathic biomolecules; insoluble in water; soluble in organic solvents.
- ☐ **NOT polymers** in the strict sense.
- ☐ **Major classes:** Simple (FA, TAG, waxes), Compound (phospholipids, glycolipids, lipoproteins), Derived (steroids, fat-soluble vitamins).
- ☐ **Saturated FA formula:** $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+1}\text{COOH}$; each C=C removes 2H.
- ☐ **Saponification Number (SN):** mg KOH per g fat; **inversely proportional** to average FA molecular weight.
- ☐ **Acid Number (AN):** mg KOH per g fat to neutralize **free FA**; indicator of rancidity.
- ☐ **Iodine Number (IN):** g I₂ per 100 g fat; measures **unsaturation** (C=C double bonds).
- ☐ **Phospholipids:** Amphipathic → form **bilayers** in water (hydrophobic effect).
- ☐ **Cholesterol:** Membrane **fluidity buffer**; component of **lipid rafts**.
- ☐ **Essential FAs:** **ω-3 (α-linolenic acid)** and **ω-6 (linoleic acid)** — must be dietary.
- ☐ **Cancer link:** Reprogrammed lipid metabolism (↑ FASN, ↑ lipogenesis), lipid rafts clustering oncogenic receptors, oxidized lipids = carcinogens.

### Quick-Fire Number Reminders
- α-helix: **3.6 residues/turn, 5.4 Å pitch, H-bond i to i+4**.
- Peptide bonds in a single polypeptide of $N$ residues = $N - 1$.
- 1 triglyceride + **3 KOH** → 3 soap + 1 glycerol.
- **56.1** = equivalent weight of KOH (g/eq).
- **126.9** = atomic weight of iodine.
- **110 Da** = average amino acid residue weight in proteins.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** "**Peptide = covalent; H-bond = secondary; Disulfide = tertiary/quaternary; SN = chain length; IN = unsaturation; pI = zero net charge.**" Master this and you will score full marks in Module 1 of PEBBT521.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module 1

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Cell Membrane – The Dynamic Biological Interface

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> The **plasma membrane** is a selectively permeable, fluid-mosaic bilayer composed primarily of **phospholipids**, **cholesterol**, **integral/transmembrane proteins**, **peripheral proteins**, and **glycoconjugates (glycoproteins & glycolipids)**. In cancer biology, alterations in membrane composition directly drive uncontrolled signaling, trafficking defects, and metastatic behavior.

The cell membrane is not a static wall — it is a **~5–10 nm thick**, dynamic, two-dimensional fluid in which lipids and proteins diffuse laterally. Its integrity governs how a cancer cell communicates, invades, evades immunity, and resists chemotherapy.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🏙️

> [!NOTE]
> Think of the cell membrane as a **bustling city border checkpoint**:
> - **Phospholipids** = the **brick walls & fence** (structural barrier).
> - **Cholesterol** = the **plasticizer in the walls** (keeps them fluid yet firm).
> - **Membrane proteins** = the **security gates, customs officers, and signal towers** (control what enters/exits & send messages).
> - **Glycocalyx (sugars)** = the **ID badges & name plates** (cellular identity).
>
> In **cancer**, the checkpoint becomes corrupted — gates stay open, ID badges are forged, and signals of "stop growing" are ignored. This is exactly what membrane trafficking dysregulation causes.

### 1.2 Major Membrane Components at a Glance

| Component | Chemical Nature | % of Membrane Mass | Key Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Phospholipids** | Amphipathic glycerol/sphingolipids | **~40–50%** | Bilayer scaffold, fluidity |
| **Cholesterol** | Sterol lipid | **~10–30%** | Modulates fluidity, lipid rafts |
| **Membrane Proteins** | Polypeptides (integral/peripheral) | **~25–50%** | Transport, signaling, adhesion |
| **Glycoconjugates** | Sugar–lipid/sugar–protein | **<10%** | Recognition, protection, signaling |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key constant for KTU exams:** Membrane thickness ≈ **7–10 nm** (≈ 70 Å). This is a frequently tested one-mark value.

---

## 1.3 The Fluid Mosaic Model (Singer & Nicolson, 1972)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Fluid Mosaic Model** states that the membrane is a **two-dimensional fluid** of oriented lipids and proteins, where proteins are dispersed and individually mobile in the lipid matrix, much like icebergs floating in a sea of phospholipids.

### Core Principles Embedded in the Model
1. **Lipid bilayer** forms the structural matrix.
2. Membrane is **fluid** — lipids and proteins move laterally (~10⁻⁸ cm²/s diffusion).
3. **Asymmetry** exists between inner and outer leaflets.
4. **Mosaic** — proteins are heterogeneously distributed (often clustered in **lipid rafts**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse "Fluid Mosaic" with "static layers." Always emphasize **LATERAL MOBILITY** in your answer for full marks.

---

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters in Cancer Biology

Membrane biology is the **frontline of cancer** because:
- **Oncogenic receptors** (EGFR, HER2, FGFR) sit ON the membrane.
- **Lipid rafts** cluster growth-promoting receptors.
- **Endocytosis** controls receptor downregulation (lost in cancer).
- **Membrane trafficking** drives secretion of MMPs → metastasis.
- **Glycan alterations** (sialylation, fucosylation) enable immune evasion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 scheme, this topic directly maps to **CO1: Understanding the molecular & cellular basis of cancer.** You can expect 14-mark questions linking membrane biology to oncogenesis.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Phospholipids — The Amphipathic Foundation

A **phospholipid** has:
- **Hydrophilic head** (phosphate + glycerol/sphingosine) — **polar**.
- **Hydrophobic tails** (2 fatty acid chains, typically 14–24 carbons).

The **critical packing parameter** and **shape** dictate membrane curvature:

| Phospholipid | Head Group | Net Charge | Position | Cancer Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Phosphatidylcholine (PC)** | Choline | Neutral | Outer leaflet | Reduced → apoptosis evasion |
| **Phosphatidylethanolamine (PE)** | Ethanolamine | Neutral | Inner leaflet | Promotes membrane curvature/fusion |
| **Phosphatidylserine (PS)** | Serine | **Negative (−)** | Inner leaflet (normal) | Externalized in apoptosis ("eat-me" signal) |
| **Phosphatidylinositol (PI)** | Inositol | **Negative (−)** | Inner leaflet | PI3-K / Akt signaling (cancer axis) |
| **Sphingomyelin (SM)** | Choline (sphingosine backbone) | Neutral | Outer leaflet | Lipid rafts; ceramide → apoptosis |

### 2.1.1 The Phospholipid Asymmetry Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Asymmetry is functional, not accidental.**
> - **Outer leaflet:** PC, SM (neutral → less reactive, protective).
> - **Inner leaflet:** PE, PS, PI (negatively charged → interact with cytosolic proteins).
>
> **Flippases, Floppases, and Scramblases** actively maintain this:
> - **Flippases (ATP11C):** Move PS/PE from outer → inner.
> - **Floppases (ABCA1, ABCG1):** Move lipids inner → outer.
> - **Scramblases (TMEM16F, Xkr8):** Bidirectional, Ca²⁺-dependent (activated in apoptosis).

> **Cancer Connection:** Loss of flippase activity → externalized PS → tumor immune evasion + pro-coagulant (linked to Trousseau's syndrome in pancreatic cancer).

---

## 2.2 Cholesterol — The Fluidity Buffer

> [!NOTE]
> **Cholesterol** inserts its rigid **steroid ring system** between fatty acid tails and its small polar –OH group aligns with phospholipid heads.

### 2.2.1 Dual Effect of Cholesterol on Fluidity

$$ \text{At } T > T_m \ (\text{fluid state}): \ \text{Cholesterol} \downarrow \text{fluidity} \ (\text{orders tails}) $$

$$ \text{At } T < T_m \ (\text{gel state}): \ \text{Cholesterol} \uparrow \text{fluidity} \ (\text{prevents tight packing}) $$

> **Engineering analogy:** Cholesterol is like the **anti-freeze + stiffening agent** in the asphalt of a road — keeps the road workable in both summer and winter.

- **Cholesterol : Phospholipid molar ratio** in mammalian membranes ≈ **0.3 – 0.5** (KTU one-mark favorite).
- **Lipid rafts / Membrane microdomains:** Cholesterol + sphingolipid-rich, **liquid-ordered (Lo)** phase, **20–200 nm** in size, enrich GPI-anchored proteins, Src-family kinases, EGFR.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cancer link:** Lipid rafts cluster **receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs)**. Disrupting rafts pharmacologically (e.g., with **methyl-β-cyclodextrin**) impairs EGFR signaling — a research-grade anti-cancer strategy.

---

## 2.3 Membrane Proteins — The Functional Workforce

### 2.3.1 Classification

| Class | Location | Detergent Soluble? | Example | Cancer Role |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Integral (transmembrane)** | Span bilayer | Yes | EGFR, HER2 | RTK signaling |
| **Peripheral** | Surface-attached | No (need pH/salt) | Src, G-proteins | Downstream signaling |
| **Lipid-anchored** | Covalently linked to lipid | Variable | GPI-anchored receptors | Raft localization |
| **Peripheral (cytoskeletal)** | Spectrin, ankyrin-bound | No | Band 3, ankyrin | Membrane skeleton |

### 2.3.2 Membrane Protein Topology

> [!NOTE]
> The number of **α-helices spanning** the bilayer defines topology. For example:
> - **GPCRs (e.g., β-adrenergic receptor):** 7-TM α-helical bundle.
> - **EGFR:** single-pass Type I receptor (N-terminus extracellular).
> - **GLUT1:** 12-TM transporter — often **overexpressed in cancer** (Warburg effect).

### 2.3.3 Hydropathy & Transmembrane Prediction

A transmembrane α-helix is **~20 residues long** of mostly **hydrophobic** amino acids. The **Kyte-Doolittle hydropathy index** predicts TM segments — a frequently tested KTU fact.

> **Cancer Insight:** Mutations in TM domains (e.g., **EGFR vIII** in glioblastoma) cause **constitutive activation** without ligand — pure membrane biology driving cancer.

---

## 2.4 Glycoconjugates — The Cellular Glycocalyx

- **Glycoproteins** (N-linked to Asn; O-linked to Ser/Thr).
- **Glycolipids** (cerebrosides, gangliosides like **GM1, GD2, GD3**).
- Form the **glycocalyx** (5–200 nm thick sugar coat).

### 2.4.1 Hallmarks of Cancer — Glycan Alterations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Altered glycosylation is now an **emerging hallmark of cancer** (Cancers 2021 review, flagged in KTU 2024 syllabus notes).
> - **Increased sialylation** → immune evasion.
> - **Increased fucosylation** (e.g., **AFP-L3** biomarker in HCC).
> - **Truncated O-glycans** (Tn, sialyl-Tn antigens) → pancreatic, breast cancer.
> - **GD2, GD3 gangliosides** → neuroblastoma, melanoma markers.

---

## 2.5 Membrane Trafficking — The Logistics Network

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Membrane trafficking** = the vesicular transport of proteins & lipids between membrane-bound organelles (ER → Golgi → PM → endosomes → lysosomes) and the extracellular space. In cancer, **trafficking dysregulation** promotes invasion, signaling, and drug resistance.

### 2.5.1 The Two Major Pathways

**A. Exocytosis (Secretory / Biosynthetic Pathway):**

$$ \text{Nucleus} \rightarrow \text{ER} \rightarrow \text{Golgi} \rightarrow \text{Secretory vesicles} \rightarrow \text{Plasma Membrane} \rightarrow \text{Extracellular space} $$

**B. Endocytosis (Uptake / Degradative / Recycling):**

$$ \text{Extracellular space} \rightarrow \text{Plasma Membrane} \rightarrow \text{Early Endosome} \rightarrow \text{Late Endosome} \rightarrow \text{Lysosome} $$

### 2.5.2 Types of Endocytosis (KTU-High-Yield)

| Type | Mechanism | Cargo | Cancer Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Clathrin-mediated (CME)** | Adaptor AP2, dynamin scission | RTKs (EGFR), transferrin, LDL | EGFR downregulation — defective in cancer |
| **Caveolae-mediated** | Caveolin-1 (Cav-1), flask-shaped invaginations | Simian virus 40, cholera toxin | Caveolin-1 acts as tumor suppressor in many cancers |
| **Macropinocytosis** | Actin-driven, non-selective | Extracellular fluid, albumin | **Fuel for Ras-driven cancers** (pancreatic, lung) |
| **Phagocytosis** | Engulfment of large particles (>0.5 µm) | Apoptotic bodies, bacteria | Tumor-associated macrophages |
| **CLIC/GEEC** | Tubular endosomes, GPI-anchored proteins | GPI-APs, fluid-phase | Nutrient acquisition |

### 2.5.3 The Exocyst Complex & Polarized Secretion

The **exocyst (Sec3, Sec5, Sec6, Sec8, Sec10, Sec15, Exo70, Exo84)** tethers vesicles to the plasma membrane at sites of polarized secretion.
- **Cancer link:** **Exo70** overexpression → mislocalized MMP secretion → **metastasis**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet & Quick-Reference Table

> [!NOTE]
> **No algebraic derivations are involved in membrane biology**, but the following **key quantitative facts** are tested repeatedly:

| # | Fact / Constant | Value | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Plasma membrane thickness | **7–10 nm** (~70 Å) | Frequently asked one-mark value |
| 2 | Lipid bilayer hydrophobic core | **~3 nm** | Determines solubility of gases |
| 3 | Phospholipid surface area (head group) | **~0.5–0.7 nm²** | Determines lipid packing |
| 4 | Cholesterol : Phospholipid molar ratio | **0.3–0.5** | Defines membrane order |
| 5 | Lipid raft diameter | **20–200 nm** | Microdomain biology |
| 6 | Lateral diffusion coefficient (lipid) | **~10⁻⁸ cm²/s** | FRAP measurements |
| 7 | Membrane electrical resistance | **10⁴ Ω·cm²** | Biophysics |
| 8 | Membrane capacitance | **~1 µF/cm²** | Patch-clamp & Nernst eqn |
| 9 | Tetrameric/hexameric ATPases | Rotary motors | Vacuolar H⁺-ATPase acidifies endosomes |
| 10 | Endosomal pH gradient | **Early EE: 6.0–6.5; Late LE: 5.0–5.5; Lysosome: 4.5** | Receptor–ligand dissociation |

### 2.6.1 Nernst Equation (Membrane Potential) — Often Tested

$$ E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}} $$

Where:
- $E_{\text{ion}}$ = equilibrium potential (V)
- $R$ = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** (gas constant)
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)
- $z$ = ion valence
- $F$ = **96,485 C·mol⁻¹** (Faraday's constant)
- $[\text{ion}]_{\text{out/in}}$ = external/internal concentration

At **physiological T = 310 K (37 °C)**, this simplifies to:

$$ E_{\text{ion}} \approx \frac{61.5}{z} \log_{10} \frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}} \quad (\text{mV}) $$

> **Real-world utility:** Cancer cells often have **depolarized resting membrane potentials** (e.g., −10 to −30 mV in glioma vs. −70 mV normal) — exploited by **oncolytic virus therapies**.

---

## 2.7 Real-World / Engineering Utility of This Topic

1. **Drug Delivery:** Liposomes (Doxil®), lipid nanoparticles (mRNA COVID vaccines), exosome mimetics — all exploit membrane biophysics.
2. **Diagnostic Imaging:** Gadolinium-loaded lipid vesicles target **PS externalization** in cancer.
3. **Biosensors:** Membrane protein–based FET sensors for circulating tumor cells.
4. **Biotechnology:** CHO cells engineered for high-density lipid rafts improve monoclonal antibody production.
5. **Cryo-EM & AI:** AlphaFold predictions of membrane proteins drive oncology target discovery (KRAS-G12D, p53 mutants).
