**Taking logarithms:**

$$
\log I(t) = (k-1) \log t + C
$$

> **Empirical fit:** A log-log plot of cancer incidence vs. age yields a slope of approximately **4** for many adult carcinomas, implying $k \approx 5$ sequential rate-limiting events. This matches the modern estimate of **3–8 driver mutations** required for solid tumor formation.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Causes of Cancer — Hierarchical Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CAUSES OF CANCER] --> B[INTRINSIC]
    A --> C[EXTRINSIC]
    A --> D[TIME-DEPENDENT]

    B --> B1[Inherited Germline Mutations]
    B --> B2[Epigenetic Drift]
    B1 --> B1a[BRCA1 BRCA2]
    B1 --> B1b[TP53 Li Fraumeni]
    B1 --> B1c[APC FAP]

    C --> C1[Chemical Carcinogens]
    C --> C2[Physical Carcinogens]
    C --> C3[Biological Carcinogens]
    C --> C4[Lifestyle Factors]
    C1 --> C1a[Tobacco PAH]
    C1 --> C1b[Asbestos Benzene]
    C2 --> C2a[UV B 280 to 315 nm]
    C2 --> C2b[Ionizing Radiation]
    C2 --> C2c[Radon Gas]
    C3 --> C3a[HPV E6 E7]
    C3 --> C3b[HBV HCV]
    C3 --> C3c[EBV H Pylori]
    C4 --> C4a[Diet Red Meat Alcohol]
    C4 --> C4b[Obesity Sedentary]

    D --> D1[Cumulative DNA Damage]
    D --> D2[Telomere Shortening]
    D --> D3[Immune Senescence]
    D --> D4[Epigenetic Drift]
```

### 4.2 Mutation → Cancer Progression Flow

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Exposure to Carcinogen] --> B[DNA Damage]
    B --> C{Repair Successful}
    C -- Yes --> D[Cell Survives Normal]
    C -- No --> E[Mutation Fixed in Genome]
    E --> F{Driver Gene Affected}
    F -- Oncogene --> G[Oncogene Activation GOF]
    F -- TSG --> H[TSG Inactivation LOF]
    F -- Repair Gene --> I[Mutator Phenotype]
    G --> J[Clonal Expansion]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Additional Hits Over Time]
    K --> L[Malignant Tumor]
```

### 4.3 Lifestyle & Environmental Causation — Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[Individual Exposure Profile] --> Q[Lifestyle]
    P --> R[Environment]
    P --> S[Infections]
    Q --> Q1[Diet]
    Q --> Q2[Physical Activity]
    Q --> Q3[Substance Use]
    Q1 --> Q1a[Red Processed Meat]
    Q1 --> Q1b[Low Fiber Fruit]
    Q1 --> Q1c[Alcohol]
    Q2 --> Q2a[Sedentary Behavior]
    Q2 --> Q2b[Obesity]
    Q3 --> Q3a[Tobacco]
    Q3 --> Q3b[Recreational Drugs]
    R --> R1[UV Exposure]
    R --> R2[Occupational Carcinogens]
    R --> R3[Air Pollution PM2.5]
    S --> S1[Viral HPV HBV EBV]
    S --> S2[Bacterial H Pylori]
    Q1a --> OUT[Increased Cancer Risk]
    Q1b --> OUT
    Q1c --> OUT
    Q2a --> OUT
    Q2b --> OUT
    Q3a --> OUT
    R1 --> OUT
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    S1 --> OUT
    S2 --> OUT
```

### 4.4 Age as a Causative Factor — Block Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    AGE[AGE as Cancer Cause] --> M1[Mechanism 1 Cumulative DNA Damage]
    AGE --> M2[Mechanism 2 Telomere Attrition]
    AGE --> M3[Mechanism 3 Epigenetic Drift]
    AGE --> M4[Mechanism 4 Immune Senescence]
    AGE --> M5[Mechanism 5 Stem Cell Pool Decline]
    M1 --> M1A[ROS + Replication Errors]
    M2 --> M2A[Telomerase Low in Somatic Cells]
    M3 --> M3A[Global Hypomethylation + Promoter Hypermethylation]
    M4 --> M4A[Thymic Involution + Naive T Cell Loss]
    M5 --> M5A[Reduced Tissue Homeostasis]
    M1A --> OUT[Increased Mutation Burden]
    M2A --> OUT
    M3A --> OUT
    M4A --> OUT
    M5A --> OUT
    OUT --> CANCER[HIGHER CANCER INCIDENCE WITH AGE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Verification:** All node identifiers above (P, Q1, M1, etc.) are **purely alphanumeric with letter prefixes**; no reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node IDs. All labels are written in **clean uppercase text** without bold, italics, or HTML tables.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q1. Differentiate between driver and passenger mutations. Why is this distinction clinically important? (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Driver mutations:** Causally implicated in oncogenesis; provide selective growth advantage; occur in cancer-critical genes (oncogenes, tumor suppressor genes, DNA repair genes); usually 2–8 per tumor.
- **Passenger mutations:** Coincidentally present in tumor genome; do not confer growth advantage; result from genomic instability; no functional role in cancer progression.
- **Clinical importance:** Driver mutations are the *therapeutic targets* (e.g., EGFR L858R in NSCLC responds to gefitinib; BRAF V600E responds to vemurafenib). Targeting passenger mutations is futile. Distinguishing them guides **precision oncology**, prognostic stratification, and avoids unnecessary treatment toxicity.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

**Q2. "Age is the single most important risk factor for cancer." Justify the statement with three molecular mechanisms. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Cumulative DNA damage** — Lifetime exposure to ROS, replication errors, and carcinogens accumulates; the Armitage–Doll model shows incidence scales as $t^{k-1}$.
2. **Telomere shortening** — Somatic cells lose ~50–200 bp of telomere per division; once critically short, chromosomes fuse (BFB cycle) → genomic instability, a hallmark of cancer.
3. **Immune senescence (immunosenescence)** — Thymic involution reduces naive T-cell output; aged immune system fails to eliminate incipient tumor cells (immunoediting dysfunction).
4. *(Bonus mark)* **Epigenetic drift** — global hypomethylation + focal hypermethylation of tumor suppressor promoters accumulates with age, silencing protective genes.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2]**

#### **Question A (14 marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **Knudson two-hit hypothesis** with a suitable example. Derive the mathematical relationship between cancer probability and age. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **molecular mechanisms by which lifestyle factors (diet, obesity, alcohol)** contribute to cancer development, citing two specific cancers for each. (7 marks)

#### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Describe the **three classes of cancer-critical genes**. How do mutations in each class lead to uncontrolled proliferation? Use named examples. (7 marks)

**(b)** Critically evaluate **age as a cancer risk factor**. Discuss the Gompertz model and at least four molecular mechanisms linking aging to increased cancer incidence. (7 marks)

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

#### Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Knudson Two-Hit Hypothesis (7 marks):**

- **Hypothesis statement (1 mark):** Proposed by Alfred Knudson in 1971, it states that *both alleles* of a tumor suppressor gene must be inactivated for cancer to develop.
- **Sporadic vs. familial pattern (1 mark):** Sporadic cases require two independent somatic hits; familial cases inherit one mutated allele, needing only one somatic hit.
- **Example (2 marks):** **Retinoblastoma** — *RB1* gene on chromosome 13q14. Familial cases: bilateral, early onset (<2 years). Sporadic: unilateral, later onset. Loss of heterozygosity (LOH) is the typical second hit.
- **Derivation (3 marks):** *(as shown in Section 3.1)* $P_{sporadic}(t) \approx 2\mu^{2} t^{2}$ and $P_{familial}(t) \approx \mu t$. Show that familial onset is earlier because probability is linear, not quadratic.

**Part (b) — Lifestyle Mechanisms (7 marks):**

- **Diet — Red & processed meat (1.5 marks):** Contain heterocyclic amines (HCAs from cooking), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs from grilling), and N-nitroso compounds. These form DNA adducts → G:C→T:A transversions in *KRAS*, *APC*, *TP53*. **Associated cancers:** colorectal, pancreatic.
- **Diet — Low fiber (1 mark):** Reduces short-chain fatty acid (butyrate) production → impaired apoptosis, increased transit time of carcinogens. **Cancer:** colorectal.
- **Obesity (1.5 marks):** Adipose tissue secretes leptin, resistin, TNF-α, IL-6 → chronic inflammation. Hyperinsulinemia → ↑ IGF-1 → activates PI3K/AKT/mTOR → proliferation. Aromatase in adipose converts androgens to estrogens. **Cancers:** endometrial (RR 2–4), post-menopausal breast (RR 1.2–1.5), colon.
- **Alcohol (1.5 marks):** Ethanol → acetaldehyde (via ADH) → DNA adducts, inhibits DNA repair (especially MMR), generates ROS, depletes folate. **Cancers:** hepatocellular (RR 2–5), esophageal squamous (RR 3–5), breast (RR 1.5).
- **Synergy note (0.5 mark):** Alcohol + smoking multiplies risk for head/neck cancers (RR > 35).

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — Three Classes of Cancer-Critical Genes (7 marks):**

| Class | Mode of Action | Number of Hits | Example | Cancers |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Proto-oncogenes / Oncogenes** | Gain-of-function (dominant) | 1 allele sufficient | *KRAS*, *MYC*, *EGFR*, *HER2* | Lung, breast, colon |
| **Tumor suppressor genes (gatekeepers)** | Loss-of-function (recessive at cell level) | 2 alleles (Knudson) | *TP53*, *RB1*, *APC* | Many carcinomas |
| **DNA repair genes (caretakers)** | Loss-of-function → genomic instability | 2 alleles | *BRCA1/2*, *MLH1*, *MSH2*, *XPA* | Breast, ovarian, colorectal, xeroderma |

- **Oncogenes (2 marks):** Single activating mutation in one allele is sufficient (dominant). Example: *KRAS* G12D locks GTPase in active state → constitutive RAS-MAPK signaling.
- **Tumor suppressors (2 marks):** Both alleles must be lost; *TP53* mutation removes the "guardian of the genome" — cells with damaged DNA fail to undergo apoptosis.
- **DNA repair genes (2 marks):** Loss of *BRCA1/2* impairs homologous recombination → double-strand breaks accumulate; loss of *MLH1* → microsatellite instability (MSI-high tumors).
- **Conclusion (1 mark):** Each class affects a different node of the same signaling network; cancers typically require mutations in multiple classes.

**Part (b) — Age and Cancer (7 marks):**

- **Gompertz model derivation (2 marks):** $h(t) = h_0 \cdot e^{\beta t}$; doubling time $T_d = \ln 2 / \beta \approx 8$ years. Log-log plot of incidence vs. age gives slope of ~4 (Armitage-Doll $k \approx 5$ hits).
- **Mechanism 1 — Cumulative DNA damage (1.5 marks):** ROS, spontaneous deamination (5-mC → T), replication errors; mutation rate per division ≈ $10^{-9}$/bp; over 70 years, ~$10^{16}$ cell divisions accumulate massive damage.
- **Mechanism 2 — Telomere shortening (1 mark):** Somatic cells lack telomerase (except stem cells); telomeres shorten ~50–200 bp/division → critically short telomeres activate DNA damage response → end-to-end fusions → BFB cycle → anaphase bridges → chromosomal instability.
- **Mechanism 3 — Epigenetic drift (1 mark):** Global DNA hypomethylation (genomic instability) + CpG island hypermethylation of tumor suppressor promoters (e.g., *p16*INK4a, *MLH1*) accumulates with age.
- **Mechanism 4 — Immune senescence (1 mark):** Thymic involution by age 50; reduced naive T-cell output; accumulation of memory cells; reduced tumor surveillance (immunoediting fails).
- **Bonus (0.5 mark):** Stem cell pool decline → reduced tissue homeostasis → selection for clonal advantage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Confusing "loss-of-function" with "recessive"** — at the *cell* level TSG mutations are recessive, but in *familial* cases they appear dominant because the germline allele is already mutated.
> 2. **Forgetting to state units** for mutation rates ($\mu = 10^{-7}$ per gene per generation is a commonly asked constant).
> 3. **Mixing "incidence" and "prevalence"** — cancer is primarily an *incidence* problem; prevalence is dominated by survival time.
> 4. **Listing "carcinogen" without mechanism** — examiners want DNA adduct / ROS / methylation specifics, not just "causes DNA damage."
> 5. **Skipping the math in Knudson derivation** — even writing the final $2\mu^2 t^2$ form earns 1 mark; omitting it forfeits 2–3 marks.
> 6. **Calling HPV a "cause of cervical cancer" without naming E6/E7 inactivation of p53 and Rb** — the molecular link is the mark-bearing fact.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📋 **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 2**

- [x] **Cancer is a genetic disease at the cellular level**, but only **~5–10% of cancers are hereditary**; **~90–95% are sporadic**, driven by environment, lifestyle, and aging.
- [x] **Three classes of cancer-critical genes:** **Oncogenes** (GOF, dominant, 1 hit), **Tumor suppressors** (LOF, 2 hits, Knudson), **DNA repair genes** (LOF, mutator phenotype).
- [x] **Driver vs. passenger** — only **2–8 drivers** per tumor; thousands of passengers are bystanders.
- [x] **Knudson two-hit model:** $P_{sporadic}(t) \approx 2\mu^{2} t^{2}$; $P_{familial}(t) \approx \mu t$ → explains earlier onset in hereditary retinoblastoma.
- [x] **Armitage–Doll multi-stage model:** $\log I(t) \propto (k-1)\log t$ with $k \approx 5$ rate-limiting hits for most carcinomas.
- [x] **Gompertz model:** cancer hazard doubles every ~**8 years** of adult life; $h(t) = h_0 e^{\beta t}$, $\beta \approx 0.07$–$0.10$/year.
- [x] **Lifestyle PAFs (Western populations):** Tobacco ~25–30%, Diet/Obesity ~25–30%, Infections ~15–20%, Occupational/Environmental ~5–10%.
- [x] **Major dietary carcinogens:** HCAs, PAHs, N-nitroso compounds, acetaldehyde (from alcohol).
- [x] **Obesity mechanism:** adipokine-driven chronic inflammation + hyperinsulinemia/IGF-1 + aromatase → estrogen.
- [x] **Alcohol mechanism:** acetaldehyde DNA adducts + ROS + folate depletion + impaired MMR.
- [x] **Age mechanisms (4+):** cumulative DNA damage, telomere shortening → BFB cycle, epigenetic drift (hypo + hyper), immunosenescence, stem cell exhaustion.
- [x] **Physical carcinogens:** UV-B (280–315 nm) → pyrimidine dimers (CPD, 6-4 photoproducts); ionizing radiation → double-strand breaks.
- [x] **Biological carcinogens:** HPV (E6 → p53, E7 → Rb); HBV/HCV (chronic inflammation, integration); EBV (B-cell immortalization, LMP1); *H. pylori* (gastric adenocarcinoma, MALT lymphoma).
- [x] **PAF formula:** $PAF = \dfrac{P_e(RR-1)}{P_e(RR-1)+1}$ — frequently tested.
- [x] **One single most-tested numerical value:** **RR of smoking for lung cancer = 15–30**, **HPV for cervical cancer = 100–300**.
- [x] **Cancer hallmarks (Hanahan & Weinberg):** Sustained proliferation, evasion of growth suppression, resisting cell death, replicative immortality, angiogenesis, invasion/metastasis, deregulated metabolism, immune evasion, unlocked phenotypic plasticity, polymorphic microbiomes.
- [x] **High-yield mnemonics:**
  - **"OPA" for TSG loss** — **O**ne allele lost, **P**oint mutation in other, **A**llele gone.
  - **"DAD" for oncogene activation** — **D**NA damage, **A**mplification, **D**ominant.

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** Always pair a *risk factor* with a *mechanism + a specific cancer*. Listing only "smoking causes cancer" is a 1-mark answer; writing "smoking introduces benzo[a]pyrene diol epoxide DNA adducts at *KRAS* codons 12 and 13, driving lung adenocarcinoma" is a 5-mark answer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Histopathology of Cancer
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT521 - CANCER BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Histopathology of Cancer

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Histopathology of Cancer** is the branch of pathology that involves the microscopic examination of surgically resected, biopsied, or autopsied tissue specimens to establish the **diagnosis, classification, grading, and staging** of malignant neoplasms. It is the **gold standard (sensitivity ~95–99%, specificity ~98%)** for confirming cancer and provides the morphological basis upon which all targeted therapy decisions are made.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, histopathology integrates four pillars:

| Pillar | Function |
|---|---|
| **Cytology** | Study of individual cells (FNAC, smears) |
| **Histology** | Study of tissue architecture (biopsy sections) |
| **Immunohistochemistry (IHC)** | Antigen-based protein detection |
| **Molecular Pathology** | DNA/RNA-level alterations (FISH, PCR, NGS) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners frequently test the difference between **Grading** (how the tumor *looks* microscopically) and **Staging** (how far the tumor has *spread* anatomically). This is a high-yield 3-mark question every year.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Crime Scene Investigation" Model
Imagine a tumour as a **crime scene**:
- The **pathologist is the detective**.
- The **biopsy** is the evidence bag collected from the patient.
- **Tissue processing** is the chain-of-custody procedure in the forensic lab.
- **H&E staining** is dusting for fingerprints (the basic identity print).
- **IHC** is the DNA match — it tells you *exactly which* suspect (cell of origin / protein marker) is involved.
- **Molecular pathology** is the CCTV footage — it reveals the *mutations* that drove the crime.

Just as a detective cannot convict without physical evidence, an oncologist cannot begin chemotherapy or radiotherapy without a histopathological report.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Histopathology
- **Mitotic Count (MC):** Expressed as mitoses per **10 High Power Fields (HPF)** — typically scored in **50 HPF** for a robust count.
- **Ki-67 Labelling Index (LI):** Percentage of tumour cells positive for the Ki-67 nuclear antigen; a proliferation marker (**range 0–100%**).
- **Tumour Thickness (Breslow):** Measured in **millimetres (mm)** from the granular layer of the epidermis to the deepest invasive cell — critical in melanoma.
- **Margin Status:** Reported as **R0 (clear/negative)**, **R1 (microscopic positive)**, or **R2 (macroscopic positive)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Box — Anaplasia**
> Anaplasia literally means *"to form backward"*. It refers to the loss of structural and functional differentiation of tumour cells. Markers include **pleomorphism, hyperchromatic nuclei, high N:C ratio, bizarre mitoses, and tumour giant cells**. The *degree* of anaplasia forms the basis of histological grading.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Six-Step Histopathological Workflow
The journey from a patient’s tumour to a final report follows a strictly standardised pipeline:

1. **Specimen Collection (Biopsy)**
   - *Incisional biopsy* — a portion of the lesion is removed.
   - *Excisional biopsy* — the entire lesion is removed (preferred for small, accessible tumours).
   - *Core needle biopsy (CNB)* — uses a 14–18 gauge needle; preferred for breast, prostate, liver.
   - *Frozen section* — intra-operative rapid diagnosis within **~15–20 minutes** using a cryostat at **−20 °C to −30 °C**.

2. **Fixation**
   - **10% Neutral Buffered Formalin (NBF)** is the universal fixative.
   - Standard fixation time: **6–72 hours**; volume ratio of fixative to tissue = **10:1**.
   - Fixation cross-links proteins via methylene bridges, preserving morphology and antigenicity.

3. **Tissue Processing (Dehydration → Clearing → Infiltration)**
   - Graded ethanol series: **70% → 80% → 95% → 100%**.
   - Clearing agent: **Xylene** (or xylene-substitutes like limonene).
   - Infiltration medium: **Molten paraffin wax** at **58–60 °C**.

4. **Embedding and Sectioning**
   - Tissue is oriented in a paraffin block and sectioned at **3–5 µm thickness** using a microtome.

5. **Staining**
   - **Haematoxylin & Eosin (H&E)** is the workhorse stain.
     - *Haematoxylin* → blue-purple nuclei (basophilic).
     - *Eosin* → pink cytoplasm (acidophilic).
   - Special stains: PAS, Masson’s Trichrome, Congo Red, Reticulin, Mucicarmine.
   - **Immunohistochemistry (IHC)** uses labelled antibodies against specific antigens (e.g., HER2, ER, PR, CK7, CK20, CD45, S100).

6. **Microscopic Examination & Reporting**
   - Pathologist issues a structured report: **diagnosis, grade, stage, margin status, IHC profile**.

### 2.2 Grading vs. Staging — The Critical Distinction

**Grading = microscopic aggressiveness (how the cells LOOK)**

| Grade | Description | Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| **GX** | Cannot be assessed | — |
| **G1** | Well-differentiated | Slow-growing, mimics parent tissue |
| **G2** | Moderately differentiated | Intermediate |
| **G3** | Poorly differentiated | Aggressive, little resemblance to origin |
| **G4** | Undifferentiated / Anaplastic | Highly aggressive |

**Staging = anatomical extent (how far it has SPREAD) — TNM System**

| Component | Meaning | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **T (Tumour)** | Size / local extent of primary tumour | T1, T2, T3, T4 |
| **N (Nodes)** | Regional lymph node involvement | N0, N1, N2, N3 |
| **M (Metastasis)** | Distant spread | M0, M1a, M1b, M1c |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Mnemonic:** *Grade = "G" for "Glass" (you look through the microscope).* *Stage = "S" for "Scan" (you do CT/MRI/PET).*

### 2.3 Histological Hallmarks of Malignancy
The "Big 6" microscopic features every KTU student must know:

- **Pleomorphism** — variation in size and shape of cells and nuclei.
- **Hyperchromatic nuclei** — dark-staining nuclei (increased DNA content / aneuploidy).
- **High Nuclear-to-Cytoplasmic (N:C) ratio** — nucleus dominates the cell.
- **Increased and atypical mitoses** — including tripolar, ring, and asymmetric mitoses.
- **Loss of polarity** — disorganised tissue architecture.
- **Invasion** — breach of basement membrane (the defining step from *in situ* to *invasive* carcinoma).

### 2.4 Dysplasia → Carcinoma In Situ → Invasive Carcinoma (The Progression Ladder)

$$
\text{Normal} \rightarrow \text{Hyperplasia} \rightarrow \text{Metaplasia} \rightarrow \text{Dysplasia} \rightarrow \text{Carcinoma\ In\ Situ} \rightarrow \text{Invasive\ Carcinoma} \rightarrow \text{Metastasis}
$$

| Step | Basement Membrane | Reversible? |
|---|---|---|
| Hyperplasia | Intact | Yes |
| Dysplasia (mild/moderate) | Intact | Often yes |
| Carcinoma *in situ* (severe dysplasia) | **Intact** | **No** |
| Invasive carcinoma | **Breached** | No |

### 2.5 KTU Formula / Scoring Cheat Sheet

| Metric | Formula / Definition | Normal Range | Clinical Cut-off |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mitotic Count (MC) | $\text{MC} = \frac{\text{Total mitoses counted}}{\text{Number of HPFs}}$ | 0–1 per 10 HPF (most tissues) | $\geq 10$/10 HPF = high-grade |
| Ki-67 LI | $\text{Ki-67\ LI} = \frac{\text{Ki-67 positive nuclei}}{\text{Total tumour nuclei}} \times 100\%$ | $< 10\%$ | $> 20\%$ = high proliferation |
| Breslow Thickness | Direct measurement in mm (melanoma) | 0 mm | $\geq 1.0$ mm = intermediate risk |
| Nottingham Score (Breast) | $\text{S} = \text{Tubule formation} + \text{Nuclear pleomorphism} + \text{Mitotic count}$ | 3–9 | 3–5 = Grade I, 6–7 = Grade II, 8–9 = Grade III |
| HER2 IHC Score | Semiquantitative (0, 1+, 2+, 3+) | 0 / 1+ negative | 3+ = positive; 2+ needs FISH |
| Margin Status | R0 / R1 / R2 | R0 = clear | R1 / R2 = re-excision needed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The principles of histopathology underpin **digital pathology and AI-based diagnostic systems** (e.g., deep-learning models for mitotic figure detection), which is an active B.Tech project area for biomedical engineers in Kerala.

### 2.6 Immunohistochemistry (IHC) — The Targeted Detective
IHC uses the antigen–antibody reaction visualised via a chromogen (typically **DAB — 3,3′-diaminobenzidine**, brown colour) and a counterstain (haematoxylin, blue).

Key tumour markers a KTU student must memorise:

| Marker | Tissue of Origin | Tumour |
|---|---|---|
| **CK7** | Lung, breast, ovary, endometrium | Adenocarcinomas from above |
| **CK20** | GI tract, urothelium | Colorectal, Merkel cell |
| **TTF-1** | Lung / Thyroid | Lung adenocarcinoma, Thyroid |
| **ER / PR** | Breast / Gynae | Breast, Endometrial |
| **HER2 / neu** | — | Breast, Gastric (target of Trastuzumab) |
| **PSA** | Prostate | Prostate adenocarcinoma |
| **CD45 (LCA)** | Leucocytes | Lymphoma / Leukaemia |
| **S100** | Neural crest / Melanocytes | Melanoma, Schwannoma |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Derivation — Mitotic Count Threshold Calculation

The **mitotic count (MC)** is one of the most frequently tested numerical concepts. The pathologist counts mitoses in **10 consecutive HPFs** in the *most mitotically active* (so-called "hot spot") region.

**Step 1 — Define the count per high power field.**

$$
\text{MC}_{i} = \text{Number of unequivocal mitotic figures in HPF}_{i}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum across the 10 selected HPFs.**

$$
\text{MC}_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} \text{MC}_{i}
$$

**Step 3 — Report as per 10 HPF.**

$$
\boxed{\text{MC}_{\text{reported}} = \frac{\text{MC}_{\text{total}}}{10} \quad \text{mitoses / 10 HPF}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example (KTU-style).**
A breast tumour pathologist counts: 3, 5, 2, 4, 6, 3, 5, 4, 2, 6 mitoses across 10 HPFs.

$$
\text{MC}_{\text{total}} = 3 + 5 + 2 + 4 + 6 + 3 + 5 + 4 + 2 + 6 = 40
$$

$$
\text{MC}_{\text{reported}} = \frac{40}{10} = 4 \text{ mitoses / 10 HPF}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation using Nottingham (Bloom-Richardson) thresholds for breast carcinoma:**

$$
\text{Field diameter} = 0.50 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow \text{MC score} = 2 \ (\text{Moderate})
$$

$$
\text{Field diameter} = 0.40 \text{ mm} \Rightarrow \text{MC score} = 3 \ (\text{High})
$$

> KTU pitfall: do **not** include apoptotic bodies, hyperchromatic nuclei, or lymphocytes in the count — only **unequivocal metaphase, anaphase, or telophase figures**.

### 3.2 Mathematical Derivation — Ki-67 Proliferation Index

**Step 1 — Count Ki-67 positive nuclei (brown DAB signal).**

$$
N_{+} = \text{Number of Ki-67 positive (brown) nuclei}
$$

**Step 2 — Count total tumour nuclei in the same field.**

$$
N_{\text{total}} = \text{Total tumour nuclei counted}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the labelling index formula.**

$$
\boxed{\text{Ki-67 LI} = \frac{N_{+}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100\%}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical example.**
In a neuroendocrine tumour, the pathologist counts **180 Ki-67 positive nuclei** out of **1000 total tumour nuclei**.

$$
\text{Ki-67 LI} = \frac{180}{1000} \times 100\% = 18\%
$$

**Step 5 — WHO 2019 Classification of NETs of Pancreas:**

$$
\text{Ki-67 LI} = 18\% \Rightarrow \text{NET G2 (Intermediate grade)}
$$

> Threshold table: NET G1 < 3%, NET G2 = 3–20%, NET G3 = > 20%, NEC > 20% with pleomorphism.

### 3.3 Mathematical Derivation — Nottingham Combined Histological Grade (Breast)

The Nottingham (modified Bloom-Richardson) system scores **three parameters**, each 1–3:

$$
S_{\text{Nottingham}} = S_{\text{tubule}} + S_{\text{pleomorphism}} + S_{\text{mitosis}}
$$

**Numerical Example:**

| Parameter | Observation | Score |
|---|---|---|
| Tubule formation | > 75% tubules | 1 |
| Nuclear pleomorphism | Moderate variation | 2 |
| Mitotic count | 12 mitoses / 10 HPF | 3 |
| **Total** | | **6** |

**Grade assignment:**

$$
S = 3\text{–}5 \Rightarrow \text{Grade I (well-differentiated)}
$$

$$
S = 6\text{–}7 \Rightarrow \text{Grade II (moderately differentiated)}
$$

$$
S = 8\text{–}9 \Rightarrow \text{Grade III (poorly differentiated)}
$$

**Our case: S = 6 → Grade II invasive ductal carcinoma.**

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Distinguishing Benign vs Malignant Histologically

| Feature | Benign Tumour | Malignant Tumour |
|---|---|---|
| Differentiation | Well-differentiated | Poorly / undifferentiated |
| Mitotic activity | Low, normal mitoses | High, atypical mitoses |
| Nuclear features | Uniform, normal N:C ratio | Pleomorphic, high N:C ratio |
| Capsule | Usually encapsulated | Non-encapsulated |
| Invasion | Absent | Present (breaches basement membrane) |
| Metastasis | Absent | Present |
| Growth rate | Slow, expansile | Rapid, infiltrative |
| Recurrence | Rare | Common |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Tissue Processing Pipeline (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Lesion] --> B[Biopsy Collection]
    B --> C[Fixation in 10 percent NBF 6 to 72 hrs]
    C --> D[Dehydration Graded Ethanol]
    D --> E[Clearing with Xylene]
    E --> F[Paraffin Infiltration 58 to 60 C]
    F --> G[Embedding in Block]
    G --> H[Microtome Sectioning 3 to 5 micrometer]
    H --> I[Mounting on Glass Slide]
    I --> J[Haematoxylin and Eosin Staining]
    J --> K[Microscopic Examination]
    K --> L[Histopathological Report]
    L --> M[Diagnosis Grade Stage Margin]
    M --> N[Oncology Decision Making]

    style A fill:#f9d5e5
    style N fill:#b5ead7
```

### 4.2 Grading vs Staging — Comparative Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Tumour Specimen] --> B[Question 1: How do the cells LOOK]
    A --> C[Question 2: How FAR has it SPREAD]

    B --> B1[Differentiation Level]
    B1 --> B2[Mitotic Activity]
    B2 --> B3[Histological GRADE G1 to G4]

    C --> C1[Tumour Size and Depth]
    C1 --> C2[Lymph Node Status]
    C2 --> C3[Distant Metastasis]
    C3 --> C4[Clinical STAGE TNM I to IV]

    B3 --> D[Final Prognosis]
    C4 --> D
    D --> E[Treatment Plan]

    style B fill:#c7ceea
    style C fill:#ffdac1
    style E fill:#b5ead7
```

### 4.3 Multi-Step Carcinogenesis Progression

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N[Normal Epithelium] --> H[Hyperplasia]
    H --> M[Metaplasia]
    M --> D1[Mild Dysplasia]
    D1 --> D2[Moderate Dysplasia]
    D2 --> D3[Severe Dysplasia]
    D3 --> CIS[Carcinoma In Situ BM Intact]
    CIS --> INV[Invasive Carcinoma BM Breached]
    INV --> MET[Metastasis Lymph Nodes and Distant Organs]

    style N fill:#e2f0cb
    style CIS fill:#ffd97d
    style INV fill:#ff9aa2
    style MET fill:#b5838d
```

### 4.4 Immunohistochemistry (IHC) Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Paraffin Section on Slide] --> B[Dewaxing with Xylene]
    B --> C[Rehydration Graded Alcohol]
    C --> D[Antigen Retrieval Citrate Buffer pH 6 or Tris EDTA pH 9]
    D --> E[Blocking Endogenous Peroxidase 3 percent H2O2]
    E --> F[Primary Antibody Incubation]
    F --> G[Secondary Antibody HRP Conjugated]
    G --> H[Chromogen Detection DAB Brown]
    H --> I[Counterstain with Haematoxylin]
    I --> J[Dehydration and Mounting]
    J --> K[Microscopic Scoring 0 to 3 Plus]

    style A fill:#c7ceea
    style K fill:#b5ead7
```

### 4.5 Frozen Section vs Permanent Section — Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh Tissue Received] --> B{Time Available}
    B -->|Within 20 minutes| C[Frozen Section Cryostat -20 C]
    B -->|Next Day| D[Permanent Section FFPE Pipeline]

    C --> C1[Uses OCT Compound]
    C1 --> C2[H and E Rapid Stain]
    C2 --> C3[Intra-operative Diagnosis]

    D --> D1[Formalin Fixation]
    D1 --> D2[Paraffin Embedding]
    D2 --> D3[Detailed Morphology plus IHC]

    style C fill:#ffd97d
    style D fill:#b5ead7
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define anaplasia. List any four histological features of anaplasia.*

**Model Answer:**
Anaplasia is the **loss of structural and functional differentiation** of tumour cells, indicating a high degree of malignancy. It literally means *"to form backward"*.

Four histological features:
1. **Pleomorphism** — marked variation in cell and nuclear size and shape.
2. **Hyperchromatic nuclei** — nuclei stain intensely dark due to increased DNA content.
3. **High Nuclear-to-Cytoplasmic (N:C) ratio.**
4. **Bizarre / atypical mitotic figures** (tripolar, ring, asymmetric mitoses).
5. *Tumour giant cells* (any one additional feature acceptable).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse *anaplasia* with *dysplasia*. Remember: **dysplasia is reversible and confined by the basement membrane**, whereas **anaplasia is irreversible and a hallmark of full-blown malignancy**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between grading and staging of tumours.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Grading | Staging |
|---|---|---|
| Basis | Microscopic appearance | Anatomical extent of spread |
| Assesses | Degree of differentiation, anaplasia, mitotic count | Size of tumour, lymph node involvement, distant metastasis |
| Tools | Light microscopy, IHC | Imaging (CT, MRI, PET), surgical exploration |
| System | G1, G2, G3, G4 | TNM (Tumour, Node, Metastasis) → Stage I–IV |
| Reflects | Tumour biology and aggressiveness | Disease burden and prognosis |
| Reversible | No | Stage may change with progression |

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Describe the standard procedure for **tissue processing in histopathology**. Add a note on the role of **H&E staining**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **TNM staging system** with suitable examples. How is it different from **histological grading**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Tissue Processing Steps:**

1. **Fixation** — Tissue is immersed in **10% Neutral Buffered Formalin (NBF)** for **6–72 hours** (10:1 fixative-to-tissue ratio). This cross-links proteins and prevents autolysis/putrefaction.

2. **Dehydration** — Water is removed by passing the tissue through ascending grades of ethanol:
$$
70\% \rightarrow 80\% \rightarrow 95\% \rightarrow 100\%\ \text{ethanol}
$$

3. **Clearing** — Ethanol is replaced by **xylene** (a miscible intermediate), since paraffin is not miscible with alcohol.

4. **Infiltration / Impregnation** — Tissue is immersed in **molten paraffin wax (58–60 °C)** for 1–2 hours, which replaces xylene inside the cells.

5. **Embedding / Blocking** — The infiltrated tissue is oriented in a mould and fresh molten paraffin is added to form a solid block.

6. **Sectioning** — Using a **rotary microtome**, sections of **3–5 µm thickness** are cut and floated on a water bath (45–50 °C) before mounting on a glass slide.

7. **Staining** — Sections are stained with **Haematoxylin & Eosin (H&E)**:
   - **Haematoxylin** is a basic dye that binds acidic components (DNA, RNA) → stains **nuclei blue-purple**.
   - **Eosin** is an acidic dye that binds basic components (cytoplasmic proteins) → stains **cytoplasm pink**.

8. **Mounting** — A coverslip is applied using a mounting medium (e.g., DPX) for permanent preservation.

**H&E provides the foundational morphologic contrast** — without it, nuclear detail and cytoplasmic differentiation cannot be assessed. It is the **cornerstone stain** of all histopathology.

**(b) TNM Staging System:**

The TNM system (developed by the **UICC / AJCC**) classifies tumours based on three parameters:

$$
\text{TNM} = (T,\ N,\ M)
$$

| Component | Description | Example (Breast Cancer) |
|---|---|---|
| **T (Tumour)** | Size and local extent of primary tumour (T1–T4) | T2 = tumour > 2 cm but ≤ 5 cm |
| **N (Nodes)** | Involvement of regional lymph nodes (N0–N3) | N1 = 1–3 positive axillary nodes |
| **M (Metastasis)** | Distant metastatic spread (M0 / M1) | M0 = no distant mets |

**Stage Grouping Example (Breast):**

| TNM | Stage |
|---|---|
| T1 N0 M0 | Stage I |
| T2 N1 M0 | Stage II |
| T3 N2 M0 | Stage III |
| Any T / Any N / M1 | Stage IV |

**Difference from Grading:**

| Aspect | Grading | Staging |
|---|---|---|
| Domain | Microscopic (cellular) | Macroscopic (anatomical) |
| Reflects | Tumour biology | Tumour burden |
| Variable | Subjective but pathologist-driven | Imaging / surgical |
| Example | G2 (moderately differentiated) | Stage IIIA |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** What is **immunohistochemistry (IHC)**? Explain its principle and list **six commonly used tumour markers** with their diagnostic significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **histological features of malignancy** with a neat comparison between **benign and malignant tumours**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Immunohistochemistry:**

**Definition:** Immunohistochemistry (IHC) is a laboratory technique that uses **labelled antibodies** to detect specific **antigens (proteins)** in tissue sections, enabling precise identification of cell type, origin, and prognostic/predictive markers.

**Principle:**
$$
\text{Antigen (in tissue)} + \text{Antibody (primary)} \rightarrow \text{Antigen–Antibody Complex}
$$

The bound primary antibody is detected by a **secondary antibody conjugated to an enzyme (Horse-Radish Peroxidase / HRP, or Alkaline Phosphatase / AP)**. The enzyme converts a **chromogenic substrate (DAB)** into a coloured precipitate (brown) at the site of antigen localisation. Slides are then counterstained with **haematoxylin** for nuclear contrast.

**Six Commonly Used Tumour Markers:**

| Marker | Type of Tumour Identified | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **CK7** | Lung, breast, ovarian, endometrial adenocarcinoma | Tissue of origin |
| **CK20** | Colorectal, urothelial, Merkel cell | GI origin |
| **TTF-1** | Lung adenocarcinoma, thyroid carcinoma | Lung / thyroid origin |
| **ER / PR** | Breast, endometrial carcinoma | Hormonal therapy eligibility |
| **HER2 / neu** | Breast, gastric carcinoma | Trastuzumab eligibility |
| **PSA** | Prostate adenocarcinoma | Prostate origin / recurrence |
| **CD45 (LCA)** | Lymphoma / leukaemia | Haematolymphoid origin |
| **S100** | Melanoma, Schwannoma | Neural crest origin |

**(b) Histological Features of Malignancy:**

1. **Pleomorphism** — variation in cell and nuclear size/shape.
2. **Hyperchromatic nuclei** with coarse chromatin clumping.
3. **High N:C ratio** — nucleus dominates cell volume.
4. **Increased and atypical mitoses** — abnormal mitotic figures.
5. **Loss of cellular / tissue polarity** — disorganised architecture.
6. **Invasion** — breach of basement membrane (defining feature of malignancy).
7. **Tumour necrosis** (in high-grade lesions).
8. **Lymphovascular invasion** — tumour cells within vessels.

**Benign vs Malignant Comparison:**

| Feature | Benign | Malignant |
|---|---|---|
| Differentiation | Well | Variable to poor |
| Mitotic rate | Low | High, atypical |
| Capsulation | Usually present | Absent |
| Invasion | No | Yes |
| Metastasis | Never | Yes |
| Recurrence after removal | Rare | Common |
| Growth | Slow, expansile | Rapid, infiltrative |
| Nuclear features | Uniform, normal N:C | Pleomorphic, high N:C |
| Prognosis | Excellent after excision | Variable, often poor |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - In Q on **TNM**, students often **forget to mention T1–T4, N0–N3, M0/M1** with concrete examples — *always write the sub-categories*.
> - In Q on **IHC**, merely writing "it uses antibodies" is **not enough** — you MUST mention the **chromogen (DAB)**, **enzyme (HRP)**, and **counterstain (haematoxylin)** to secure full 7 marks.
> - In Q on **anaplasia**, do **not** confuse with dysplasia — examiners specifically deduct marks for this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Histopathology** is the **gold standard** for cancer diagnosis; **H&E is the workhorse stain**.
- Tissue processing pipeline: **Fixation → Dehydration → Clearing → Infiltration → Embedding → Sectioning → Staining → Mounting**.
- Universal fixative: **10% NBF**; section thickness: **3–5 µm**.
- **Grading = microscopic (G1–G4)**; **Staging = anatomical (TNM → Stage I–IV)**.
- Six hallmarks of malignancy: **Pleomorphism, Hyperchromasia, High N:C ratio, Atypical mitoses, Loss of polarity, Invasion**.
- Carcinoma **in situ** = basement membrane **intact**; Invasive = basement membrane **breached**.
- **IHC** = Antigen–Antibody reaction visualised via **HRP–DAB** (brown) + **Haematoxylin** (blue counterstain).
- Key IHC markers: **CK7, CK20, TTF-1, ER, PR, HER2, PSA, CD45, S100**.
- **Mitotic count** is reported as mitoses per **10 HPF** in the most active ("hot spot") region.
- **Ki-67 LI (%)** = (Ki-67 positive nuclei / total tumour nuclei) × 100; used in NET grading.
- **Frozen section** = intra-operative (~15–20 min, cryostat at **−20 to −30 °C**); **Permanent section** = FFPE pipeline (next day, with IHC).
- **Margin status** is reported as **R0 / R1 / R2** — critical for surgical oncology decisions.
- **Nottingham score** (breast) = Tubule + Pleomorphism + Mitotic count (each 1–3; total 3–9 → Grade I/II/III).
- **Breslow thickness** (mm) — single most important prognostic factor in cutaneous melanoma.
- Mnemonic: *"Grade = Glass (microscope); Stage = Scan (CT/MRI)."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hallmarks of Cancer - Cancer Cell Growth and Spreading of Cancer - Cell Signalling - Effects of Receptors in Cell Signaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT521 - CANCER BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hallmarks of Cancer - Cancer Cell Growth and Spreading of Cancer - Cell Signalling - Effects of Receptors in Cell Signaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Hallmarks of Cancer — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hallmarks of Cancer** are a set of functional capabilities acquired by human cells as they transition from a normal physiological state to a neoplastic (tumor-forming) growth state. They were originally codified by **Douglas Hanahan and Robert A. Weinberg** in the landmark *Cell* paper (2000) and revised in 2011 (10 hallmarks) and 2022 (14 hallmarks). The hallmarks provide a logical framework for understanding the complex biology of **tumorigenesis** and **malignant progression**.

The hallmarks collectively describe *why* a cancer cell behaves differently from a normal cell — at the level of **cell-autonomous capabilities**, **microenvironmental influences**, and **emergent enabling properties**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Broken City"

Imagine a **city** (your body) where every citizen (cell) is supposed to follow a strict set of rules. Now imagine a **gang of rogue citizens** (cancer cells) that have learned to:
- **Generate their own food supply** (self-sufficiency in growth signals)
- **Ignore the city's stop signals** (insensitivity to anti-growth signals)
- **Refuse to die when their term is up** (evading apoptosis)
- **Build endlessly without permits** (limitless replicative potential)
- **Construct their own blood pipelines** (angiogenesis)
- **Travel to other cities and colonize them** (invasion & metastasis)
- **Tap into illegal power grids** (deregulated metabolism)
- **Hide from city police** (immune evasion)
- **Recruit local mobs to help** (tumor-promoting inflammation)

This "Broken City" is exactly what the **14 Hallmarks of Cancer** describe biologically.

### 1.3 The Complete List of Hallmarks (Hanahan & Weinberg 2000 / 2011 / 2022)

| Era | Hallmark | Core Idea |
|-----|----------|-----------|
| **2000** | Self-sufficiency in growth signals | Cancer cells make their own proliferation signals |
| **2000** | Insensitivity to anti-growth signals | Cancer cells ignore stop-commands from tumour suppressors |
| **2000** | Evading apoptosis | Cancer cells resist programmed cell death |
| **2000** | Limitless replicative potential | Cancer cells overcome the Hayflick limit via telomerase |
| **2000** | Sustained angiogenesis | Cancer cells recruit new blood vessels |
| **2000** | Tissue invasion & metastasis | Cancer cells spread to distant organs |
| **2011** | Deregulating cellular energetics | Warburg effect — aerobic glycolysis dominance |
| **2011** | Avoiding immune destruction | Cancer cells evade T-cell and NK-cell surveillance |
| **2011** | Tumour-promoting inflammation | Tumours co-opt inflammatory cells for growth |
| **2022** | Unlocking phenotypic plasticity | Loss of terminal differentiation, lineage switching |
| **2022** | Non-mutational epigenetic reprogramming | Histone/DNA-methylation changes without DNA mutation |
| **2022** | Polymorphic microbiomes | Tumour microbiome influences cancer progression |
| **2022** | Senescent cells | Senescence-induced secretome supports tumour growth |
| **2022** | Mechanical forces | ECM stiffness, solid stress, interstitial pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 2)**: The first 6 hallmarks (canonical "growth and spreading") form the foundation, with **Cell Signalling** and **Receptor Effects** acting as the molecular "switches" that drive most of these capabilities.

### 1.4 Cancer Cell Growth — The Proliferative Engine

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cancer Cell Growth** is defined as the **uncontrolled, autonomous increase in the mass and number of malignant cells**, sustained by dysregulated cell-cycle progression, evasion of contact inhibition, and reprogrammed bioenergetics.

A normal mammalian cell divides only when it receives an **external mitogenic cue** (e.g., EGF, PDGF). A cancer cell, in contrast, achieves **mitogen independence** through:

- **Autocrine stimulation** (secreting its own growth factor)
- **Constitutive receptor activation** (mutated or overexpressed receptors)
- **Downstream pathway activation independent of upstream signals** (e.g., *RAS* or *BRAF* mutations)
- **Loss of negative-feedback loops** (e.g., *PTEN* loss → AKT hyperactivation)

### 1.5 Spreading of Cancer — The Metastatic Cascade

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metastasis** is the process by which cancer cells leave the **primary tumour**, travel through the **circulatory or lymphatic system**, and establish **secondary tumours** in distant organs. It accounts for approximately **90% of cancer-related mortality**.

The cascade proceeds through sequential steps, often called the **"Invasion–Metastasis Cascade"**:

1. **Detachment** — Loss of E-cadherin (epithelial–mesenchymal transition, EMT)
2. **Invasion** — Secretion of matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2, MMP-9)
3. **Intravasation** — Entry into blood/lymph vessels
4. **Survival in circulation** — Evading anoikis
5. **Extravasation** — Exit at a distant capillary bed
6. **Colonization** — Survival and proliferation in the new organ (often follows the "seed and soil" hypothesis)

### 1.6 Cell Signalling — The Master Switchboard

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cell Signalling** is the process by which a cell receives, processes, and responds to **extracellular cues** (ligands, mechanical cues, cell–cell contacts) through a coordinated cascade of **receptors, second messengers, adaptor proteins, and transcription factors** that ultimately alter gene expression, metabolism, or cytoskeletal dynamics.

Four major classes of signalling operate in cancer:

| Class | Example | Cancer Relevance |
|-------|---------|------------------|
| **Autocrine** | Cancer cell secretes EGF to its own EGFR | Glioblastoma, breast cancer |
| **Paracrine** | Stromal fibroblasts secrete HGF to tumour MET receptor | Pancreatic, breast cancer |
| **Endocrine** | Distant gland releases hormone acting on tumour | Oestrogen → ER+ breast cancer |
| **Juxtacrine** | Notch ligand on neighbour cell binds Notch on tumour | T-ALL, breast cancer |

### 1.7 Effects of Receptors in Cell Signalling

Receptors are the **gatekeepers** of cell signalling. In cancer, three key alterations ("the 3 R's") occur:

1. **Receptor Overexpression** — e.g., **HER2/ErbB2** is amplified 50–100× in ~20% of breast cancers
2. **Receptor Mutation** — e.g., **EGFR L858R** in non-small-cell lung cancer (NSCLC)
3. **Receptor Constitutive Activation** — e.g., **BCR-ABL** fusion protein in CML

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constant / Metric**: The **dissociation constant ($K_d$)** of a receptor–ligand pair quantifies binding affinity. A *lower* $K_d$ (typically **$10^{-9}$ to $10^{-12}$ M** for high-affinity receptors) means stronger binding. Cancer-associated mutations often *decrease* $K_d$ for unwanted ligands or create **ligand-independent activation** (effectively $K_d \to \infty$ requirement eliminated).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" and "How" of Each Hallmark

#### Hallmark 1 — Sustaining Proliferative Signalling

**Why does a normal cell need external mitogens?**
Because the default state of a quiescent cell is **G0 arrest**. Mitogens (e.g., EGF, PDGF, FGF) push the cell from G0 → G1 by activating cyclin-D and CDK4/6, which phosphorylate **Rb** to release **E2F** transcription factors.

**How does cancer bypass this?**
- **Autocrine loops** (cancer cell produces its own ligand)
- **Receptor amplification** (e.g., *EGFR* amplification in GBM)
- **Downstream activating mutations** (*RAS*, *BRAF*, *PI3K*)
- **Inactivating mutations in negative regulators** (*NF1*, *PTEN*)

#### Hallmark 2 — Evading Growth Suppressors

Major tumour suppressors and their loss-of-function in cancer:

| Tumour Suppressor | Normal Function | Cancer Example |
|-------------------|-----------------|----------------|
| **RB1** | Inhibits E2F, blocks G1/S transition | Retinoblastoma, osteosarcoma |
| **TP53** | "Guardian of the genome"; induces p21, BAX, PUMA | >50% of all human cancers |
| **PTEN** | Dephosphorylates PIP3 → PIP2 (opposes PI3K) | Endometrial, prostate, GBM |
| **CDKN2A (p16)** | Inhibits CDK4/6 | Melanoma, pancreatic |
| **APC** | Promotes β-catenin degradation | Colorectal (FAP) |
| **VHL** | Targets HIF-1α for degradation | Renal cell carcinoma |

#### Hallmark 3 — Resisting Cell Death (Apoptosis Evasion)

Apoptosis occurs via the **intrinsic (mitochondrial, BCL-2 family)** and **extrinsic (death receptor, FADD/caspase-8)** pathways. Cancer cells evade by:

- **Overexpressing anti-apoptotic BCL-2** (follicular lymphoma: t(14;18))
- **Losing BAX/BAK** pro-apoptotic effectors
- **Reducing caspase-8** expression
- **Upregulating IAPs** (Inhibitors of Apoptosis Proteins)
- **Loss of p53 → reduced PUMA/NOXA**

#### Hallmark 4 — Enabling Replicative Immortality

Normal cells have a **Hayflick limit** of ~50–70 divisions due to **telomere shortening** (each division loses ~50–200 bp from the 3' end). Cancer cells maintain telomere length by:

- **Up-regulating telomerase** (hTERT catalytic subunit) — seen in ~85–90% of cancers
- **ALT pathway** (Alternative Lengthening of Telomeres) — recombination-based

#### Hallmark 5 — Inducing Angiogenesis

Tumours require a **blood supply** beyond 1–2 mm³. The **angiogenic switch** is governed by a balance of pro- and anti-angiogenic factors:

- **Pro-angiogenic**: VEGF-A, FGF-2, PDGF, angiopoietins, IL-8
- **Anti-angiogenic**: thrombospondin-1, angiostatin, endostatin

Cancer cells tilt this balance by:
- Hypoxia → HIF-1α stabilization → VEGF transcription
- *VHL* loss → constitutive HIF-1α → "pseudohypoxia"

#### Hallmark 6 — Activating Invasion & Metastasis

Driven by **Epithelial–Mesenchymal Transition (EMT)**: a reversible programme in which epithelial cells lose cell–cell adhesion (E-cadherin down) and gain mesenchymal motility (N-cadherin, vimentin up). Master regulators: **SNAIL, SLUG, ZEB1/2, TWIST**.

### 2.2 The Central Dogma of Growth Factor Receptor Signalling

Receptor Tyrosine Kinase (RTK) signalling follows a universal "ligand → receptor → adaptor → RAS → cascade → nucleus" pattern:

$$\text{Ligand} \rightarrow \text{RTK dimerization} \rightarrow \text{Autophosphorylation} \rightarrow \text{GRB2/SOS recruitment} \rightarrow \text{RAS-GTP loading} \rightarrow \text{RAF} \rightarrow \text{MEK} \rightarrow \text{ERK} \rightarrow \text{Transcription factors} \rightarrow \text{Gene expression}$$

This single cascade drives the **MAPK pathway** — the most frequently mutated in cancer (mutated in **~40% of all tumours**).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Formula | Description | Unit / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Receptor–Ligand Binding (Langmuir)** | $B = \dfrac{B_{max} \cdot \lbrack L \rbrack}{K_d + \lbrack L \rbrack}$ | Fractional receptor occupancy | $K_d \in \lbrack 10^{-9}, 10^{-12} \rbrack$ M |
| **Michaelis–Menten (Enzyme)** | $v = \dfrac{V_{max} \lbrack S \rbrack}{K_m + \lbrack S \rbrack}$ | Reaction velocity vs. substrate | $K_m$ in M |
| **Hill Equation (Cooperativity)** | $\theta = \dfrac{\lbrack L \rbrack^n}{K_d + \lbrack L \rbrack^n}$ | Cooperative receptor binding (e.g., dimeric RTK: $n=2$) | $n > 1$ = positive cooperativity |
| **Doubling Time (Exponential Growth)** | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t/T_d}$ | Tumour cell number vs. time | $T_d$ = doubling time (days) |
| **Gompertzian Growth** | $N(t) = N_\infty \cdot e^{-b e^{-ct}}$ | Realistic tumour growth (saturating) | Carrying capacity $N_\infty$ |
| **Michaelis–Menten of EGFR** | $v \propto \dfrac{\lbrack EGF \rbrack}{K_m^{EGF} + \lbrack EGF \rbrack}$ | EGFR kinase activity | $K_m^{EGF} \approx 10^{-8}$ M |
| **Hill Coefficient for dimeric RTK** | $n = 2$ | Two ligand-binding events | Dimensionless |
| **Log Cell Kill (Skipper–Schabel)** | $N_{kill} = \log_{10}(\text{fold reduction})$ | Chemotherapy efficacy | e.g., 1-log kill = 90% kill |
| **Michaelis–Menten of PI3K** | $v \propto \dfrac{\lbrack PIP2 \rbrack}{K_m + \lbrack PIP2 \rbrack}$ | PI3K kinetics | $K_m$ in micromolar range |
| **Hill Coefficient for tetrameric channels** | $n = 4$ | e.g., voltage-gated channels | Dimensionless |
| **Cell Cycle Time** | $T_{cell} = T_{G1} + T_{S} + T_{G2} + T_{M}$ | Total cycle | ~24 h typical |
| **Membrane Potential (Nernst)** | $E = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln \dfrac{\lbrack ion \rbrack_{out}}{\lbrack ion \rbrack_{in}}$ | Resting potential | $E_{K^+} \approx -90$ mV |
| **Receptor Density** | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{receptors}}{A_{membrane}}$ | Receptors per μm² | 10²–10⁶/μm² |
| **Cascade Amplification** | $A = n_1 \cdot n_2 \cdot ... \cdot n_k$ | $k$ steps, each $n$-fold amplification | Can reach $10^4$–$10^6$ |
| **Mutation Frequency** | $\mu = \dfrac{N_{mut}}{N_{total}}$ | Driver mutations | ~$10^{-6}$ per gene per cell |
| **P53 Half-life** | $t_{1/2}^{p53} \approx 20$ min (normal) | Stabilized by DNA damage | Hours after stress |
| **Telomere Loss Rate** | $\Delta L = 50–200$ bp/division | Per cell division | Bases |
| **VEGF Expression** | $\lbrack VEGF \rbrack \propto \lbrack HIF\text{-}1\alpha \rbrack$ | Hypoxia-driven | nmol/L range |
| **p53 Decay Constant** | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Tumour regression | $\lambda = \ln 2 / t_{1/2}$ |
| **Tumour Volume** | $V = \dfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$ | Spherical tumour | cm³ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Tip**: Note the use of `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute values in formulas (e.g., $K_d + \lbrack L \rbrack$ is rendered as $\lbrack L \rbrack$ to avoid breaking table syntax). The vertical pipe character $\vert$ is reserved in markdown tables.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Cancer Therapy

- **HER2+ breast cancer** → Trastuzumab (Herceptin) monoclonal antibody targets HER2 receptor
- **EGFR-mutant NSCLC** → Erlotinib, Gefitinib, Osimertinib (small-molecule TKIs)
- **BRAF V600E melanoma** → Vemurafenib, Dabrafenib (RAF inhibitors)
- **BCR-ABL CML** → Imatinib, Dasatinib (ABL kinase inhibitors)
- **Anti-angiogenic** → Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF), Sunitinib (multi-RTK inhibitor)
- **Telomerase** → Imetelstat (oligonucleotide inhibitor in trials)
- **Apoptosis restoration** → Venetoclax (BCL-2 inhibitor) for CLL

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The Hill Equation for a Dimeric Receptor Tyrosine Kinase

We want to derive the binding isotherm for an RTK (e.g., EGFR) that **dimerizes upon ligand binding**. This is a foundational equation in cancer signalling.

**Step 1 — Define the receptor states**
For a monomeric receptor $R$ binding ligand $L$:

$$R + L \rightleftharpoons RL \quad K_a = \dfrac{\lbrack RL \rbrack}{\lbrack R \rbrack \lbrack L \rbrack}$$

The dissociation constant is:

$$K_d = \dfrac{1}{K_a} = \dfrac{\lbrack R \rbrack \lbrack L \rbrack}{\lbrack RL \rbrack}$$

**Step 2 — Define total receptor concentration**
The total receptor $\lbrack R_T \rbrack$ is the sum of free and bound forms:

$$\lbrack R_T \rbrack = \lbrack R \rbrack + \lbrack RL \rbrack$$

**Step 3 — Fractional occupancy (monomeric)**
Define the fraction of bound receptors as $\theta$:

$$\theta = \dfrac{\lbrack RL \rbrack}{\lbrack R_T \rbrack}$$

Substituting the expression for $\lbrack RL \rbrack$ from the binding equation:

$$\lbrack RL \rbrack = \dfrac{\lbrack R \rbrack \lbrack L \rbrack}{K_d}$$

We get:

$$\theta = \dfrac{\lbrack R \rbrack \lbrack L \rbrack / K_d}{\lbrack R \rbrack + \lbrack R \rbrack \lbrack L \rbrack / K_d}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by cancelling $\lbrack R \rbrack$**
Dividing numerator and denominator by $\lbrack R \rbrack$:

$$\theta = \dfrac{\lbrack L \rbrack / K_d}{1 + \lbrack L \rbrack / K_d}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $K_d$:

$$\theta = \dfrac{\lbrack L \rbrack}{K_d + \lbrack L \rbrack}$$

**Step 5 — Extend to cooperative dimerization (Hill)**
For an RTK that requires **two** ligand-binding events to fully activate (cooperativity), the equilibrium is:

$$R + nL \rightleftharpoons RL_n$$

with $n$ = number of ligand-binding sites (for a dimer, $n = 2$).

**Step 6 — The Hill equation**
Following identical algebra with the cooperative binding constant $K_d^{(n)}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\theta &= \dfrac{\lbrack RL_n \rbrack}{\lbrack R_T \rbrack} \\
&= \dfrac{\lbrack R \rbrack \lbrack L \rbrack^n / K_d^{(n)}}{\lbrack R \rbrack + \lbrack R \rbrack \lbrack L \rbrack^n / K_d^{(n)}} \\
&= \dfrac{\lbrack L \rbrack^n}{K_d^{(n)} + \lbrack L \rbrack^n}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Hill equation** with Hill coefficient $n$. For a dimeric RTK, $n = 2$.

**Step 7 — Physical interpretation in cancer**
- **Wild-type EGFR**: $n \approx 1.7$–$1.9$ (near-2, positive cooperativity)
- **EGFR L858R mutant** (lung cancer): $n$ *increases* to ~2.0, meaning the mutant is **fully cooperative** and requires less ligand for activation
- **HER2 dimerization partner** (no known ligand): Heterodimerizes with HER1/HER3, achieving constitutive activation

### 3.2 Derivation: Tumour Growth Kinetics (Exponential → Gompertzian)

**Step 1 — Exponential (early tumour growth)**
In the absence of resource limitation:

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = k N$$

where $k$ is the growth rate constant. Solving:

$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{k t}$$

The **doubling time** is:

$$T_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$$

**Step 2 — Logistic correction**
Real tumours cannot grow forever — they reach a **carrying capacity $N_\infty$** due to nutrient/space limits:

$$\dfrac{dN}{dt} = k N \left(1 - \dfrac{N}{N_\infty}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Gompertz solution**
Empirically, tumour growth follows a **Gompertzian** curve (faster initial decay of growth rate):

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dN}{dt} &= k N \ln \left(\dfrac{N_\infty}{N}\right) \\
\Rightarrow N(t) &= N_\infty \cdot e^{-b \, e^{-c t}}
\end{aligned}$$

where $b$ sets the initial size and $c$ is the decay constant of growth rate.

**Step 4 — Why this matters clinically**
- **Gompertzian growth** explains why late-stage tumours grow *more slowly* than early-stage ones
- Chemotherapy that kills a *constant fraction* (e.g., 99.9%) per cycle produces a *constant log-kill*, but the **absolute number killed decreases** as the tumour shrinks
- This is the **Goldie–Coldman** and **Skipper–Schabel** foundation of cancer chemotherapy dosing

| **HER3** | Overexpression | Breast, gastric | Yes (with HER2) | Patritumab |

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: How an EGFR Mutation Drives a Hallmark

**Step 1 — Normal EGFR activation**
Ligand (EGF) binds → receptor dimerizes → intracellular tyrosine kinase domain autophosphorylates C-terminal tyrosines.

**Step 2 — Adaptor recruitment**
Phospho-Tyr residues recruit **GRB2** (via SH2 domain), which is constitutively bound to **SOS** (a RAS-GEF).

**Step 3 — RAS-GTP loading**
SOS catalyses **RAS-GDP → RAS-GTP** exchange. Active RAS recruits **RAF** to the membrane.

**Step 4 — The MAPK cascade**
- **RAF** phosphorylates and activates **MEK1/2**
- **MEK** phosphorylates and activates **ERK1/2 (MAPK)**
- **ERK** translocates to the nucleus and phosphorylates **ELK1, MYC, FOS**

**Step 5 — Transcriptional output**
MYC and FOS drive transcription of **cyclin-D1**, **CDK4/6**, promoting G0→G1 transition → **sustained proliferation** (Hallmark 1).

**Step 6 — Cancer-specific hijack**
In **EGFR L858R** (lung adenocarcinoma), Step 1 is bypassed: the kinase domain is **constitutively active** without ligand. This means:

- **No external growth factor is required** (autonomy)
- The signal is **continuous** (no downregulation)
- The cell becomes **addicted** to this signal (oncogene addiction)

**Step 7 — Therapeutic vulnerability**
This addiction is exactly what **TKIs exploit**: they bind the ATP-binding pocket of the mutant EGFR, blocking the constitutive kinase activity and inducing **oncogene shock → apoptosis** in addicted cells.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Hallmarks of Cancer". List any four hallmarks with a one-line description of each.**

**Model Answer:**

The *Hallmarks of Cancer* are a logical framework, proposed by **Hanahan and Weinberg (2000, revised 2011, 2022)**, that describes the **distinct functional capabilities acquired by normal cells during their transformation into malignant tumour cells**. They provide a unified set of principles explaining the multi-step nature of tumorigenesis.

**Four hallmarks:**

1. **Sustained proliferative signalling** — Cancer cells produce or respond to growth signals autonomously, bypassing external mitogen requirements.
2. **Evasion of growth suppressors** — Cancer cells inactivate tumour suppressors (e.g., RB, p53) that normally restrain division.
3. **Resisting cell death** — Cancer cells evade apoptosis via BCL-2 overexpression, p53 loss, or IAP upregulation.
4. **Inducing angiogenesis** — Cancer cells recruit new blood vessels by secreting VEGF and other pro-angiogenic factors.

*Each hallmark is worth 1 mark; definition is worth the 4th implicit mark. Total: 3 marks.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the "Warburg effect"? How does it support the metabolic hallmark of cancer?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Warburg effect**, first described by **Otto Warburg in 1924**, is the observation that cancer cells preferentially use **aerobic glycolysis** — converting glucose to lactate even in the presence of abundant oxygen — rather than the more efficient mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation.

**How it supports the metabolic hallmark:**

- It provides rapid ATP generation to support **proliferative demand** (Hallmark 1)
- It generates **glycolytic intermediates** for biosynthesis of nucleotides, amino acids, and lipids
- The acidic tumour microenvironment (from lactate) promotes **invasion and immune evasion** (Hallmarks 6, 8)
- It is now designated as **Hallmark 7: Deregulating cellular energetics** (Hanahan & Weinberg, 2011)

*Definition: 1 mark; mechanism: 1 mark; relation to hallmark: 1 mark. Total: 3 marks.*

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe in detail the **MAPK signalling pathway** activated by receptor tyrosine kinases. Explain how **oncogenic mutations** in this pathway contribute to the hallmark of **sustained proliferative signalling** in cancer.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **mechanisms by which cancer cells resist apoptosis**, citing specific molecular alterations (e.g., BCL-2, p53, IAPs) and providing one therapeutic example that targets each.

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

The **Mitogen-Activated Protein Kinase (MAPK) pathway** is a central kinase cascade transmitting mitogenic signals from cell-surface receptors to the nucleus. It is mutated in approximately **40% of all human cancers**, making it a major driver of tumour proliferation.

**Step 1 — Ligand binding and receptor activation**
Growth factors such as **EGF, PDGF, FGF, HGF** bind to their corresponding **Receptor Tyrosine Kinases (RTKs)** like EGFR, PDGFR, FGFR, and MET. Binding induces receptor **dimerization** and **autophosphorylation of intracellular tyrosine residues**.

**Step 2 — Adaptor recruitment**
Phosphotyrosine residues recruit adaptor proteins such as **GRB2** (via its SH2 domain), which is constitutively bound to **SOS** (Son of Sevenless, a RAS-Guanine Exchange Factor or GEF).

**Step 3 — RAS activation**
**SOS** catalyses the exchange of **GDP for GTP on RAS** (H-RAS, K-RAS, N-RAS), activating it. Active **RAS-GTP** anchors to the inner plasma membrane and recruits downstream effectors.

**Step 4 — The three-tiered kinase cascade**

$$\text{RAF} \rightarrow \text{MEK} \rightarrow \text{ERK (MAPK)}$$

- **RAF** (ARAF, BRAF, CRAF) is a MAPKKK that phosphorylates and activates
- **MEK1/2** (MAPK/ERK kinase), a MAPKK that phosphorylates and activates
- **ERK1/2** (Extracellular signal-Regulated Kinase), the terminal MAPK

**Step 5 — Nuclear translocation and transcription**
Activated **ERK1/2** dimerizes and translocates to the nucleus, where it phosphorylates transcription factors including **ELK1, MYC, FOS, JUN**, driving expression of:
- **Cyclin-D1** → activates CDK4/6
- **CDK4/6-cyclin D** → phosphorylates RB → releases E2F
- **E2F** → drives S-phase gene expression → **cell cycle progression**

**Step 6 — Oncogenic mutations in the pathway**

| Component | Common Alteration | Cancer |
|-----------|-------------------|--------|
| EGFR | L858R, exon 19 deletion | NSCLC |
| HER2 | Amplification | Breast |
| RAS (K-RAS) | G12D, G12V | Pancreatic (95%), colorectal (40%) |
| BRAF | V600E | Melanoma (50%), thyroid, colorectal |
| NF1 (RAS-GAP) | Loss-of-function | Neurofibromatosis, melanoma |
| ERK2 | Rare amplification | Some gastric cancers |

**Contribution to the Hallmark of Sustained Proliferative Signalling:**

These mutations make the pathway **constitutively active**, meaning the cell is permanently pushed into the cycle regardless of external growth factor availability. This achieves the **mitogen independence** that defines the first hallmark.

*RTK activation: 1 mark; GRB2-SOS-RAS: 1.5 marks; RAF-MEK-ERK cascade: 1.5 marks; nuclear events: 1 mark; oncogenic mutations table: 1 mark; link to hallmark: 1 mark. Total: 7 marks.*

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

Apoptosis is a tightly regulated, evolutionarily conserved cell death programme. Two principal pathways exist:

**1. Intrinsic (Mitochondrial) Pathway** — triggered by cellular stress (DNA damage, growth factor withdrawal, oxidative stress).
- **p53** induces transcription of **BAX, PUMA, NOXA**
- **BAX/BAK** oligomerize on the mitochondrial outer membrane
- **Cytochrome c** is released into the cytosol
- **Apaf-1 + cytochrome c** form the **apoptosome**
- **Caspase-9** is activated → cleaves and activates **executioner caspases-3/7**
- **BCL-2, BCL-XL, MCL-1** block this pathway

**2. Extrinsic (Death Receptor) Pathway** — triggered by ligand binding to death receptors (Fas/CD95, DR4/5, TNFR1).
- Ligand (FasL, TRAIL, TNF) binds receptor
- **FADD** (Fas-Associated Death Domain) is recruited
- **Caspase-8** is activated → cleaves and activates **caspase-3** directly *or* cleaves **BID** → tBID → mitochondrial amplification

**Mechanisms of cancer cell resistance to apoptosis:**

| Mechanism | Molecular Alteration | Cancer Example | Therapy |
|-----------|----------------------|----------------|---------|
| **BCL-2 overexpression** | t(14;18) translocation → BCL-2 under IgG enhancer | Follicular lymphoma | **Venetoclax** (BCL-2 inhibitor) — FDA approved for CLL |
| **p53 loss-of-function** | TP53 mutation in >50% cancers | Many solid tumours | **APR-246** (reactivates mutant p53) — in trials |
| **BAX/BAK loss** | Epigenetic silencing | Colorectal, gastric | — (no direct drug) |
| **Caspase-8 downregulation** | Epigenetic silencing | Neuroblastoma, SCLC | — |
| **IAP overexpression** (XIAP, cIAP1/2, survivin) | Gene amplification | Many cancers | **LCL-161** (Smac mimetic) — in trials |
| **Fas decoy receptor (DcR3)** | Overexpression | Colon, lung | — |
| **FLIP overexpression** (inhibits caspase-8) | Transcriptional upregulation | Many cancers | — |

**Therapeutic Examples (one each):**

- **Venetoclax** (ABT-199): A BH3-mimetic that binds BCL-2, displacing pro-apoptotic BAX/BAK. Approved for **chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (CLL)** and AML.
- **APR-246 / Eprenetapopt**: Restores wild-type conformation to mutant p53 (cysteine reactivity at the core domain). In trials for **TP53-mutant MDS and AML**.

*Intrinsic pathway: 1.5 marks; extrinsic pathway: 1.5 marks; resistance mechanisms table: 2 marks; therapeutic examples: 1 mark each. Total: 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Hallmarks of Cancer** proposed by Hanahan and Weinberg. Discuss the **original six hallmarks (2000)** in detail with appropriate molecular examples.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **process of tumour metastasis** (the invasion–metastasis cascade). Explain the role of **Epithelial–Mesenchymal Transition (EMT)** and **matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs)** in this process.

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Introduction:**
The *Hallmarks of Cancer* are a conceptual framework proposed by **Hanahan and Weinberg in their 2000 *Cell* paper ("The Hallmarks of Cancer")** and updated in 2011 and 2022. They summarize the **acquired functional capabilities** that allow normal cells to become malignant tumours. The original 2000 paper outlined **six core hallmarks**.

**The Six Hallmarks of Cancer (2000):**

**1. Self-Sufficiency in Growth Signals (Sustained Proliferative Signalling)**
Normal cells require external mitogens to proliferate. Cancer cells bypass this need by:
- **Autocrine production** of growth factors (e.g., PDGF in glioblastoma)
- **Overexpression of receptors** (e.g., HER2 in breast cancer)
- **Constitutive activation of downstream pathways** (e.g., *K-RAS* mutations in pancreatic cancer)

**2. Insensitivity to Anti-Growth Signals (Evading Growth Suppressors)**
Cells normally obey signals that halt proliferation. Cancer cells subvert these by:
- **Loss of RB** (retinoblastoma, osteosarcoma)
- **Loss or mutation of p53** (>50% of all cancers)
- **Loss of CDKN2A/p16** (melanoma, pancreatic)

**3. Evading Apoptosis (Resisting Cell Death)**
Apoptosis is the cell's self-destruct mechanism. Cancer cells evade via:
- **BCL-2 overexpression** (t(14;18) in follicular lymphoma)
- **Loss of p53** (reduces BAX/PUMA/NOXA induction)
- **Upregulation of IAPs** (e.g., survivin)

**4. Limitless Replicative Potential (Enabling Replicative Immortality)**
Normal cells have a finite number of divisions (Hayflick limit). Cancer cells overcome this via:
- **Telomerase reactivation** (~85–90% of cancers upregulate hTERT)
- **ALT pathway** (alternative lengthening of telomeres, recombination-based)

**5. Sustained Angiogenesis (Inducing Access to Vasculature)**
Tumours need a blood supply. The **angiogenic switch** is driven by:
- **VEGF-A** overexpression (induced by HIF-1α in hypoxia)
- **Loss of VHL** → constitutive HIF-1α → pseudohypoxia in renal cell carcinoma
- **Therapy**: Bevacizumab (anti-VEGF monoclonal antibody)

**6. Tissue Invasion and Metastasis**
Cancer cells spread via the **invasion–metastasis cascade**, involving:
- **EMT** (loss of E-cadherin, gain of vimentin)
- **MMP secretion** (MMP-2, MMP-9 degrade collagen IV in basement membrane)
- **Intravasation → extravasation → colonization**

**Enabling Conditions (added later but foundational):**

- **Genomic instability** — defects in DNA repair (BRCA1/2 in breast/ovarian; MMR in Lynch syndrome)
- **Tumour-promoting inflammation** — TAMs, TANs support tumour growth

*Introduction: 1 mark; 6 hallmarks × 1 mark each = 6 marks. Total: 7 marks.*

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Diagram of the Metastasis Cascade:**

```
PRIMARY TUMOR
   ↓
EMT (Loss of E-cadherin; Gain of N-cadherin, Vimentin)
   ↓
INVASION (MMP-2, MMP-9 degrade basement membrane)
   ↓
INTRAVASATION (entering blood/lymph)
   ↓
SURVIVAL IN CIRCULATION (resistance to anoikis; platelet cloaking)
   ↓
EXTRAVASATION (exiting at distant capillary bed)
   ↓
COLONIZATION (micrometastasis → angiogenic switch → macrometastasis)
   ↓
SECONDARY TUMOR (e.g., liver, lung, bone, brain)
```

**Step-by-Step Explanation:**

**Step 1 — Epithelial–Mesenchymal Transition (EMT)**
EMT is a **reversible cellular programme** in which epithelial tumour cells lose cell–cell junctions and acquire mesenchymal migratory properties.

- **E-cadherin** is transcriptionally repressed by **SNAIL, SLUG, ZEB1/2, TWIST**
- **Vimentin, N-cadherin, fibronectin** are upregulated
- Cytoskeletal remodelling allows the cell to detach and move

**Step 2 — Invasion through the Basement Membrane**
Tumour cells secrete **matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs)**:
- **MMP-2 (Gelatinase A)** and **MMP-9 (Gelatinase B)** degrade **Type IV collagen**, the principal component of the basement membrane
- Other MMPs (MMP-1, MMP-7, MMP-14/MT1-MMP) degrade various ECM components
- This allows tumour cells to breach the basement membrane and enter the **interstitial stroma**

**Step 3 — Intravasation**
Cancer cells enter thin-walled lymphatic or blood vessels (often via "transendothelial migration"). This is facilitated by:
- Increased vascular permeability (VEGF)
- Tumour-associated macrophages (TAMs) that secrete CSF1

**Step 4 — Survival in Circulation**
- **Anoikis** (detachment-induced apoptosis) is resisted via **PI3K-AKT signalling, TrkB, and caveolin-1**
- **Platelets** coat circulating tumour cells (CTCs), shielding them from NK cell attack
- Less than 0.01% of CTCs successfully form metastases

**Step 5 — Extravasation**
CTCs adhere to the endothelium of distant capillary beds (lung, liver, bone, brain being common sites — Paget's "seed and soil" hypothesis) and squeeze through the vessel wall.

**Step 6 — Colonization**
A few CTCs survive at the new site, proliferate to form a **micrometastasis**, undergo the **angiogenic switch**, and grow into a clinically detectable **macrometastasis**.

*Diagram: 2 marks; EMT mechanism: 1.5 marks; MMP role: 1.5 marks; cascade steps: 2 marks. Total: 7 marks.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Don't list hallmarks without a molecular example** — KTU examiners award only ~50% credit for plain lists. Always couple the hallmark with a gene/protein and a cancer type.
> 2. **Don't confuse "metastasis" with "angiogenesis"** — these are *separate* hallmarks. Angiogenesis is local blood-vessel recruitment; metastasis is *distant* spread.
> 3. **Don't say "EGFR mutation causes cancer"** without specifying *which* mutation (L858R, T790M, exon 19 del) and *which* cancer (NSCLC). Generic statements lose 50% of the marks.
> 4. **EMT answer without master regulators (SNAIL, SLUG, ZEB, TWIST)** is incomplete. Always mention them.
> 5. **Hill equation for cooperative receptor binding** — don't forget $n = 2$ for dimeric RTKs. Many students write the standard Michaelis–Menten form (i.e., $n = 1$), which is for monomeric binding.
> 6. **BCL-2 is anti-apoptotic, not pro-apoptotic** — this is a common error. BAX, BAK, BID, BCL-XS are pro-apoptotic; BCL-2, BCL-XL, MCL-1 are anti-apoptotic.
> 7. **Avoid the generic "radiation/chemotherapy" answer** when asked about therapy. KTU wants the *specific drug* (e.g., Imatinib, Trastuzumab, Vemurafenib).

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hallmarks of Cancer (Hanahan & Weinberg)**: 6 original (2000) → 10 (2011) → 14 (2022). Know all 6 original ones thoroughly; be aware of the additions.
- **Two enabling conditions**: Genomic instability + Tumour-promoting inflammation (these *enable* the hallmarks to be acquired).
- **Six canonical hallmarks**: Proliferative signalling, growth suppressor evasion, resisting cell death, replicative immortality, angiogenesis, invasion/metastasis.
- **Cancer cell growth** = uncontrolled proliferation driven by mitogen independence (RTK amplification, RAS/RAF/PI3K mutations, autocrine loops).
- **Spreading of cancer (metastasis)** = invasion–metastasis cascade. EMT + MMPs + intravasation + survival + extravasation + colonization.
- **Cell signalling** = four classes (autocrine, paracrine, endocrine, juxtacrine). All cancers exploit these.
- **Receptor alterations in cancer**: Overexpression (HER2), Mutation (EGFR L858R), Constitutive activation (BCR-ABL), Ligand-independent dimerization.
- **MAPK pathway**: RTK → GRB2/SOS → RAS → RAF → MEK → ERK → nucleus → cyclin D1 → proliferation. Mutated in ~40% of all cancers.
- **PI3K-AKT pathway**: RTK → PI3K → PIP3 → AKT → mTOR + BAD inactivation + FoxO → survival + growth. Loss of PTEN removes a brake.
- **Apoptosis pathways**: Intrinsic (mitochondrial, BCL-2/BAX, p53) and Extrinsic (death receptors, FADD, caspase-8). Cancer evades both.
- **Angiogenesis**: VEGF is the master regulator; HIF-1α drives it in hypoxia; VHL loss gives "pseudohypoxia".
- **Telomerase**: Reactivated in ~85–90% cancers; ALT is the alternative.
- **Hayflick limit**: ~50–70 divisions for normal cells; telomere attrition is the molecular clock.
- **EMT master regulators**: SNAIL, SLUG, ZEB1, ZEB2, TWIST. Loss of E-cadherin is the molecular signature.
- **MMPs in invasion**: MMP-2 and MMP-9 degrade Type IV collagen of the basement membrane.
- **Warburg effect**: Aerobic glycolysis in cancer; Hallmark 7. Supports both bioenergetics and biosynthesis.
- **Oncogene addiction**: Cancer cells become dependent on a single oncogenic signal (e.g., EGFR L858R); this is the basis of targeted therapy success.
- **Hill equation for dimeric RTK**: $\theta = \dfrac{\lbrack L \rbrack^n}{K_d^{(n)} + \lbrack L \rbrack^n}$, with $n = 2$.
- **Doubling time formula**: $T_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{k}$. Exponential at first, Gompertzian later.
- **Drug examples to memorize** (very high yield):
  - **Trastuzumab** → HER2+ breast cancer
  - **Imatinib** → BCR-ABL (CML), c-KIT (GIST)
  - **Erlotinib / Osimertinib** → EGFR-mutant NSCLC
  - **Vemurafenib** → BRAF V600E melanoma
  - **Bevacizumab** → anti-VEGF (multiple cancers)
  - **Venetoclax** → BCL-2 (CLL)
  - **Tamoxifen / Fulvestrant** → ER+ breast cancer
  - **Enzalutamide** → AR+ prostate cancer
- **VHL loss → HIF-1α stabilization → VEGF → renal cell carcinoma angiogenesis** is a complete one-line answer worth full marks for "give an example of pseudohypoxia in cancer".
- **Seed and soil hypothesis** (Paget, 1889): Metastasis requires compatibility between tumour cell (seed) and distant organ microenvironment (soil). Examples: Prostate → bone; colorectal → liver; breast → bone, brain, lung, liver.
- **Angiogenic switch** = the shift from avascular to vascular tumour growth, controlled by pro- vs. anti-angiogenic factors. Once a tumour exceeds ~1–2 mm³, it *must* undergo this switch or remain dormant.
- **Anoikis** = apoptosis induced by loss of cell–matrix attachment. Cancer cells resist it via TrkB, caveolin-1, and PI3K-AKT signalling during transit in circulation.
- **Exosomes and CTCs (Circulating Tumour Cells)** are emerging biomarkers for metastasis monitoring in modern clinical practice.
- **Final high-yield tip**: Always draw a labelled diagram for any pathway-based question. KTU examiners routinely award 1–2 marks for clear, accurate, hand-drawn or schematic diagrams with arrows showing directionality.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal Switches - G-protein Coupled Receptors
topics.content:
