> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT521 - CANCER BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Signal Switches - G-protein Coupled Receptors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **G-protein Coupled Receptors (GPCRs)** are the **largest superfamily of cell-surface receptors** in eukaryotes, characterized by a canonical **seven-transmembrane (7TM) α-helical topology**. They function as **molecular signal switches** that transduce extracellular chemical cues (hormones, neurotransmitters, lipids, photons, chemokines) into intracellular responses by catalyzing the **GDP → GTP exchange** on heterotrimeric G-proteins (Gαβγ).

In the context of **cancer biology**, GPCRs occupy a central oncogenic position because they couple to downstream effector cascades that control the **hallmarks of cancer**: sustained proliferative signaling, evasion of growth suppression, resistance to cell death, induced angiogenesis, activation of invasion/metastasis, and reprogramming of cellular metabolism.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 scheme treats GPCRs as **"Signal Switches"** — a deliberate pedagogical framing emphasizing their binary on/off behavior (active GTP-bound vs. inactive GDP-bound) and their role as **gatekeepers of mitogenic signaling axes** frequently hijacked in malignancy.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **letterbox on a house door** that is wired to the **electricity meter inside**.

- The **letterbox (GPCR)** sits in the cell membrane — it has a slot outside (extracellular ligand-binding domain) and a switch mechanism inside.
- The **letter (hormone/ligand)** drops into the slot.
- This triggers the **internal switch (G-protein)** to flip from "OFF" (GDP bound) to "ON" (GTP bound).
- Once ON, the switch sends a **signal to appliances inside the house** (adenylyl cyclase, phospholipase C, ion channels, Rho GTPases) — the lights turn on (second messengers generated).
- After a brief period, the **switch auto-flips back to OFF** (intrinsic GTPase activity hydrolyzes GTP → GDP), turning off the lights.
- In **cancer**, the letterbox is jammed open, the switch is stuck in the ON position, or the appliances have learned to behave like lights on a generator that never stops — leading to uncontrolled growth.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following constants and metrics are critical for quantitative GPCR pharmacology questions in KTU examinations.

- **Number of human GPCRs encoded:** ~**800 functional receptors** (excluding olfactory receptors) — the largest druggable receptor family.
- **GPCR share of FDA-approved drug targets:** **~34%** of all marketed drugs act on GPCRs.
- **Oncogenic GPCRs identified in cancer:** >**20 distinct receptors** are causally linked to tumorigenesis.
- **GTP hydrolysis rate (kcat) of Gα subunit:** ~**1–5 min⁻¹** at 37 °C.
- **Standard nomenclature:** GPCRs are classified by the **GRAFS system** into **Glutamate (G), Rhodopsin (R), Adhesion (A), Frizzled/Taste2 (F), and Secretin (S)** families.

---

## 1.4 Cancer-Relevance Snapshot

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study GPCRs in cancer biology?**
> Because they sit at the **apex of the signaling pyramid** that drives the 10 Hallmarks of Cancer (Hanahan & Weinberg, 2011/2022). Dysregulated GPCR signaling is detected in **>90% of breast, prostate, colon, and pancreatic cancers**, and **GPCRs are the most successful target class in modern pharmacology** — making them the natural bridge between cancer biology and translational therapeutics.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a GPCR — The 7-Transmembrane Template

A prototypical GPCR (e.g., β₂-adrenergic receptor) is composed of:

- **Extracellular N-terminus:** Often glycosylated; binds large ligands.
- **7 transmembrane α-helices (TM1–TM7):** Hydrophobic core, traverse the lipid bilayer.
- **3 Extracellular loops (ECL1–ECL3):** Participate in ligand recognition.
- **3 Intracellular loops (ICL1–ICL3):** Critical contact sites for heterotrimeric G-proteins and β-arrestins.
- **Intracellular C-terminus:** Contains palmitoylation site, serine/threonine clusters for GRK phosphorylation.

---

## 2.2 The Heterotrimeric G-Protein — The Molecular Switch

The **G-protein** is a heterotrimer composed of three subunits:

| Subunit | Molecular Weight | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Gα** | **~39–46 kDa** | Binds GDP/GTP; has intrinsic **GTPase** activity; main switch |
| **Gβ** | **~36 kDa** | Forms obligate dimer with Gγ; scaffolds effectors |
| **Gγ** | **~7–9 kDa** | Lipid-anchored (prenylated); tethers complex to membrane |

### The Switch Cycle (4-State Model)

1. **State 0 — Resting (OFF):** Gα·GDP + Gβγ trimer is pre-assembled and docked at the receptor's ICL surface.
2. **State 1 — Activation:** Agonist binding to GPCR → conformational shift → GPCR acts as a **Guanine nucleotide Exchange Factor (GEF)** → GDP dissociates → GTP binds Gα.
3. **State 2 — Active (ON):** Gα·GTP dissociates from Gβγ. Both **Gα·GTP** and **Gβγ** independently activate downstream effectors.
4. **State 3 — Deactivation:** Gα's intrinsic GTPase hydrolyzes GTP → GDP + Pi → Gα·GDP reassociates with Gβγ → returns to State 0.

> [!NOTE]
> The **turnover number (kcat)** of the GTPase is accelerated ~1000-fold by **Regulators of G-protein Signaling (RGS proteins)** — critical negative regulators frequently lost in cancer.

---

## 2.3 Gα Subfamilies and Their Effectors (The Core Onco-Relevant Pathways)

| Gα Family | Members | Key Effector | Second Messenger | Cancer Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Gαs** | Gαs, Gαolf | **Adenylyl cyclase ↑** | cAMP ↑ | PKA activation → CREB → proliferation, survival |
| **Gαi/o** | Gαi1/2/3, Gαo, Gαz | **Adenylyl cyclase ↓** | cAMP ↓ | Releases PKA inhibition; activates PI3K/AKT |
| **Gαq/11** | Gαq, Gα11, Gα14, Gα16 | **Phospholipase C-β (PLCβ)** | IP₃ + DAG | Ca²⁺ release, PKC activation → MAPK, NF-κB |
| **Gα12/13** | Gα12, Gα13 | **RhoGEFs (p115, PDZ-RhoGEF)** | Rho-GTP ↑ | Cytoskeletal remodeling, invasion, YAP/TAZ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Memorize the **Gαs → cAMP ↑** and **Gαq → PLC → IP₃/DAG** cascades — these appear in **>70% of past university exam questions** on signal transduction.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Relationship | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Receptor occupancy (Langmuir) | $\theta = \dfrac{[L]}{[L] + K_d}$ | dimensionless | Fractional occupancy |
| Ligand binding affinity | $K_d = \dfrac{k_{off}}{k_{on}}$ | M (molar) | Lower Kd = higher affinity |
| Hill equation (cooperativity) | $\theta = \dfrac{[L]^n}{[L]^n + K_d^n}$ | dimensionless | n > 1 = positive cooperativity |
| G-protein amplification | $N_{effector} = R \cdot A \cdot E$ | dimensionless | R=receptors, A=active Gα, E=effectors activated |
| Intrinsic GTPase (1st order) | $[G\alpha \cdot GTP]_t = [G\alpha \cdot GTP]_0 \cdot e^{-k_{cat} t}$ | M | k_cat in s⁻¹ |
| cAMP production rate | $\dfrac{d[cAMP]}{dt} = V_{max}^{AC} \cdot \dfrac{[G\alpha_s \cdot GTP]}{K_m + [G\alpha_s \cdot GTP]}$ | M s⁻¹ | Classic Michaelis-Menten |
| EC50 (potency) | Concentration giving 50% maximal response | M | Often converted to pEC₂₀ = -log₁₀(EC₅₀) |
| GTPase acceleration by RGS | $k_{cat}^{obs} = k_{cat}^{basal} + k_{cat}^{RGS}$ | s⁻¹ | Additive acceleration |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Constraint:** All vertical bars use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking the markdown table parser.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering & Biomedical Utility

- **Drug discovery:** ~**34% of FDA-approved drugs** target GPCRs (antihistamines, antipsychotics, opioids, β-blockers).
- **Cancer therapeutics in trials:** CXCR4 antagonists (AMD3100/Plerixafor), PAR1 antagonists (Vorapaxar), LPA1 inhibitors (BMS-986020).
- **Biosensor design:** GPCR-based **FRET/BRET biosensors** are engineered to monitor real-time cAMP, Ca²⁺, and RhoA dynamics in living tumor cells.
- **Systems biology:** GPCRs are nodes in **Boolean signaling networks** used to model tumor cell-fate decisions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — The Steady-State Active Gα Fraction

Let $G_T$ be the total G-protein concentration, $k_{on}^G$ be the receptor-catalyzed GDP→GTP exchange rate, and $k_{cat}$ be the intrinsic GTP hydrolysis rate.

**Step 1.** Active Gα·GTP is formed at rate $k_{on}^G \cdot [GPCR^*] \cdot [G\alpha \cdot GDP]$ and destroyed at rate $k_{cat} \cdot [G\alpha \cdot GTP]$.

**Step 2.** At steady state, formation = destruction:

$$k_{on}^G \cdot [GPCR^*] \cdot [G\alpha \cdot GDP] = k_{cat} \cdot [G\alpha \cdot GTP]$$

This implies the steady-state active fraction:

$$\dfrac{[G\alpha \cdot GTP]_{ss}}{[G_T]} = \dfrac{k_{on}^G \cdot [GPCR^*]}{k_{cat} + k_{on}^G \cdot [GPCR^*]}$$

**Step 3.** This has the same mathematical form as a **Michaelis-Menten saturation** — a high receptor activation pushes the fraction toward 1 (full ON), while high GTPase activity (RGS overexpression) pushes it back toward 0 (OFF).

**Step 4.** In **cancer cells**, activating mutations in Gα subunits (e.g., **Gαs gsp oncogene** in pituitary tumors; **Gαq Q209L** in uveal melanoma) or **GPCR overexpression** (CXCR4, PAR1) push this fraction upward → **constitutive signaling**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — GPCR Switches in Specific Cancers

| Cancer Type | GPCR Involved | Genetic Event | Downstream Pathway | Therapeutic Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pituitary adenoma** | Gαs (gsp) | **gsp oncogene (R201C/H)** | cAMP ↑ → GH hypersecretion | Somatostatin receptor ligands |
| **Uveal melanoma** | Gαq (Q209L/P) | Activating mutation | PLCβ → PKC → MAPK | MEK inhibitors (clinical trial) |
| **Breast cancer** | CXCR4, CXCR7 | Overexpression | Gαi → PI3K/AKT, MAPK | CXCR4 antagonists (AMD3100) |
| **Pancreatic cancer** | PAR1, LPA1/2 | Hyperexpression | Gαq, Gα12/13 → Rho, YAP | Vorapaxar (PAR1) |
| **Colorectal cancer** | Prostaglandin E2 receptors (EP2) | COX-2 overexpression | Gαs → cAMP → β-catenin | Aspirin chemoprevention |
| **Lymphoma** | S1PR1 | Mutation in ICL3 | Sustained signaling | FTY720/Fingolimod |
| **Gastric cancer** | CXCR2, FPR1 | Neutrophil recruitment | Gαi → AKT, STAT3 | CXCR2 antagonists |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note for valuation:** In KTU answer sheets, always cite **specific receptor names, specific Gα family, specific effector, and specific cancer outcome** — generic "GPCR signaling promotes cancer" answers receive only partial marks.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Amplification Through a GPCR Cascade

**Problem:** A single tumor cell expresses **5 × 10⁴ CXCR4 receptors**. Each activated receptor catalytically activates **10 G-proteins per second**. Each Gαs·GTP activates **1 adenylyl cyclase** molecule, which produces **10³ cAMP molecules per second**. Calculate the cAMP amplification ratio (cAMP per second per ligand-bound receptor).

**Step 1.** Receptors activated: $R = 5 \times 10^4$ (assume fully occupied at saturating SDF-1).

**Step 2.** G-proteins activated per second: $G = 10 \times R = 5 \times 10^5$ s⁻¹.

**Step 3.** Adenylyl cyclases activated per second: $AC = 1 \times G = 5 \times 10^5$ s⁻¹.

**Step 4.** cAMP molecules per second: $cAMP = 10^3 \times AC = 5 \times 10^8$ s⁻¹.

**Step 5.** Amplification ratio per receptor:

$$\text{Amplification} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^8}{5 \times 10^4} = 10^4 \text{ cAMP/receptor/s}$$

**Biological interpretation:** A single chemokine-bound CXCR4 receptor can drive the production of **10,000 cAMP molecules per second**, illustrating how **one ligand molecule triggers massive intracellular amplification** — a hallmark feature exploited by cancer cells for sustained mitogenic signaling.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — EC50 from a Dose-Response Curve

A tumor cell line is treated with a CXCR4 agonist, and cAMP is measured:

| [Agonist] (nM) | cAMP response (% max) |
|---|---|
| 1 | 5 |
| 10 | 28 |
| 100 | 70 |
| 1000 | 95 |

**Step 1.** Find the concentration at 50% response — from the table, this lies between **10 and 100 nM**, closer to 100 nM.

**Step 2.** Linear interpolation between (10 nM, 28%) and (100 nM, 70%):

$$[L]_{50} = 10 + (100 - 10) \times \dfrac{50 - 28}{70 - 28} = 10 + 90 \times \dfrac{22}{42} \approx 47.1 \text{ nM}$$

**Step 3.** Therefore:

$$EC_{50} \approx 47.1 \text{ nM}$$

**Step 4.** Compute pEC₅₀:

$$pEC_{50} = -\log_{10}(47.1 \times 10^{-9}) \approx 7.33$$

This value is consistent with **high-potency** CXCR4 agonism, useful for comparing drug candidates in KTU experimental design questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow — GPCR Signal Switch

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L[Extracellular Ligand / Hormone / Chemokine] --> R[GPCR 7TM Receptor in Plasma Membrane]
    R -->|Agonist binding| RA[Activated Receptor Conformational State]
    RA -->|GEF activity| GEF[G-protein GEF Catalysis]
    GEF -->|GDP release GTP binding| GA[Active G alpha GTP plus G beta gamma]
    GA -->|Branch 1 Gs| AC1[Adenylyl Cyclase Activation]
    GA -->|Branch 2 Gi| AC2[Adenylyl Cyclase Inhibition]
    GA -->|Branch 3 Gq| PLC[Phospholipase C beta Activation]
    GA -->|Branch 4 G12 13| RHO[Rho GEF Activation]
    AC1 -->|cAMP up| PKA1[PKA Activation]
    AC2 -->|cAMP down| PKA2[PKA Inhibition]
    PLC -->|IP3 plus DAG| PKC[PKC plus Ca2+ Release]
    PKA1 -->|CREB phosphorylation| PROL[Proliferation and Survival]
    PKA2 -->|AKT activation| PROL
    PKC -->|MAPK and NF kB| INV[Invasion and Inflammation]
    RHO -->|Rho GTP and YAP| INV
    PROL --> CANCER[Hallmarks of Cancer Activation]
    INV --> CANCER
    RA -->|GRK phosphorylation| BETA[Beta Arrestin Recruitment]
    BETA -->|Desensitization| DES[Receptor Internalization and Signal Termination]
    BETA -->|Scaffolding| MAPK2[Arrestin Dependent MAPK Signaling]
    RGS[RGS Proteins] -->|GTPase acceleration| GA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The flowchart shows the four major Gα branches (Gαs, Gαi, Gαq, Gα12/13) that converge on the **Hallmarks of Cancer**. The right-side limb shows **β-arrestin-mediated desensitization** and **arrestin-scaffolded signaling** — a second, G-protein-independent wave of signal that is itself onco-relevant.

---

## 4.2 Companion Mermaid — Switch Cycle State Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Resting
    Resting --> Active: Ligand binds GPCR GPCR acts as GEF GDP released GTP bound
    Active --> Effectors: G alpha GTP and G beta gamma dissociate activate adenylyl cyclase PLC Rho GEF
    Effectors --> Active: Signaling
    Active --> Resting: RGS accelerates GTPase GTP hydrolyzed to GDP Pi G alpha rebinds G beta gamma
    Resting --> Desensitized: GRK phosphorylates C terminus beta arrestin binds receptor internalized
    Desensitized --> Resting: Receptor recycled resensitized
    Desensitized --> ArrestinSignaling: Beta arrestin scaffolds MAPK NF kB
    ArrestinSignaling --> [*]
```

---

## 4.3 Schematic of GPCR Topology in the Plasma Membrane

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EXT[Extracellular Space] -->|Ligand binding pocket| ECL1
    ECL1[ECL1] --> TM1[TM1 Helix]
    TM1 --> ICL1[ICL1]
    ICL1 --> TM2[TM2]
    TM2 --> ECL2[ECL2]
    ECL2 --> TM3[TM3]
    TM3 --> ICL2[ICL2 G protein contact]
    ICL2 --> TM4[TM4]
    TM4 --> ECL3[ECL3]
    ECL3 --> TM5[TM5]
    TM5 --> ICL3[ICL3 critical for G protein coupling]
    ICL3 --> TM6[TM6]
    TM6 -->|Drifts outward on activation| TM7[TM7]
    TM7 --> CT[C terminus palmitoylation and GRK sites]
    CT --> CYTO[Cytoplasm Heterotrimeric G protein docks here]
    GA[G alpha GDP G beta gamma] --> CYTO
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 2]`
**Define a G-protein coupled receptor. Why is it considered a molecular "signal switch" in the context of cancer signaling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** A GPCR is a seven-transmembrane domain cell-surface receptor that couples extracellular ligand binding to intracellular G-protein activation.
- **Signal switch property (1 mark):** The GPCR catalyzes the binary exchange of GDP for GTP on the Gα subunit, toggling it between an **inactive (GDP-bound, OFF)** state and an **active (GTP-bound, ON)** state.
- **Cancer relevance (1 mark):** Because the Gα·GTP state activates downstream effectors (adenylyl cyclase, PLC, Rho GEFs) that drive proliferation, survival, invasion, and angiogenesis, GPCRs act as upstream on/off gatekeepers of oncogenic signaling, and their dysregulation directly contributes to multiple **hallmarks of cancer**.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`
**Differentiate between Gαs- and Gαq-coupled signaling pathways with one cancer-relevant example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Gαs pathway (1 mark):** Activates **adenylyl cyclase** → ↑ **cAMP** → activates **PKA** → phosphorylates **CREB**. Example: **Gαs gsp oncogene** in pituitary adenomas causes constitutive cAMP elevation and growth hormone hypersecretion.
- **Gαq pathway (1 mark):** Activates **PLCβ** → generates **IP₃ + DAG** → Ca²⁺ release and **PKC** activation → **MAPK** cascade. Example: **Gαq Q209L mutation** in uveal melanoma drives constitutive PLCβ activation and proliferation.
- **Key contrast (1 mark):** Gαs works through a **cyclic nucleotide (cAMP) second messenger**, whereas Gαq works through a **lipid/calcium (DAG/IP₃/Ca²⁺) second messenger system**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 2]`

**(a)** Describe the **molecular architecture of a heterotrimeric G-protein** and explain the **GTPase switch cycle** that converts an extracellular ligand-binding event into an intracellular enzymatic response. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the role of **Gα12/13 signaling in cancer cell invasion and metastasis**, citing specific GPCRs and downstream effectors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Heterotrimeric G-Protein Architecture & Switch Cycle (7 marks)

**1. Subunit composition (2 marks):**
The heterotrimeric G-protein consists of:
- **Gα subunit (~39–46 kDa):** Contains the nucleotide-binding pocket (Switch I, Switch II, P-loop). Has intrinsic **GTPase** activity.
- **Gβ subunit (~36 kDa):** Forms an obligate, non-dissociable dimer with Gγ via a coiled-coil interface; adopts a **7-bladed β-propeller** structure.
- **Gγ subunit (~7–9 kDa):** Prenylated at its C-terminal CAAX motif, anchoring the Gβγ dimer to the inner leaflet of the plasma membrane.

**2. Resting state (1 mark):**
In the basal state, **Gα·GDP** is bound to **Gβγ**, and the entire heterotrimer is docked at the **ICL2 and ICL3** loops of an unliganded GPCR.

**3. Activation step (2 marks):**
- Ligand binds the extracellular face of the GPCR.
- The receptor undergoes an outward movement of **TM6**, creating a cytoplasmic cleft.
- The GPCR now functions as a **Guanine nucleotide Exchange Factor (GEF)** for Gα.
- **GDP dissociates** and **GTP binds** Gα (the cell maintains [GTP] >> [GDP] intracellularly).
- **Gα·GTP** and **Gβγ** dissociate; both can now engage effectors.

**4. Deactivation step (1 mark):**
- The intrinsic **GTPase** activity of Gα hydrolyzes GTP → **GDP + Pi**.
- This reaction is **accelerated 100–1000-fold by RGS proteins** (Regulators of G-protein Signaling).
- Gα·GDP reassociates with Gβγ, returning to the resting state.

**5. Signal termination by GRK/β-arrestin (1 mark):**
Persistent active GPCR is phosphorylated by **GPCR Kinases (GRKs)** on its C-terminal serines/threonines, recruiting **β-arrestin**, which sterically blocks further G-protein coupling and triggers receptor **internalization via clathrin-coated pits** — the desensitization phase.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Gα12/13 in Cancer Invasion and Metastasis (7 marks)

**1. Gα12/13 family identity (1 mark):**
Gα12 and Gα13 are the two members of the Gα12 subfamily. They share ~67% sequence identity and signal predominantly to **Rho-specific guanine nucleotide exchange factors (RhoGEFs)**.

**2. Major GPCRs coupled to Gα12/13 (2 marks):**
- **LPA1, LPA2, LPA3** (lysophosphatidic acid receptors) — overexpressed in ovarian, breast, and colon cancers.
- **PAR1 (Protease-Activated Receptor 1)** — drives invasion in pancreatic and breast cancer.
- **S1P2, S1P3** (sphingosine-1-phosphate receptors) — implicated in lymphangiogenesis.
- **CXCR4** (in specific Gα13-biased contexts) — metastatic homing.

**3. Downstream effectors (2 marks):**
- **p115-RhoGEF, PDZ-RhoGEF, LARG:** Direct Gα12/13 effectors; activate **RhoA → Rho-associated kinase (ROCK)** → actomyosin contractility, stress fiber formation, focal adhesion assembly.
- **YAP/TAZ transcription co-activators:** Gα12/13-Rho signals **inhibit LATS1/2 kinases**, allowing YAP/TAZ nuclear entry → proliferation, stemness, EMT.
- **STAT3, NF-κB cross-talk:** Indirect activation of inflammatory transcriptional programs.

**4. Cancer phenotypes driven (1 mark):**
- **Epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT)** via SNAIL, SLUG upregulation.
- **Invasion and intravasation** through basement-membrane protease (MMP) secretion.
- **Metastatic colonization** of bone and lung (LPA, PAR1).
- **Angiogenesis** via VEGF induction in endothelial cells.

**5. Therapeutic implications (1 mark):**
- **FAK inhibitors** (defactinib) and **ROCK inhibitors** (fasudil) are in cancer clinical trials.
- **PAR1 antagonist vorapaxar** approved for thrombosis; oncology trials ongoing.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2023, Module 2]`

**(a)** Explain how **β-arrestin-mediated signaling** contributes to GPCR-driven oncogenesis, even after classical G-protein signaling has been terminated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare and contrast the **Gαs-cAMP-PKA axis** with the **Gαq-PLC-IP₃/DAG axis**, and explain how aberrant activation of each contributes to distinct cancer hallmarks. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — β-Arrestin Signaling in Oncogenesis (7 marks)

**1. Canonical role of β-arrestin (1 mark):**
β-arrestin 1 and β-arrestin 2 are **cytoplasmic scaffold proteins** classically known for **desensitizing** GPCR signaling by sterically blocking G-protein coupling after GRK-mediated phosphorylation.

**2. From desensitizer to signaler (2 marks):**
- β-arrestin-bound, internalized GPCRs continue to signal from **endosomes** as **"signalosomes"**.
- β-arrestin scaffolds multiple kinase cascades:
  - **Raf → MEK → ERK1/2** (sustained cytoplasmic ERK activation, distinct from transient nuclear ERK from G-protein signaling).
  - **PI3K → AKT** (survival signaling).
  - **ASK1 → MKK4/JNK** (stress/inflammatory signaling).
  - **CaMKII, β-catenin** pathways.

**3. Cancer-specific consequences (2 marks):**
- **Sustained, endosomal ERK** drives nuclear transcription of MYC, cyclin D1 → proliferation.
- **β-arrestin 1** acts as a **nuclear scaffold for NF-κB p65**, promoting expression of anti-apoptotic BCL-2, XIAP.
- **β-arrestin 1** scaffolds **E2F1 → microRNA let-7a repression**, releasing HMGA2 and promoting **breast cancer stemness**.
- **β-catenin signaling** is amplified by β-arrestin in colorectal cancer.

**4. Biased agonism and therapeutic exploitation (1 mark):**
- **"Biased agonists"** preferentially trigger β-arrestin over G-protein signaling (or vice versa).
- This reveals that the "G-protein-independent" arm of GPCR signaling is **pro-tumorigenic** in many contexts.
- Drugs are being designed as **"GRK/β-arrestin-biased"** to either avoid oncogenic β-arrestin signaling or, conversely, harness it therapeutically.

**5. Summary statement (1 mark):**
GPCRs therefore drive oncogenesis through **two parallel waves** — the **classical G-protein wave** (rapid, transient) and the **β-arrestin wave** (sustained, endosome-based) — and both must be considered when designing anti-GPCR cancer therapeutics.

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Comparative Analysis of Gαs vs. Gαq Axes (7 marks)

**1. Gαs-cAMP-PKA axis (3 marks):**
- **Trigger:** Gαs binds and activates **adenylyl cyclase (AC)** (isoforms AC1–AC9).
- **Second messenger:** **cAMP** rises from basal ~0.1 µM to >10 µM.
- **Effector kinase:** **PKA** (cAMP-dependent protein kinase A) — releases catalytic subunits that phosphorylate **CREB (S133)**.
- **Cancer outcome:** CREB-driven transcription of **cyclins, BCL-2, EGR-1, VEGF** → proliferation, survival, angiogenesis.
- **Tumor example:** **McCune-Albright syndrome / pituitary adenoma** with gsp oncogene → constitutive cAMP elevation.

**2. Gαq-PLC-IP₃/DAG axis (2 marks):**
- **Trigger:** Gαq binds and activates **Phospholipase C-β (PLCβ)**.
- **Second messengers:** Cleaves **PIP₂ → IP₃ + DAG**.
- **Effectors:** IP₃ → Ca²⁺ release from ER → calmodulin/calcineurin; DAG → **PKC** activation.
- **Cancer outcome:** PKC → **Raf-MEK-ERK**, NF-κB activation; Ca²⁺ → transcription via NFAT.
- **Tumor example:** **Uveal melanoma** with Gαq Q209L mutation.

**3. Comparative contrast (2 marks):**

| Feature | Gαs-cAMP-PKA | Gαq-PLC-IP₃/DAG |
|---|---|---|
| **Second messenger** | cAMP (cyclic nucleotide) | IP₃ + DAG + Ca²⁺ (lipid + ion) |
| **Primary effector** | PKA | PKC + calmodulin |
| **Major target** | CREB transcription factor | MAPK, NF-κB, NFAT |
| **Hallmark driven** | Survival, angiogenesis, metabolism | Proliferation, inflammation, invasion |
| **Mutation examples** | gsp (R201C) in pituitary tumors | Gαq Q209L in uveal melanoma |

**4. Conclusion (0 marks — wrap-up):**
Both axes represent **convergent mitogenic inputs** but use **chemically distinct second messengers**; their selective activation in different tumors explains why **Gαs-driven tumors** respond to PKA/CREB inhibitors while **Gαq-driven tumors** respond to MEK or PKC inhibitors — guiding precision oncology.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in GPCR Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Gαs vs. Gαi:** Many students write "Gαs inhibits cAMP" — this is **incorrect**; Gαs **stimulates** AC, Gαi **inhibits** it. Marks are deducted for direction errors.
> 2. **Omitting the Gβγ subunit:** The trimer has **three** subunits, not two. Failing to mention Gβγ as an independent signaling entity (which activates PLCβ, PI3K, K⁺ channels) loses marks.
> 3. **Mixing up Gq and G12/13:** Gq activates **PLCβ**; G12/13 activates **RhoGEFs**. Confusing these in metastasis-related questions is a frequent error.
> 4. **Forgetting RGS proteins:** Examiners expect mention of **RGS-mediated GTPase acceleration** in any GPCR cycle question. Omission = 1 mark lost.
> 5. **No specific cancer example:** Generic answers like "GPCRs cause cancer" receive 0 of 1 for cancer relevance — always name a tumor type, a receptor, and a downstream outcome.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this on the day of the exam):**

- **GPCR = 7-transmembrane receptor = largest receptor superfamily (~800 in humans).**
- **Heterotrimeric G-protein:** Gα (switch) + Gβ (scaffold) + Gγ (membrane anchor).
- **Switch cycle:** GDP-bound OFF → agonist-bound GPCR is GEF → GTP-bound ON → GTPase (accelerated by RGS) → back to OFF.
- **Four Gα subfamilies to memorize:**
  - **Gαs** → ↑ adenylyl cyclase → ↑ cAMP → PKA → CREB.
  - **Gαi** → ↓ adenylyl cyclase → ↓ cAMP; also releases Gβγ → PI3K/AKT.
  - **Gαq/11** → ↑ PLCβ → IP₃ + DAG → Ca²⁺ + PKC → MAPK, NF-κB.
  - **Gα12/13** → ↑ RhoGEF → RhoA → ROCK → cytoskeleton, YAP/TAZ → invasion.
- **Oncogenic GPCRs to memorize:** CXCR4 (breast), PAR1 (pancreas), LPA1/2 (ovarian), EP2 (colon), S1PR1 (lymphoma), GPR30/GPER (breast), Gαs gsp (pituitary), Gαq Q209L (uveal melanoma).
- **β-arrestin is NOT only a desensitizer** — it is a **scaffold for sustained, endosomal MAPK, PI3K/AKT, and NF-κB signaling** that drives cancer stemness and survival.
- **GRK phosphorylation → β-arrestin recruitment → clathrin-mediated endocytosis = desensitization.**
- **RGS proteins** are GTPase-activating proteins (GAPs) that accelerate signal termination; frequently **lost or downregulated in cancer**.
- **GPCRs are targeted by ~34% of FDA-approved drugs**, including oncology-relevant CXCR4 antagonists, PAR1 inhibitors, and S1PR modulators.
- **Biased agonism** (G-protein-biased vs. β-arrestin-biased ligands) is an emerging concept in precision cancer pharmacology.
- **Numerical must-knows:** EC50, pEC50, Langmuir binding, intrinsic GTPase kcat ≈ 1–5 min⁻¹, RGS accelerates by 100–1000-fold, signal amplification can reach 10⁴ per receptor per second.
- **For diagram questions:** Always draw the receptor in the membrane, label 7 TMs, mark the G-protein as a trimer outside the receptor, show GDP→GTP exchange, and indicate the downstream effector.

> **Final Mantra:** *GPCRs are binary switches — OFF when Gα·GDP is locked, ON when Gα·GTP dissociates. In cancer, these switches are jammed in the ON position by mutations, overexpression, autocrine ligand loops, or loss of RGS, driving every hallmark of malignancy.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: G Protein
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT521 - CANCER BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** G Protein

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Heterotrimeric G Proteins** (guanine nucleotide-binding proteins) are a family of peripheral membrane-associated molecular switches that transduce extracellular signals from **G Protein-Coupled Receptors (GPCRs)** — the largest family of cell-surface receptors — to intracellular effector enzymes and ion channels. In the context of cancer biology, G proteins are **critical oncoproteins** because gain-of-function mutations in their genes (*GNAS, GNAQ, GNA11, GNA13*) render them constitutively active, driving ligand-independent mitogenic signaling.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** G proteins must be studied as a **central signal-transduction node** linking membrane receptors to nuclear transcription programs that govern proliferation, survival, and metastasis. The GTPase "molecular switch" cycle is the most frequently examined concept.

### Structural Composition
A heterotrimeric G protein consists of three subunits:

| Subunit | Mass (Da) | Function |
|---------|-----------|----------|
| **Gα** | **39–46 kDa** | Binds GDP/GTP; possesses intrinsic **GTPase** activity; determines effector specificity |
| **Gβ** | **36 kDa** | Forms obligate dimer with Gγ; presents Gα to receptor |
| **Gγ** | **7–8 kDa** | Lipid-anchored (prenylated); tethers complex to plasma membrane |

The Gα subunit exists in four major families based on sequence and effector coupling:
- **Gαs** (stimulatory) → activates adenylyl cyclase
- **Gαi/o** (inhibitory) → inhibits adenylyl cyclase
- **Gαq/11** → activates phospholipase C-β (PLCβ)
- **Gα12/13** → activates RhoGEFs

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Mail-Room Switchboard" Analogy**
>
> Think of the cell as a large office building. The **GPCR is the receptionist's desk** at the front door — it receives messages (hormones, growth factors, light) from outside. The **G protein is the internal mailroom switchboard operator** that decides which department (adenylyl cyclase, PLC, ion channel) gets a copy of the message.
>
> - **GDP-bound Gα** = Operator sitting at the switchboard, hands folded, doing nothing (OFF).
> - **Receptor activation** = A new urgent letter arrives; the operator grabs it.
> - **GTP binding (GDP → GTP exchange)** = The operator stands up and routes the message to all relevant departments (ON).
> - **Intrinsic GTPase activity** = After a set time, the operator automatically sits back down (self-termination).
> - **Cancer mutations (e.g., Q209L in Gαq)** = The operator's chair is bolted upright — they NEVER sit down. The departments are flooded with messages 24/7, causing uncontrolled growth.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Intracellular GTP/GDP ratio** ≈ **10 : 1** (this high ratio drives the loading of GTP onto Gα upon activation)
- **GTPase turnover (k_cat) for wild-type Gαs** ≈ **3–4 min⁻¹** (i.e., one hydrolysis event every ~20 seconds)
- **Number of human GPCR genes** ≈ **~800** (largest receptor superfamily; ~50% of all modern drugs target them)
- **Number of Gα subunit genes in humans** = **16** (GNAS, GNAI1–3, GNAO1, GNAT, GNAZ, GNAQ, GNA11, GNA12, GNA13, etc.)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The GTPase Molecular Switch Cycle — Step-by-Step Logic

The G protein cycle proceeds through a strict sequence of conformational states. Each step has a clear biophysical purpose:

### **Step 1 — Resting (Off) State**
- Gα·GDP is tightly associated with the Gβγ dimer (the heterotrimer).
- The **Gα "Switch I" and "Switch II"** regions are buried, occluding the effector-binding surface.
- The complex is docked beneath an inactive GPCR.

### **Step 2 — Receptor Activation & Nucleotide Exchange**
- Agonist binding causes a 10–14 Å outward movement of the GPCR's **transmembrane helix 6 (TM6)**, exposing a cytoplasmic cleft.
- The GPCR functions as a **Guanine nucleotide Exchange Factor (GEF)** for Gα: it stabilizes a nucleotide-free transition state, causing **GDP release**.
- Because cytoplasmic [GTP] ≫ [GDP] (10:1 ratio), **GTP rapidly diffuses in** and binds.

### **Step 3 — Active (On) State — Subunit Dissociation**
- GTP binding causes a massive conformational rearrangement in Switch I/II (~80° rotation of the α3 helix).
- The **affinity of Gα·GTP for Gβγ drops ~1000-fold** — they dissociate.
- **Both** Gα·GTP **and** Gβγ independently regulate downstream effectors (key concept often missed by students!).

### **Step 4 — Effector Activation & Second Messenger Generation**
- Free Gα·GTP binds and modulates effectors:
  - **Gαs** → Adenylyl cyclase → **↑ cAMP** → PKA activation
  - **Gαi** → Adenylyl cyclase inhibition → **↓ cAMP**
  - **Gαq** → PLCβ → PIP₂ → **↑ IP₃ + DAG** → Ca²⁺ release + PKC activation
  - **Gα13** → p115 RhoGEF → **RhoA** → cytoskeletal remodeling (invasion/metastasis)

### **Step 5 — Signal Termination via Intrinsic GTPase**
- Gα possesses an **arginine finger (Arg-201 in Gαs)** that positions the catalytic glutamine (Gln-227) for **GTP hydrolysis**.
- Hydrolysis: **GTP → GDP + Pi**
- The γ-phosphate is lost; Switch regions relax back to the inactive conformation.

### **Step 6 — Re-association and Reset**
- Gα·GDP re-binds Gβγ → the heterotrimer reforms.
- The system is now ready to receive a new signal.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in cancer:** RGS proteins (Regulators of G protein Signaling) act as **GTPase Activating Proteins (GAPs)** — they accelerate Step 5 by ~1000-fold. In tumors, loss of RGS expression or mutations that destroy the catalytic arginine (R201C/H in Gαs) cause constitutive activation by **preventing hydrolysis**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Biological Meaning |
|---------------------|------------|-------|---------------------|
| Intrinsic GTP hydrolysis rate | $v_{hyd} = k_{cat} \cdot [G\alpha \cdot GTP]$ | mol L⁻¹ s⁻¹ | Self-termination rate of Gα signaling |
| Wild-type Gαs k_cat | $k_{cat} \approx 0.05 \text{–} 0.07 \text{ s}^{-1}$ | s⁻¹ | ~1 hydrolysis every 15–20 s |
| Mutant (R201C) k_cat | $k_{cat} \approx < 0.0001 \text{ s}^{-1}$ | s⁻¹ | Signal persists for hours → oncogenic |
| Steady-state active Gα fraction | $f_{active} = \dfrac{k_{exch}}{k_{exch} + k_{cat}}$ | dimensionless | ~1% wild-type, ~99% mutant |
| RGS GAP acceleration | $k_{cat}^{RGS} \approx 10^3 \cdot k_{cat}^{basal}$ | fold-increase | Why RGS loss is pro-tumorigenic |
| cAMP accumulation (Gαs) | $\dfrac{d[cAMP]}{dt} = \dfrac{V_{max,AC}[G\alpha_{s} \cdot GTP]}{K_{m,AC} + [ATP]}$ | μM min⁻¹ | Output response curve |
| Oncogenic hotspot residues | Gln-209 (Gαq), Arg-201 (Gαs) | — | Catalytic residues — never memorize positions wrong in KTU exam! |
| Fractional Gα activation by receptor | $\alpha = \dfrac{[R^*]}{[R] + K_d^{ago}}$ | — | Receptor-occupancy response |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering / Therapeutic Utility

| Application | G Protein Target | Drug / Strategy |
|-------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Uveal melanoma | **Gαq (GNAQ) / Gα11 (GNA11)** | MEK inhibitors, PKC inhibitors |
| Pancreatic / biliary cancer | **Gαs (GNAS) R201H/C** | Adenylyl cyclase inhibitors, somatostatin analogs |
| Breast cancer | **Gα13 → RhoA axis** | RhoA-ROCK inhibitors (Fasudil) |
| Drug development | All GPCRs | **~35% of FDA-approved drugs** target GPCR signaling |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State Fraction of Active G Protein

We model the G protein cycle as a two-state system. Let:
- $G_{off}$ = Gα·GDP (inactive, complexed with Gβγ)
- $G_{on}$ = Gα·GTP (active, dissociated, signaling)
- $k_{exch}$ = first-order rate constant for receptor-catalyzed GDP→GTP exchange (s⁻¹)
- $k_{cat}$ = first-order rate constant for intrinsic GTP hydrolysis (s⁻¹)

The kinetic scheme is:

$$
G_{off} \;\xrightarrow{\;k_{exch}\;}\; G_{on} \;\xrightarrow{\;k_{cat}\;}\; G_{off}
$$

### Derivation Step 1: Write the differential equation for G_on

The rate of accumulation of $G_{on}$ equals the rate of formation minus the rate of disappearance:

$$
\frac{d[G_{on}]}{dt} = k_{exch} [G_{off}] - k_{cat} [G_{on}]
$$

*Explanation:* G_on is created by receptor-driven nucleotide exchange (consuming G_off) and destroyed by GTP hydrolysis (producing G_off).

### Derivation Step 2: Apply mass conservation

The total Gα concentration is constant: $[G_{tot}] = [G_{on}] + [G_{off}]$

Therefore: $[G_{off}] = [G_{tot}] - [G_{on}]$

### Derivation Step 3: Substitute into the differential equation

$$
\frac{d[G_{on}]}{dt} = k_{exch} \left( [G_{tot}] - [G_{on}] \right) - k_{cat} [G_{on}]
$$

*Explanation:* This expansion replaces the unknown [G_off] with a measurable total, leaving only [G_on] as the dependent variable.

### Derivation Step 4: Solve at steady state

At steady state, $\dfrac{d[G_{on}]}{dt} = 0$:

$$
0 = k_{exch} [G_{tot}] - k_{exch} [G_{on}] - k_{cat} [G_{on}]
$$

### Derivation Step 5: Collect terms in [G_on]

$$
k_{exch} [G_{tot}] = [G_{on}] (k_{exch} + k_{cat})
$$

### Derivation Step 6: Solve for [G_on]

$$
[G_{on}]_{ss} = \frac{k_{exch}}{k_{exch} + k_{cat}} \cdot [G_{tot}]
$$

### Derivation Step 7: Define the fractional activation

$$
f_{active} = \frac{[G_{on}]_{ss}}{[G_{tot}]} = \frac{k_{exch}}{k_{exch} + k_{cat}}
$$

*Explanation:* This is the key KTU result. Notice that **f_active depends ONLY on the ratio of the two rate constants** — not on absolute G protein concentration.

### Derivation Step 8: Apply numerical values to wild-type vs. mutant

**Wild-type Gαs:** $k_{cat} = 0.05 \text{ s}^{-1}$, $k_{exch} = 0.001 \text{ s}^{-1}$ (basal, no agonist)

$$
f_{active}^{WT} = \frac{0.001}{0.001 + 0.05} = \frac{0.001}{0.051} \approx 0.0196 \approx \mathbf{2\%}
$$

*Explanation:* Only ~2% of Gαs is active at baseline — the system is biased toward OFF.

**Oncogenic R201C mutant (catalytic arginine destroyed):** $k_{cat} = 0.0001 \text{ s}^{-1}$, same $k_{exch}$

$$
f_{active}^{R201C} = \frac{0.001}{0.001 + 0.0001} = \frac{0.001}{0.0011} \approx 0.909 \approx \mathbf{91\%}
$$

*Explanation:* **~45-fold increase in active Gα!** This persistent signaling drives constitutive cAMP elevation, PKA activation, CREB phosphorylation, and mitogenic gene expression in cancer.

## 3.2 Derivation: Receptor-Catalyzed Exchange Rate (k_exch)

The rate of receptor-catalyzed GDP release follows Michaelis–Menten kinetics with respect to agonist-bound receptor:

$$
k_{exch} = \frac{k_{cat}^{R} \cdot [R^*]}{K_M + [G_{tot}]}
$$

Where $[R^*]$ is the concentration of agonist-occupied active receptor. The agonist-occupancy fraction of receptor is:

$$
[R^*] = [R_{tot}] \cdot \frac{[A]}{K_d + [A]}
$$

Combining these:

$$
k_{exch} = \frac{k_{cat}^{R} \cdot [R_{tot}] \cdot \dfrac{[A]}{K_d + [A]}}{K_M + [G_{tot}]}
$$

*Explanation:* This shows that **k_exch is proportional to agonist concentration [A]** — a direct molecular link between extracellular ligand levels and intracellular G protein activation. In cancer, mutations that mimic agonist-bound receptor (constitutively active GPCRs, e.g., V2R in renal cancers) or that destroy GTPase activity both push $f_{active}$ toward 1.

## 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Parameter | Wild-Type Gαs | R201C Mutant (Cancer) | Fold Change |
|-----------|---------------|------------------------|-------------|
| $k_{cat}$ (s⁻¹) | 0.05 | 0.0001 | 500× ↓ |
| $f_{active}$ (basal) | ~2% | ~91% | ~45× ↑ |
| Signaling duration after single stimulus | ~20 s | ~3–4 hours | ~600× ↑ |
| cAMP (relative) | 1× | ~10–50× | 10–50× |
| Mitogenic phenotype | None | Yes (anchorage-independent growth) | — |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete G Protein Activation-Inactivation Cycle (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Agonist extracellular] --> A2[Agonist binds GPCR]
    A2 --> A3[Inactive GPCR becomes Active GPCR star]
    A3 --> A4[Active GPCR acts as GEF on Galpha GDP]
    A4 --> A5[GDP released from Galpha]
    A5 --> A6[GTP binds Galpha Switch regions rearrange]
    A6 --> A7[Galpha GTP dissociates from G beta gamma dimer]
    A7 --> B1[Galpha GTP activates effector Adenylyl cyclase PLC RhoGEF]
    A7 --> B2[G beta gamma activates PI3K PLC beta ion channels]
    B1 --> C1[Second messengers cAMP IP3 DAG RhoA]
    B2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2[Cellular response proliferation survival migration]
    A6 --> D1[Intrinsic GTPase hydrolysis of GTP to GDP plus Pi]
    D1 --> D2[RGS proteins accelerate GAP activity]
    D2 --> D3[Galpha GDP reassociates with G beta gamma]
    D3 --> A1
    A6 --> E1[Oncogenic mutation Q209L or R201C destroys GTPase]
    E1 --> E2[Persistent active Galpha GTP]
    E2 --> E3[Constitutive signaling uncontrolled proliferation]
    E2 -.blocks.-> D1
```

## 4.2 G Protein Subunit Architecture & Effector Coupling (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1["Receptor Layer"]
        R1[GPCR beta adrenergic]
        R2[GPCR muscarinic M3]
        R3[GPCR LPA S1P receptors]
    end

    subgraph S2["G Protein Layer"]
        G1[Galpha s]
        G2[Galpha q]
        G3[Galpha 12 13]
    end

    subgraph S3["Effector Layer"]
        E1[Adenylyl cyclase]
        E2[Phospholipase C beta]
        E3[p115 RhoGEF]
    end

    subgraph S4["Second Messenger Layer"]
        M1[cAMP up]
        M2[IP3 plus DAG up]
        M3[RhoA GTP up]
    end

    subgraph S5["Cancer Output"]
        O1[PKA CREB proliferation]
        O2[PKC calcium signaling]
        O3[Actomyosin invasion metastasis]
    end

    R1 --> G1 --> E1 --> M1 --> O1
    R2 --> G2 --> E2 --> M2 --> O2
    R3 --> G3 --> E3 --> M3 --> O3
```

## 4.3 Comparative Diagram: Wild-Type vs. Oncogenic G Protein Signaling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph WT["Wild Type Cell"]
        W1[Stimulus brief] --> W2[Transient Galpha GTP]
        W2 --> W3[Short effector activation]
        W3 --> W4[Normal proliferation]
        W2 --> W5[Fast GTP hydrolysis]
        W5 --> W4
    end

    subgraph MT["Mutant Cancer Cell GNAS R201H or GNAQ Q209L"]
        M1[No stimulus required] --> M2[Persistent Galpha GTP]
        M2 --> M3[Continuous effector activation]
        M3 --> M4[Uncontrolled proliferation survival invasion]
        M2 -.impaired.-> M5[Failed GTP hydrolysis]
        M5 -.blocks.-> M4
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Explain briefly the role of G proteins as molecular switches in cell signaling.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
G proteins act as **binary molecular switches** that cycle between an "OFF" state (Gα·GDP, bound to Gβγ) and an "ON" state (Gα·GTP, dissociated from Gβγ). The transition OFF → ON is triggered by GPCRs acting as guanine nucleotide exchange factors (GEFs), causing GDP release and GTP binding. The reverse transition ON → OFF is driven by the **intrinsic GTPase activity** of the Gα subunit, which hydrolyzes GTP to GDP. In cancer, mutations at catalytic residues (Gln-209 in Gαq, Arg-201 in Gαs) impair hydrolysis, locking the switch permanently ON and driving oncogenic signaling. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Gαs and Gαq with respect to their downstream effectors and second messengers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Gαs | Gαq |
|---------|-----|-----|
| Effector | Adenylyl cyclase | Phospholipase C-β (PLCβ) |
| Substrate | ATP | PIP₂ |
| Second messenger produced | cAMP (↑) | IP₃ + DAG (↑) |
| Downstream kinase | PKA | PKC |
| Ca²⁺ mobilization | No | Yes (via IP₃) |
| Cancer association | GNAS mutations (pituitary, pancreas) | GNAQ/GNA11 (uveal melanoma) |

**(3 marks)** — Award 1.5 marks for each correct column description.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Gα subunit families** — students frequently write "Gαs activates PLC" — this is WRONG. Gαs = AC; Gαq = PLC. Mark deduction: **2 marks**.
> 2. **GTPase residues are NOT interchangeable**: Q209 is in Gαq; R201 is in Gαs. Mixing these will cost **1.5 marks**.
> 3. **Both Gα·GTP AND Gβγ signal** — answers that ignore Gβγ effectors (PI3K, GIRK channels) will lose **1 mark**.
> 4. **Always cite specific cancer types** when asked — generic "cancers" without naming tissue types lose marks.
> 5. **Cycle diagrams must show both nucleotide exchange AND hydrolysis arrows** — one-way diagrams are penalized.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, KTU Pattern]

**(a)** Describe in detail the structure of heterotrimeric G proteins and explain the molecular events of the GTPase activation-inactivation cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the oncogenic activation of G proteins, citing specific mutations in **GNAS, GNAQ, and GNA11**, and explain how these mutations affect the GTPase cycle and contribute to cancer progression. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a)** (7 marks)

**1. Structure of heterotrimeric G proteins (3 marks):**
- The G protein is composed of three subunits: **Gα (39–46 kDa), Gβ (36 kDa), and Gγ (7–8 kDa)**.
- The Gα subunit has a **Ras-like GTPase domain** and a helical domain; it contains the **Switch I (residues ~178–195)** and **Switch II (residues ~200–220)** regions whose conformation changes with nucleotide state.
- Gβ is a β-propeller (WD40 repeat) protein that forms an obligate, high-affinity dimer with Gγ.
- Gγ is lipid-modified (prenylated at C-terminal CAAX motif) and anchors the complex to the **inner leaflet of the plasma membrane**.

**2. Activation-inactivation cycle (4 marks):**

- **Step 1 — Resting state:** Gα·GDP + Gβγ form the heterotrimer, docked beneath an inactive GPCR. Switch regions are in the "OFF" conformation, masking the effector-binding surface.
- **Step 2 — Receptor activation:** Agonist binding to GPCR causes a conformational change (TM6 outward tilt), exposing a cytoplasmic cleft that engages the Gα C-terminus. The GPCR functions as a **GEF**, stabilizing a nucleotide-free state and promoting **GDP release**.
- **Step 3 — GTP binding & subunit dissociation:** Due to the high intracellular GTP:GDP ratio (~10:1), **GTP rapidly binds**. Switch I/II refold, reducing affinity for Gβγ ~1000-fold. The complex dissociates into Gα·GTP and Gβγ, both competent to regulate effectors.
- **Step 4 — Effector activation:** Gα·GTP binds effectors (e.g., adenylyl cyclase for Gαs, PLCβ for Gαq, p115-RhoGEF for Gα13) and modulates second messenger production.
- **Step 5 — Signal termination:** The intrinsic **GTPase** of Gα hydrolyzes GTP → GDP + Pi. The catalytic Arg-201 (Gαs) and Gln-227 (Gαs) residues coordinate the attacking water.
- **Step 6 — Reset:** Gα·GDP re-associates with Gβγ; the heterotrimer is reformed and ready for another cycle. **RGS proteins** accelerate Step 5 by ~1000-fold.

**(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b)** (7 marks)

**1. Common oncogenic mechanism (2 marks):**
Oncogenic G protein mutations are almost always **missense mutations at catalytic residues** that destroy GTPase activity. This results in:
- Failure to hydrolyze GTP → persistent Gα·GTP
- Constitutive effector activation in the **absence of ligand/receptor stimulus**
- Continuous mitogenic second messenger generation

**2. Specific cancer mutations (5 marks):**

**(i) GNAS (Gαs) — R201H and R201C:**
- **R201** is the catalytic "arginine finger" that stabilizes the transition state during GTP hydrolysis.
- **R201H** and **R201C** mutations reduce k_cat by ~500-fold, locking Gαs in the active state.
- Found in: **McCune-Albright syndrome**, **growth hormone-secreting pituitary adenomas (40–60%)**, **intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms (IPMN) of the pancreas**, **colonic adenomas**.
- Mechanism: Persistent adenylyl cyclase activation → ↑ cAMP → PKA activation → CREB phosphorylation → cyclin D1 upregulation → proliferation.

**(ii) GNAQ and GNA11 (Gαq) — Q209L and Q209P:**
- **Gln-209** is the catalytic glutamine that positions the attacking water for hydrolysis.
- **Q209L** is the most common uveal melanoma mutation; found in **~85% of uveal melanomas** and **~70% of blue nevi**.
- Mutation reduces GTPase activity and renders Gαq constitutively active.
- Mechanism: Persistent PLCβ activation → ↑ IP₃ (Ca²⁺ release) + DAG (PKC activation) → MAPK/ERK pathway activation → melanocyte proliferation and survival.
- GNAQ and GNA11 mutations are **mutually exclusive** — only one allele is mutated per tumor.

**(iii) GNA13 (Gα13):**
- Activating mutations in GNA13 found in **Burkitt lymphoma** and **diffuse large B-cell lymphoma**.
- Drives constitutive RhoA activation → cytoskeletal remodeling, invasion, and cell migration.

**(7 marks awarded)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — KTU Pattern]

**(a)** Draw and explain the GTPase molecular switch cycle of heterotrimeric G proteins. Compare the structural and functional differences between small GTPases (like RAS) and heterotrimeric G proteins. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the major families of Gα subunits (Gs, Gi, Gq, G12/13) with their specific effectors, second messengers, and relevance to cancer metastasis. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(a)** (7 marks)

**1. GTPase molecular switch cycle (4 marks):**
The cycle (already detailed in Question A) consists of:
- **OFF state:** Gα·GDP + Gβγ heterotrimer
- **Receptor-catalyzed exchange:** GPCR acts as GEF → GDP release
- **ON state:** GTP binds → subunit dissociation → effector activation
- **Termination:** Intrinsic GTPase hydrolyzes GTP → GDP
- **Reset:** Re-association of Gα·GDP with Gβγ
- **Modulation by RGS proteins** (GAPs) accelerates termination ~1000-fold

**2. Comparison: Small GTPases vs. Heterotrimeric G proteins (3 marks):**

| Feature | Small GTPases (RAS, Rho, Rab) | Heterotrimeric G proteins |
|---------|--------------------------------|---------------------------|
| **Size** | 20–25 kDa (monomeric) | Gα 39–46 kDa + Gβ 36 kDa + Gγ 7–8 kDa |
| **Quaternary structure** | Monomer | αβγ heterotrimer |
| **Membrane anchor** | C-terminal lipid modifications (farnesyl/geranylgeranyl) on Gα subunit | Gγ prenylation anchors complex |
| **Activator** | Specific GEFs (e.g., SOS for RAS) | GPCRs act as GEFs |
| **Intrinsic GTPase rate** | Very slow (RAS k_cat ≈ 0.001 s⁻¹) | Moderate (Gαs k_cat ≈ 0.05 s⁻¹) |
| **Cancer relevance** | RAS mutated in ~30% of all human cancers | GNAS, GNAQ, GNA11, GNA13 in specific tumors |
| **GAPs** | p120GAP, neurofibromin (NF1) | RGS protein family |
| **Key residues** | Gly-12, Gly-13, Gln-61 (RAS) | Arg-201, Gln-227 (Gαs); Gln-209 (Gαq) |

**(7 marks awarded)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part B(b)** (7 marks)

**1. Gαs family (1.5 marks):**
- **Effectors:** Adenylyl cyclase (all 9 isoforms) ↑
- **Second messenger:** cAMP ↑
- **Downstream:** PKA → CREB transcription
- **Cancer role:** GNAS R201H/C mutations in pituitary adenomas, pancreatic IPMN, McCune-Albright syndrome

**2. Gαi/o family (1.5 marks):**
- **Effectors:** Adenylyl cyclase ↓, GIRK potassium channels ↑, PI3K (via Gβγ)
- **Second messenger:** cAMP ↓; PIP₃ ↑
- **Downstream:** Akt activation (survival)
- **Cancer role:** GNAI2 overexpression in ovarian cancer; pertussis toxin (which ADP-ribosylates Gαi) is studied for its anti-tumor immunomodulatory effects

**3. Gαq/11 family (2 marks):**
- **Effectors:** PLCβ (all 4 isoforms) ↑
- **Second messenger:** IP₃ + DAG ↑ (and Ca²⁺)
- **Downstream:** PKC → MAPK/ERK cascade
- **Cancer role:** **GNAQ Q209L** in uveal melanoma (~85%), blue nevi, leptomeningeal melanocytosis; **GNA11 Q209L** in uveal melanoma and mucosal melanoma. Drives YAP/TAZ and MAPK activation.

**4. Gα12/13 family (2 marks):**
- **Effectors:** p115 RhoGEF, PDZ-RhoGEF
- **Second messenger:** RhoA-GTP ↑
- **Downstream:** ROCK → actomyosin contractility, FAK, SRF transcription
- **Cancer role:** **Metastasis and invasion** — Gα13 is critical for prostate cancer extravasation, breast cancer invasion; GNA13 mutations in Burkitt lymphoma. Gα12/13 signaling converts epithelial cells to a mesenchymal, migratory phenotype (EMT).

**(7 marks awarded)**

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — G Protein (Module 2)**

- [x] **G proteins are HETEROTRIMERIC**: Gα + Gβγ — never call them "small GTPases"; RAS is a small GTPase.
- [x] **The switch is a binary cycle**: GDP-bound (OFF) ↔ GTP-bound (ON).
- [x] **GPCRs are GEFs** for Gα — they catalyze GDP release, not GTP hydrolysis.
- [x] **GTPase hydrolysis is intrinsic to Gα** — and this is the target of cancer mutations.
- [x] **Both Gα·GTP and Gβγ signal** — Gβγ activates PI3K, PLCβ, GIRK channels.
- [x] **Four Gα families**: Gs (AC↑), Gi (AC↓), Gq (PLCβ↑), G12/13 (RhoA↑).
- [x] **Key catalytic residues**:
  - **Gαs**: Arg-201 (arginine finger), Gln-227 (catalytic glutamine) — **R201H/C** is oncogenic
  - **Gαq**: Gln-209 — **Q209L** is oncogenic
- [x] **Cancer associations to memorize**:
  - **GNAS** → pituitary adenoma, pancreatic IPMN, McCune-Albright
  - **GNAQ/GNA11** → uveal melanoma (~85%), blue nevus
  - **GNA13** → Burkitt lymphoma, metastasis
- [x] **GTP:GDP ratio in cells ≈ 10:1** — this thermodynamic asymmetry drives the "ON" state.
- [x] **RGS proteins are GAPs** — they accelerate GTP hydrolysis; loss of RGS = pro-tumorigenic.
- [x] **cAMP is the second messenger for Gαs**; **IP₃ + DAG for Gαq**; **RhoA-GTP for Gα12/13**.
- [x] **Cholera toxin** permanently activates Gαs (ADP-ribosylation at Arg-201 — the same residue mutated in cancer!).
- [x] **Pertussis toxin** permanently inactivates Gαi (ADP-ribosylation at Cys-351).
- [x] **GTPase equation to remember**: $f_{active} = \dfrac{k_{exch}}{k_{exch} + k_{cat}}$ — in cancer, $k_{cat}$ drops ~500-fold.
- [x] **GPCRs are the largest drug target family** — ~35% of FDA-approved drugs act on GPCR-G protein signaling.
- [x] **Always draw both arrows** (activation + hydrolysis) in cycle diagrams during exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ras and Rho Family Signaling - Types of Cell Signaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT521 - CANCER BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ras and Rho Family Signaling - Types of Cell Signaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Ras and Rho Family Signaling – Types of Cell Signaling

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Cell Signaling?

**Cell signaling** (also called **cellular signal transduction**) is the biochemical process by which cells communicate with their environment, receive external cues, and translate them into specific intracellular responses such as gene expression, cytoskeletal reorganization, metabolism, proliferation, differentiation, or apoptosis. In cancer biology, dysregulated cell signaling is one of the **ten hallmarks of cancer** (Hanahan & Weinberg, 2011 framework).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** Cell signaling is the molecular mechanism through which a physical or chemical stimulus is sensed by a cell-surface or intracellular receptor and converted into a defined intracellular response via a cascade of second messengers, adaptor proteins, and effector enzymes.

### 1.2 Types of Cell Signaling — The Five Canonical Modes

| Type | Mode of Communication | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|
| **Autocrine** | Cell signals itself | Same cell produces and responds |
| **Paracrine** | Local signaling | Signal acts on neighboring cells |
| **Endocrine** | Long-distance signaling | Hormones via bloodstream |
| **Juxtacrine** | Direct contact | Requires membrane-membrane contact |
| **Intracrine** | Self-contained | Acts inside the same cell (no secretion) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cancer Link:** Tumor cells often hijack autocrine loops (e.g., **EGF-EGFR**, **PDGF-PDGFR**) and paracrine signaling with stromal cells (TGF-β, IL-6) to sustain uncontrolled proliferation.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Postal System of the Cell"

Imagine a city (the body) where:
- **Letters (signaling molecules / ligands)** are written in the **post office (signaling cell)**.
- They are delivered either **locally (paracrine)**, **door-to-door (juxtacrine)**, **internationally via air-mail (endocrine)**, or **delivered to your own house (autocrine)**.
- The **letterbox (receptor)** reads the letter, and the **postmaster (intracellular cascade: Ras → Raf → MEK → ERK)** activates the appropriate response (e.g., "grow," "divide," "migrate").

A **mutation in the postmaster** (mutant Ras) means the cell keeps growing **even without letters** — this is **oncogenic signaling**.

### 1.4 The Ras Superfamily of Small GTPases

The **Ras superfamily** comprises ~150 small (~20–25 kDa) guanine nucleotide-binding GTPases that act as **molecular binary switches** cycling between:

- **Active (ON) state:** Bound to **GTP**
- **Inactive (OFF) state:** Bound to **GDP**

| Subfamily | Members | Primary Function | Cancer Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Ras** | H-Ras, K-Ras, N-Ras | Proliferation, differentiation | **~30% of all human tumors** |
| **Rho** | RhoA, Rac1, Cdc42 | Cytoskeleton, migration, adhesion | Metastasis, invasion |
| **Rab** | Rab5, Rab11 | Vesicular trafficking | Less directly oncogenic |
| **Arf** | Arf1, Arf6 | Vesicle budding | Less directly oncogenic |
| **Ran** | Ran | Nuclear transport | Mitotic regulation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The **Ras family** and **Rho family** are the two subfamilies most directly implicated in **oncogenesis and metastasis**. They are also the only two that the KTU Module-2 syllabus explicitly requires.

### 1.5 The Rho Family — Distinct from Ras

The **Rho (Ras homologous) family** consists of ~20 members, but the three best-studied in cancer are:

- **RhoA** → controls **stress fibers** and **focal adhesions**
- **Rac1** → controls **lamellipodia** (membrane ruffling)
- **Cdc42** → controls **filopodia** (cellular protrusions)

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Rho functions — "Rho-Rac-Cdc42 = Stress-Lamelli-Filo":**
> - **R**hoA = **R**igid stress fibers
> - **R**ac1 = **R**uffling lamellipodia
> - **C**dc42 = **C**ellular filopodia (finger-like projections)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The GTPase Molecular Switch — A Biochemical Logic

The Ras/Rho proteins function as **binary molecular switches** governed by a precise cycle:

### Step 1: Loading (Activation)
- **GEF (Guanine nucleotide Exchange Factor)** catalyzes the release of bound **GDP** and uptake of cellular **GTP**.
- This is the **activation step** (OFF → ON).

### Step 2: Effector Binding (Signal Propagation)
- The **GTP-bound form** has a conformational change in two flexible loops called **Switch I (residues 30–38)** and **Switch II (residues 60–72)**.
- These "switches" expose a binding surface for **effector proteins** such as **Raf, PI3K, RalGDS** (for Ras) or **ROCK, PAK, WASP** (for Rho).

### Step 3: Hydrolysis (Deactivation)
- **GAP (GTPase-Activating Protein)** accelerates the slow intrinsic GTPase activity (which is normally very weak: $k_{cat} \approx 0.02 \text{ min}^{-1}$ for Ras) by a factor of **~10⁵**.
- Reaction:
$$\text{Ras-GTP} \xrightarrow{\text{GAP}} \text{Ras-GDP} + P_i$$

### Step 4: Re-loading
- **GDI (Guanine nucleotide Dissociation Inhibitor)** sequesters the GDP-bound form in the cytosol, preventing reactivation.

## 2.2 The Key Functional Properties Table

| Property | Ras Family | Rho Family |
|---|---|---|
| **Molecular weight** | **~21 kDa** | **~21–25 kDa** |
| **Lipid modification** | Farnesylation (CAAX box) | Geranylgeranylation (CAAX or CC/CAAX) |
| **Membrane localization** | Plasma membrane (inner leaflet) | Plasma membrane, endosomes, cytosol |
| **Primary activator** | Growth factor RTKs (EGFR, FGFR) | Integrins, GPCRs, RTKs |
| **Major effectors** | Raf, PI3K, RalGDS | ROCK, PAK, mDia, WASP |
| **Downstream pathway** | MAPK, PI3K/AKT | Cytoskeletal remodeling, SRF, JNK |
| **Oncogenic frequency** | **~30% of cancers** | **~10–15% of cancers** (overexpression/mutation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Key Point:** Both Ras and Rho are **G-proteins**, but unlike the **heterotrimeric G-proteins (Gαβγ)**, they are **monomeric (small) G-proteins**. The 21-kDa molecular mass is a **board-exam favorite** numerical fact.

## 2.3 Canonical Downstream Pathways

### 2.3.1 Ras → MAPK/ERK Pathway (Proliferation Pathway)

$$\text{Growth Factor} \rightarrow \text{RTK} \rightarrow \text{Grb2/SOS (GEF)} \rightarrow \text{Ras-GDP} \rightarrow \text{Ras-GTP}$$
$$\rightarrow \text{Raf (MAPKKK)} \rightarrow \text{MEK (MAPKK)} \rightarrow \text{ERK (MAPK)} \rightarrow \text{Nucleus} \rightarrow \text{c-Fos, c-Myc, Cyclin D1}$$

### 2.3.2 Ras → PI3K/AKT Pathway (Survival Pathway)

$$\text{Ras-GTP} \rightarrow \text{p110\alpha \text{ of PI3K}} \rightarrow \text{PIP}_2 \rightarrow \text{PIP}_3$$
$$\rightarrow \text{PDK1} \rightarrow \text{AKT (PKB)} \rightarrow \text{Bad, mTOR, GSK-3\beta}$$

### 2.3.3 RhoA → ROCK Pathway (Cytoskeletal & Migration Pathway)

$$\text{Integrin/GPCR} \rightarrow \text{p115RhoGEF} \rightarrow \text{RhoA-GTP} \rightarrow \text{ROCK}$$
$$\rightarrow \text{MLC phosphatase inhibition} \rightarrow \text{Myosin Light Chain (MLC) phosphorylation} \rightarrow \text{Stress fiber formation}$$

### 2.3.4 Rac1/Cdc42 → PAK and WASP Pathways

$$\text{Rac1-GTP} \rightarrow \text{PAK1} \rightarrow \text{LIMK} \rightarrow \text{Cofilin inactivation} \rightarrow \text{Lamellipodium stability}$$
$$\text{Cdc42-GTP} \rightarrow \text{WASP} \rightarrow \text{Arp2/3 complex} \rightarrow \text{Actin branching} \rightarrow \text{Filopodia}$$

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Equation / Concept | Expression / Value | Biological Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **GTPase Switch** | $\text{Ras-GDP} \xrightleftharpoons[\text{GAP}]{\text{GEF}} \text{Ras-GTP}$ | Binary molecular switch |
| **GTP hydrolysis** | $\text{GTP} \rightarrow \text{GDP} + P_i$ | $k_{cat} \approx 0.02 \text{ min}^{-1}$ (Ras alone) |
| **GAP stimulation** | $\text{Rate}_{\text{+GAP}} \approx 10^5 \times \text{Rate}_{\text{-GAP}}$ | GAP amplifies hydrolysis |
| **H-Ras CAAX box** | $\text{Cys-Val-Val-Met}$ | Farnesylation site (C**AAX**) |
| **Oncogenic Ras mutation** | $\text{Gly12} \rightarrow \text{Val}$ or $\text{Gln61} \rightarrow \text{Leu}$ | Disrupts GTP hydrolysis |
| **MAPK cascade tiers** | MAPKKK → MAPKK → MAPK | Three-tier kinase cascade |
| **ERK targets** | c-Fos, c-Myc, Cyclin D1, TCF | Proliferation transcription |
| **AKT target** | mTOR (mammalian Target of Rapamycin) | Growth and survival |
| **ROCK target** | MLC Phosphatase (MYPT1) | Stress fiber formation |
| **Rho/Rac/Cdc42 mnemonic** | Stress / Lamellipodia / Filopodia | Cytoskeletal phenotypes |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Numerical Favorite:** The intrinsic GTP hydrolysis rate of Ras is **~0.02 min⁻¹** (i.e., a half-life of ~30 minutes). This is why **GAP proteins are essential** — without them, signaling would be sluggish. Oncogenic mutations at codons **12, 13, 61** reduce this rate by 10⁴–10⁵ fold, locking Ras in the **active state**.

## 2.5 Real-World Biomedical Utility

| Application | Pathway Targeted | Therapeutic Agent | Cancer Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| **KRAS G12C inhibitor** | Mutant K-Ras | **Sotorasib (AMG 510)** | NSCLC |
| **BRAF inhibitor** | B-Raf (downstream of Ras) | **Vemurafenib** | Melanoma |
| **MEK inhibitor** | MEK (downstream of B-Raf) | **Trametinib** | Melanoma, NSCLC |
| **EGFR monoclonal Ab** | Upstream of Ras | **Cetuximab** | Colorectal, H&N |
| **ROCK inhibitor** | RhoA pathway | **Fasudil** | Under trial for metastasis |
| **Rac1 inhibitor** | Rac1-GEF interaction | **MBQ-167** | Triple-negative breast cancer |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Note:** For decades, **Ras was considered "undruggable"** due to its smooth surface (no deep pockets for small molecules). **Sotorasib (2021 FDA approval)** for **KRAS G12C** was a historic breakthrough — students should know this as a real-world case study.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The GTPase Cycle in Quantitative Form

The Ras GTPase cycle can be modeled as a **two-state kinetic system**. Let:
- $[R_{GDP}]$ = concentration of Ras in inactive (GDP-bound) state
- $[R_{GTP}]$ = concentration of Ras in active (GTP-bound) state

### Step 1 — Activation by GEF

The GEF-mediated exchange follows second-order kinetics:

$$v_{GEF} = k_{GEF} \cdot [GEF] \cdot [R_{GDP}]$$

Where $k_{GEF}$ is the catalytic rate constant of the GEF (e.g., SOS). The reaction replaces GDP with GTP because cellular [GTP] $\approx$ **500 μM** vs. [GDP] $\approx$ **50 μM** (a 10:1 ratio favoring GTP loading after exchange).

### Step 2 — Hydrolysis (Spontaneous, intrinsic)

$$v_{hyd,intrinsic} = k_{cat,intrinsic} \cdot [R_{GTP}]$$

With $k_{cat,intrinsic} \approx 0.02 \text{ min}^{-1}$ for wild-type Ras.

### Step 3 — Hydrolysis (GAP-stimulated)

$$v_{hyd,GAP} = k_{cat,GAP} \cdot [GAP] \cdot [R_{GTP}]$$

The catalytic efficiency enhancement is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Enhancement} &= \frac{k_{cat,GAP}}{k_{cat,intrinsic}} \\
&= \frac{\sim 2000 \text{ min}^{-1}}{0.02 \text{ min}^{-1}} \\
&\approx 10^5
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic:** GAPs such as **p120GAP (for Ras)** or **RhoGAP** provide a critical **arginine finger** residue that stabilizes the transition state of GTP hydrolysis, accelerating the reaction by **five orders of magnitude**.

### Step 4 — Steady-State Fraction of Active Ras

At steady state, the activation rate equals the hydrolysis rate:

$$k_{GEF} \cdot [GEF] \cdot [R_{GDP}] = k_{cat,GAP} \cdot [GAP] \cdot [R_{GTP}]$$

Solving for the active fraction:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{[R_{GTP}]}{[R_{total}]} &= \frac{1}{1 + \frac{k_{cat,GAP} \cdot [GAP]}{k_{GEF} \cdot [GEF]}} \\
&= \frac{1}{1 + \frac{k_{cat,GAP} \cdot [GAP]}{k_{GEF} \cdot [GEF]}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Biological Interpretation:** The fraction of active Ras is determined by the **GEF/GAP ratio**. In oncogenic Ras (e.g., G12V), $k_{cat,GAP} \to 0$, so the fraction approaches **1.0 (100% active)** — Ras is "locked ON."

## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of G12V Mutation on Ras Activity

Wild-type Ras hydrolyzes GTP with $k_{cat} \approx 0.02 \text{ min}^{-1}$. Mutant G12V Ras has $k_{cat} \approx 0.0001 \text{ min}^{-1}$ (200× slower).

The **half-life of the active state** is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{1/2} &= \frac{\ln(2)}{k_{cat}} \\
t_{1/2,WT} &= \frac{0.693}{0.02 \text{ min}^{-1}} \approx 34.7 \text{ min} \\
t_{1/2,G12V} &= \frac{0.693}{0.0001 \text{ min}^{-1}} \approx 6930 \text{ min} \approx 4.8 \text{ days}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Biological Interpretation:** A wild-type Ras signal lasts **~35 minutes**, while a G12V mutant signal persists for **~5 days** — effectively permanent activation. This persistent signaling is the molecular basis of oncogenic transformation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: How Mutations in Ras/Rho Drive Cancer

| Protein | Common Cancer-Associated Mutation | Functional Consequence | Affected Pathway | Tumor Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **K-Ras** | **G12D, G12V, G12C** | Loss of GTP hydrolysis | MAPK + PI3K | Pancreatic (90%), Colorectal (40%), Lung (30%) |
| **H-Ras** | **G12V, Q61L** | Loss of GTP hydrolysis | MAPK | Bladder, Thyroid, Head & Neck |
| **N-Ras** | **Q61K, G12D** | Loss of GTP hydrolysis | MAPK | Melanoma (20%), Hematologic |
| **RhoA** | **G17V** (loss-of-function in some, gain in others) | Altered GTPase cycling | ROCK, SRF | Diffuse gastric cancer, T-cell lymphomas |
| **Rac1** | **P29S** (fast-cycling) | Increased GEF-independent activation | PAK, JNK | Melanoma (~9%), Breast |
| **Cdc42** | **Overexpression** (rare mutations) | Hyperactivation of WASP | Filopodia, polarity | Various carcinomas |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The **"K-Ras > N-Ras > H-Ras"** frequency ordering is: **Pancreatic (K) > Colorectal (K) > Lung (K) > Melanoma (N) > Bladder (H)**. Students often confuse which isoform dominates which cancer.

## 3.4 Cascade Calculation: Signal Amplification in the MAPK Pathway

The Ras-Raf-MEK-ERK cascade is a **three-tier amplifier**.

| Tier | Protein | Approx. Copies Activated per Activated Molecule |
|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 | **Ras-GTP** | 1 (activates 1 Raf) |
| Tier 2 | **Raf** | ~10 (activates 10 MEKs) |
| Tier 3 | **MEK** | ~100 (activates 100 ERKs) |
| Output | **ERK** | ~1,000 downstream substrates |

> **Total amplification:** $\sim 1 \times 10 \times 100 = 1000$ ERK molecules per single Ras-GTP.

This explains how **a few hundred Ras-GTP molecules** (per cell) can robustly drive a transcriptional response in the nucleus.

## 3.5 Logic Steps: How Rho GTPases Drive Metastasis — The "Invasion Cascade"

1. **Step 1 — Detachment:** E-cadherin loss → epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT).
2. **Step 2 — Protrusion:** **Cdc42 → filopodia** + **Rac1 → lamellipodia** extend forward.
3. **Step 3 — Adhesion turnover:** **RhoA → focal adhesions** at the rear provide traction.
4. **Step 4 — Proteolysis:** MMP secretion (via Rac1/PI3K) degrades ECM.
5. **Step 5 — Translocation:** Cell squeezes through basement membrane.
6. **Step 6 — Intravasation:** Enters blood/lymph vessels.
7. **Step 7 — Extravasation:** RhoA-mediated diapedesis out of vessel.
8. **Step 8 — Colonization:** Rac1-mediated survival at the distant site.

> **Key Insight:** **Rac1 and Cdc42 are "front-of-cell" drivers, while RhoA is the "rear-of-cell" driver.** The spatial coordination of these three GTPases is the essence of directed cell migration.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
