# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Differentiate between autocrine and paracrine signaling with one example each.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> | Feature | Autocrine | Paracrine |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Target cell** | Same cell that secreted the signal | Neighboring cells |
> | **Distance** | Self (no diffusion needed) | Very short (local) |
> | **Example in cancer** | EGF–EGFR loop in breast cancer | TGF-β from tumor to stromal fibroblasts |
> | **Example in normal physiology** | Cytokine IL-2 on T-cells | Neurotransmitter at synapse |
>
> **Conclusion:** Autocrine loops in tumors create self-sufficiency in growth signals — a hallmark of cancer.

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
**Name the three members of the Rho family and the cytoskeletal structures each controls.**

> **Model Answer:**
>
> 1. **RhoA** → controls **stress fibers** and **focal adhesions** (cell rear contraction).
> 2. **Rac1** → controls **lamellipodia** (membrane ruffling at the leading edge).
> 3. **Cdc42** → controls **filopodia** (finger-like protrusions for environmental sensing).
>
> **Mnemonic:** **"RhoA = Rigid / Rac1 = Ruffling / Cdc42 = Cellular fingers."**
> These three GTPases act in a coordinated, **spatially segregated** manner to drive directed cell migration — a process critical for **metastasis**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the molecular mechanism of the **Ras GTPase cycle**. Explain how oncogenic mutations (G12V, Q61L) lock Ras in the active state and contribute to tumorigenesis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Illustrate the **Ras → Raf → MEK → ERK (MAPK) cascade** and explain its role in cell proliferation. Add a note on therapeutic inhibitors targeting this pathway.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**The Ras GTPase Cycle (7 marks):**

1. **Ras is a 21-kDa monomeric small GTPase** that acts as a binary molecular switch, cycling between an inactive GDP-bound state and an active GTP-bound state.

2. **Activation by GEF (Guanine nucleotide Exchange Factor):**
   - Growth factors (EGF, PDGF) bind to receptor tyrosine kinases (RTKs).
   - Adaptor **Grb2** recruits the GEF **SOS (Son of Sevenless)** to the membrane.
   - SOS catalyzes the release of GDP and loading of GTP onto Ras.
   - Cellular [GTP]/[GDP] ratio (~10:1) ensures preferential GTP loading.

3. **Effector binding:**
   - GTP-bound Ras undergoes conformational change in the **Switch I (residues 30–38)** and **Switch II (residues 60–72)** regions.
   - This exposes binding sites for downstream effectors: **Raf, PI3K, RalGDS, TIAM1**.

4. **Deactivation by GAP (GTPase-Activating Protein):**
   - GAPs (e.g., **p120GAP** for Ras) provide a catalytic **arginine finger** residue that stabilizes the transition state of GTP hydrolysis.
   - This accelerates intrinsic GTPase activity (k_cat ≈ 0.02 min⁻¹) by **~10⁵ fold**.

5. **GDI sequestration:**
   - In the GDP-bound form, **GDI (Guanine nucleotide Dissociation Inhibitor)** sequesters Ras in the cytosol, preventing reactivation.

**Oncogenic Mutations (G12V, Q61L):**

- **G12V mutation:** Glycine at position 12 is replaced by valine. This bulky residue **sterically hinders** the positioning of the catalytic arginine finger of GAP, **abolishing GAP-stimulated hydrolysis**. The intrinsic hydrolysis rate is also reduced by ~200-fold.
- **Q61L mutation:** Glutamine at position 61 is the catalytic residue that **coordinates the attacking water molecule** in the hydrolysis reaction. Its mutation to leucine disrupts catalysis.
- **Result:** Ras is **locked in the active GTP-bound state**, providing **constitutive (ligand-independent) growth signals** to the nucleus — the molecular basis of **oncogenic transformation**.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**The MAPK Cascade (7 marks):**

$$\text{Ras-GTP} \rightarrow \text{Raf (MAPKKK)} \rightarrow \text{MEK (MAPKK)} \rightarrow \text{ERK (MAPK)} \rightarrow \text{Nucleus}$$

**Step-by-step mechanism:**

1. **Ras-GTP recruits Raf** to the plasma membrane. Raf is a serine/threonine kinase (MAPKKK).
2. **Raf phosphorylates and activates MEK1/2** (MAPKK) on serine residues.
3. **MEK phosphorylates ERK1/2 (MAPK)** on both threonine and tyrosine residues (TEY motif).
4. **ERK1/2 dimerizes and translocates to the nucleus**, where it phosphorylates transcription factors:
   - **ELK-1** → induces **c-Fos** expression.
   - **c-Myc** stabilization.
   - **Cyclin D1** induction → G1 → S phase progression.
5. **Biological output:** Cell proliferation, differentiation, survival.

**Therapeutic Inhibitors:**

| Drug | Target | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Cetuximab / Panitumumab** | EGFR (upstream) | Colorectal, H&N cancer |
| **Vemurafenib / Dabrafenib** | Mutant B-Raf V600E | Melanoma |
| **Trametinib / Cobimetinib** | MEK1/2 | Melanoma, NSCLC |
| **Sotorasib (AMG 510)** | KRAS G12C | NSCLC (2021 FDA approval) |
| **Selumetinib** | MEK1/2 | NF1-related tumors |

**Clinical significance:** ~30% of all human cancers carry Ras mutations; >90% of pancreatic cancers have K-Ras mutations. Targeted therapy against this pathway is a major focus of modern oncology.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper Alternative]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the different **types of cell signaling** with suitable examples and diagrams. Highlight their relevance in cancer biology.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Rho family of GTPases** in detail, covering RhoA, Rac1, and Cdc42. Explain how they regulate cytoskeletal dynamics and contribute to **tumor metastasis**.

#### Part (a) — Model Solution

**Types of Cell Signaling (7 marks):**

1. **Autocrine Signaling:**
   - Cell secretes a signal that acts on **itself**.
   - Example: **Interleukin-6 (IL-6)** in multiple myeloma, **EGF-EGFR** in glioblastoma.
   - Cancer relevance: **Self-sufficiency in growth signals** (Hanahan & Weinberg Hallmark #1).

2. **Paracrine Signaling:**
   - Signal diffuses to act on **adjacent cells**.
   - Example: **TGF-β** from tumor cells to cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs).
   - Cancer relevance: **Tumor-stroma crosstalk**, ECM remodeling, angiogenesis (VEGF).

3. **Endocrine Signaling:**
   - Hormone released into the **bloodstream** for long-distance action.
   - Example: **Estrogen → ER+ breast cancer**, **Testosterone → prostate cancer**.
   - Cancer relevance: Hormone-dependent tumors, hormonal therapy (Tamoxifen, anti-androgens).

4. **Juxtacrine Signaling:**
   - Requires **direct cell-to-cell contact**; ligand remains membrane-bound.
   - Example: **Notch-Delta signaling**, **Membrane-bound mTGF-α** to EGFR on adjacent cell.
   - Cancer relevance: Notch is **oncogenic in T-ALL**, tumor-suppressive in squamous skin cancer.

5. **Intracrine Signaling:**
   - Ligand and receptor are both **intracellular**; signal does not leave the cell.
   - Example: **Androgen receptor (AR)** in prostate cancer, **intracrine FGF**, **PTHrP** in renal cell carcinoma.
   - Cancer relevance: Resistant to extracellular inhibitors, complicates therapy.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution

**Rho Family GTPases in Cytoskeleton and Metastasis (7 marks):**

The Rho family consists of ~20 members; the three most studied in cancer are **RhoA, Rac1, and Cdc42**. All are ~21–25 kDa small GTPases with the same GDP/GTP switch mechanism as Ras.

**1. RhoA — Stress Fibers and Focal Adhesions:**
- Activated by **GPCR ligands (LPA, thrombin)** and **integrins**.
- Activates downstream effector **ROCK (Rho-associated kinase)**.
- ROCK phosphorylates **MYPT1 (myosin phosphatase target subunit 1)**, inhibiting myosin phosphatase.
- This increases **myosin light chain (MLC) phosphorylation**, leading to actomyosin contraction.
- Result: **Stress fiber formation** and **focal adhesion maturation** at the **trailing edge** of migrating cells.
- Cancer relevance: RhoA drives **rear contraction** during invasion; **ROCK inhibitors (Fasudil)** are in trials for metastasis suppression.

**2. Rac1 — Lamellipodia and Membrane Ruffling:**
- Activated by **GEFs TIAM1, VAV, Dock180**.
- Effectors: **PAK1, WAVE, IRSp53**.
- Activates **Arp2/3 complex** (via WAVE) → branched actin networks → **lamellipodia**.
- PAK1 → LIMK → cofilin phosphorylation → **actin stabilization**.
- Result: **Forward protrusion** at the **leading edge** of the cell.
- Cancer relevance: **Rac1 P29S** is a fast-cycling oncogenic mutant in ~9% of melanomas. Drives **membrane ruffling, invasion, and MMP secretion**.

**3. Cdc42 — Filopodia and Cell Polarity:**
- Activated by **GEFs intersectin, FGD1**.
- Effectors: **WASP, N-WASP, mDia2, PAK**.
- WASP → Arp2/3 → **filopodia** (parallel actin bundles).
- Establishes **cell polarity** required for **directed migration**.
- Cancer relevance: Cdc42 regulates **asymmetric division** of stem cells; dysregulation contributes to tumor initiation and metastasis.

**Coordinated Migration Cycle (Metastasis):**
- **Cdc42** = "antenna" → senses direction.
- **Rac1** = "engine" → drives forward.
- **RhoA** = "brake" → contracts the rear.
- This spatial-temporal coordination is essential for **invasion and metastasis**, the most lethal aspect of cancer.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating Ras and Rho** — Many students describe RhoA as "downstream of Ras" or "same as Ras." Ras controls **proliferation**; Rho controls **cytoskeleton/migration**. They are **parallel, not sequential** branches.
> 2. **Forgetting the GEF/GAP concept** — Merely writing "Ras is active when bound to GTP" is incomplete. You must mention **GEF activates, GAP deactivates**.
> 3. **Wrong molecular weight** — Ras and Rho are **~21 kDa small G-proteins**, NOT heterotrimeric G-proteins (which have Gα, Gβ, Gγ subunits). Marks are deducted for this confusion.
> 4. **Forgetting the Switch I/II regions** — For 7-mark questions on Ras structure, the two flexible loops (residues 30–38 and 60–72) are board-favorite details.
> 5. **Confusing mutations** — G12V is the **most common K-Ras mutation**; Q61L is more common in **N-Ras melanoma**. Don't mix these up.
> 6. **No diagrams in 14-mark answers** — KTU examiners expect a **labeled flow diagram** in long answers. Marks are specifically allocated for diagrams (typically 2–3 marks out of 14).
> 7. **Missing the "cancer link"** — Always conclude each answer by linking the molecular concept to a **specific hallmark of cancer or a therapeutic target**. This shows OBE (Outcome-Based Education) alignment and earns full marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- [ ] **Cell signaling** is the process by which cells receive and respond to external cues via **receptors, second messengers, and effector cascades**.
- [ ] **Five types of cell signaling:** **Autocrine** (self), **Paracrine** (local), **Endocrine** (distant), **Juxtacrine** (contact), **Intracrine** (internal).
- [ ] **Ras superfamily** = small monomeric G-proteins (~21 kDa) with 5 subfamilies: **Ras, Rho, Rab, Arf, Ran**.
- [ ] **Ras family** (H-Ras, K-Ras, N-Ras) → **proliferation/differentiation** via **MAPK and PI3K** pathways.
- [ ] **Rho family** (RhoA, Rac1, Cdc42) → **cytoskeleton/migration** via **ROCK, PAK, WASP** pathways.
- [ ] **GTPase cycle:** **GEF** (activates, OFF→ON) → effectors → **GAP** (deactivates, ON→OFF, ~10⁵ fold acceleration).
- [ ] **Switch I (30–38)** and **Switch II (60–72)** loops undergo conformational change upon GTP binding.
- [ ] **Oncogenic mutations** at codons **G12, G13, Q61** lock Ras in the **active GTP-bound state**.
- [ ] **K-Ras** is the most commonly mutated isoform (90% pancreatic, 30% lung, 40% colorectal).
- [ ] **MAPK cascade** = **3-tier kinase amplifier:** **Raf (MAPKKK) → MEK (MAPKK) → ERK (MAPK)** → nucleus → c-Fos, c-Myc, Cyclin D1.
- [ ] **PI3K/AKT pathway** = **survival/growth** branch downstream of Ras → PIP3 → AKT → mTOR.
- [ ] **RhoA → ROCK** → MYPT1 inhibition → MLC phosphorylation → **stress fibers + focal adhesions** (rear).
- [ ] **Rac1 → PAK1 / WAVE → Arp2/3** → branched actin → **lamellipodia** (front).
- [ ] **Cdc42 → WASP → Arp2/3** → parallel actin bundles → **filopodia** (sensing).
- [ ] **Mnemonic:** "**RhoA = Rigid, Rac1 = Ruffling, Cdc42 = Cellular fingers**."
- [ ] **K-Ras G12C inhibitor = Sotorasib (2021 FDA approval)** — historic "undruggable" target.
- [ ] **Vemurafenib** targets **B-Raf V600E** (downstream of Ras).
- [ ] **Cytoplasmic GDI** sequesters GDP-bound Rho/Ras; membrane-bound GEFs activate them.
- [ ] **Lipid modification:** Ras is **farnesylated (CAAX)**; Rho is **geranylgeranylated**.
- [ ] **Metastasis cascade** is coordinated by **Cdc42 (front sensors), Rac1 (front engine), RhoA (rear brake)**.
- [ ] **Cell signaling dysregulation is Hallmark #1 of cancer** (Hanahan & Weinberg, "Self-sufficiency in growth signals").
- [ ] **Rac1 P29S** is a fast-cycling oncogenic mutant in **~9% of melanomas**.
- [ ] **Intrinsic GTPase rate of Ras** ≈ **0.02 min⁻¹** (t½ ≈ 35 min); G12V mutant ≈ **0.0001 min⁻¹** (t½ ≈ 5 days).
- [ ] **Total MAPK amplification:** ~**1,000 ERK molecules per single Ras-GTP** (1 × 10 × 100 cascade).
- [ ] Always end long answers with a **labeled diagram** and a **cancer/therapy link** to score full marks in KTU 2024.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hedgehog Signaling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT521 - CANCER BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Hedgehog Signaling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hedgehog (Hh) Signaling Pathway:** An evolutionarily conserved, morphogen-driven cell–cell communication cascade that regulates embryonic patterning, stem-cell self-renewal, tissue polarity, and cellular differentiation. In mammals, it is a **ligand–receptor–transducer** axis comprising three secreted ligands (SHH, IHH, DHH), the transmembrane receptor **Patched-1 (PTCH1)**, the G-protein-coupled-like transducer **Smoothened (SMO)**, and the GLI family of zinc-finger transcription factors (GLI1, GLI2, GLI3). Aberrant activation of this pathway is a hallmark driver of several human cancers (basal cell carcinoma, medulloblastoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, pancreatic, gastric, and prostate cancers).

**Key Nomenclature (KTU High-Yield Vocabulary):**

| Term | Expansion | Functional Role |
|------|-----------|-----------------|
| **SHH** | Sonic Hedgehog | Most broadly expressed ligand; neural, limb, gut patterning |
| **IHH** | Indian Hedgehog | Cartilage/bone development |
| **DHH** | Desert Hedgehog | Schwann-cell / spermatogenesis |
| **PTCH1** | Patched-1 | 12-TM ligand sink; **negative regulator** |
| **SMO** | Smoothened | 7-TM signal transducer; **proto-oncogene** |
| **GLI1/2/3** | Glioma-associated oncogene homologs | Final transcriptional effectors |
| **SUFU** | Suppressor of Fused | Cytoplasmic GLI sequesterer; tumor suppressor |
| **KIF7** | Kinesin family member 7 | GLI processing/flagellar transport |

> [!NOTE]
> The pathway name originates from the **Drosophila *hedgehog* gene**, first described by Nüsslein-Volhard & Wieschaus (1980, Nobel Prize 1995). Loss-of-function mutations produce a *cuticle covered in denticles* — a phenotype resembling a hedgehog.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English View)

Imagine a **parking-lamp system** in a city:

- **PTCH1** is a *parking attendant* standing on the lamp's switch, **keeping the light OFF** so the lamp cannot glow.
- **SMO** is the **lamp itself** — when released, it can shine.
- **Hedgehog ligand** is a *coin* dropped into the attendant's hand — it makes the attendant *walk away* to free the switch.
- Once free, **SMO turns on**, sending power to the **GLI control room** at city hall.
- **GLI1/2/3** are the **three signalists** in that control room: one *amplifies* (GLI1), one *dual-regulates* (GLI2), and one *primarily silences* (GLI3).
- They flip switches on growth-promotion genes (cell cycle, survival, stemness).

**Cancer Analogy:**
If the *parking attendant* (PTCH1) is **mutated/disabled**, the lamp (SMO) glows **permanently** even without a coin → the city never sleeps → **uncontrolled proliferation = cancer**.

---

## 1.3 Physical / Quantitative Constants Worth Memorising

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC₅₀) of Vismodegib against SMO ≈ 2.8 nM** (in vitro binding).
> - **Autoproteolysis rate of Hh precursor:** cholesterol-dependent, near-complete in the ER.
> - **Canonical GLI-binding motif:** 5′-GACCACCCA-3′ (9-bp consensus).
> - **Basal cell carcinoma (BCC) lifetime risk with PTCH1 germline mutation (Gorlin syndrome): ≈ 90 %**.
> - **Medulloblastoma SHH-subgroup frequency: ≈ 25–30 %** of all medulloblastomas.

---

## 1.4 Section-1 Visual Snapshot

```mermaid
graph LR
    cellA[Off-State Cell\nPTCH1 inhibits SMO\nGLI -> Repressor] --> cellB[On-State Cell\nHh ligand binds PTCH1\nSMO released -> GLI Activator]
    cellB --> cellC[Target Genes\nCCND1, MYC, BCL2\nStemness, Proliferation]
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference

## 2.1 The Two-State Logic of the Pathway (Binary Switch)

The Hedgehog pathway functions as a **bistable molecular switch** with two mutually exclusive states.

### 2.1.1 State-1: OFF (No Ligand)

1. **PTCH1** localises to the primary cilium and catalytically **inhibits SMO** (proposed mechanism: PTCH1 transports/permits efflux of an endogenous SMO antagonist, possibly *cholesterol-derived oxysterols* or related small molecules).
2. **SMO** is excluded from the cilium and held inactive.
3. **GLI2/GLI3** are phosphorylated by **PKA, CK1, GSK3β** in the cytoplasm.
4. Phosphorylated GLI is **proteolysed** (partial) into the **C-terminally truncated repressor form (GLIᵣ)**.
5. **GLIᵣ** translocates to the nucleus and **represses** Hh target genes.
6. **SUFU** sequesters GLI in the cytoplasm, adding a second repression layer.

### 2.1.2 State-2: ON (Ligand Present)

1. **Hh ligand** (autoproteolysed + cholesterol-modified + palmitoylated) binds **PTCH1**.
2. PTCH1 inhibition of SMO is **released**.
3. **SMO** accumulates in the primary cilium and is activated (conformational change, phosphorylation by CK1/GRK2).
4. **SUFU** releases GLI; **KIF7** translocates GLI to the ciliary tip.
5. **GLI2/GLI3** are processed into the **full-length activator form (GLIₐ)**.
6. **GLI₁** is itself a direct transcriptional target → **positive feedback amplification**.
7. Target gene transcription drives proliferation, stemness, EMT, and survival.

---

## 2.2 Key Downstream Targets (Mnemonic: "**GAMES C**")

| Target | Function | Cancer Role |
|--------|----------|-------------|
| **G**LI1 | Pathway amplifier | Oncogene |
| **A**CC (Acetyl-CoA carboxylase) | Lipid biosynthesis | Metabolism |
| **M**YC | Proliferation | Oncogene |
| **E**GFR | Growth signalling | Cross-talk |
| **S**OX2 / NANOG | Pluripotency | Stemness |
| **C**CND1 (Cyclin D1) | G1→S transition | Proliferation |
| **C**XCR4 | Chemokine receptor | Metastasis |
| **B**CL2 | Anti-apoptotic | Survival |
| **F**OXM1 | Mitotic transcription | Proliferation |
| **J**AG2 | Notch ligand | Cross-talk |

---

## 2.3 Cancer-Associated Deregulation Mechanisms (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three molecular routes to oncogenic Hh activation:**

### A) Ligand-Overexpression (Paracrine / Autocrine / Stroma-derived)

- **Ligand-dependent** cancers: pancreatic, prostate, colorectal, gastric, small-cell lung.
- Tumour cells secrete SHH → activate **stromal fibroblasts** → IGF/Wnt feedback supports tumour growth.
- **Therapeutic implication:** Hh ligand neutralising antibodies (e.g., *5E1* in mice; clinical candidates failed in monotherapy).

### B) Mutational (Ligand-Independent) Activation

| Mutation | Frequency in Cancer | Effect |
|----------|---------------------|--------|
| **PTCH1 loss-of-function** | ~90 % sporadic BCC, 30–40 % medulloblastoma (SHH subgroup) | SMO permanently active |
| **SMO gain-of-function** (e.g., W535L, M2) | ~10 % BCC; experimentally tested in mice | SMO resistant to PTCH1 inhibition |
| **SUFU loss-of-function** | ~10 % SHH-medulloblastoma, childhood desmoplastic | GLI unconstrained |
| **GLI1/2 amplification** | Rhabdomyosarcoma, BCC | Final-effector overdrive |

### C) Epigenetic / Cross-Talk Activation

- **TGF-β, KRAS, PI3K, mTOR** can lower the threshold for GLI activation.
- **HDAC inhibitors** indirectly promote GLI de-repression (context-dependent).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Quantitative Toolbox

> [!NOTE]
> Although primarily a qualitative pathway, three quantitative relations recur in KTU numerical / short-answer contexts.

| # | Relation | Formula | Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | Michaelis–Menten (ligand–receptor binding) | $\dfrac{[RL]}{[R]_{T}} = \dfrac{[L]}{K_{d} + [L]}$ | Fractional PTCH1 occupancy |
| 2 | Hill-cooperativity (GLI target switch) | $\theta = \dfrac{[L]^{n}}{K_{d} + [L]^{n}}$ | Ultra-sensitive ON/OFF transition |
| 3 | Fold-change on target gene | $FC \approx 1 + \dfrac{\alpha \cdot \vert SMO_{active} \vert}{\beta + \vert SMO_{active} \vert}$ | Steady-state GLI transcriptional output |
| 4 | Drug inhibition (Cheng–Prusoff) | $K_{i} = \dfrac{IC_{50}}{1 + \dfrac{[S]}{K_{m}}}$ | Convert IC₅₀ to Kᵢ for SMO inhibitors |
| 5 | Population lifetime risk (Gorlin) | $R_{BCC} \approx 0.90$ for PTCH1⁺/⁻ | Dominant inheritance pattern |

> Real-world engineering utility: the same Hill/logistic formalism is used in **synthetic-biology gene circuits**, **pharmacodynamic modelling of SMO inhibitors**, and **dose–response curve fitting** during preclinical oncology trials.

---

## 2.5 Therapy Landscape (KTU Pharma Pointers)

| Drug | Class | Target | FDA Status (as of 2024 knowledge cut-off) |
|------|-------|--------|-------------------------------------------|
| **Vismodegib (GDC-0449)** | Cyclopamine-derived SMO antagonist | SMO (binds TMD pocket) | Approved for advanced BCC |
| **Sonidegib (LDE225)** | SMO antagonist | SMO | Approved for locally advanced BCC |
| **Saridegib (IPI-926)** | SMO antagonist | SMO | Investigational |
| **Glasdegib (PF-04449913)** | SMO antagonist | SMO | Approved for AML (with LDAC) |
| **Taladegib / Patidegib** | Topical SMO antagonists | SMO | Phase II/III |
| **Arsenic trioxide (ATO)** | GLI inhibitor (indirect) | GLI1/2 (blocks ciliary accumulation) | Repurposed, investigational |
| **GANT-61** | Direct GLI1/2 DNA-binding inhibitor | GLI1/2 Zn-finger | Pre-clinical |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanisms of acquired resistance to Vismodegib (frequently examined):**
> 1. SMO secondary mutations (D473H, W281C, Q477E — block drug binding).
> 2. SMO copy-number amplification.
> 3. GLI2 amplification / SUFU loss (pathway activation *downstream* of SMO).
> 4. Activation of compensatory pathways (PI3K–AKT, EGFR).
> 5. ABC transporter efflux (ABCB1/MDR1).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation-1: Fractional Occupancy of PTCH1 by Hh Ligand

**Statement:** Derive the fractional receptor occupancy $\theta$ as a function of free ligand concentration $[L]$, given total receptor $[R]_{T}$ and dissociation constant $K_d$.

**Step 1 — Mass-action equilibrium for ligand–receptor binding.**

$$L + R \;\rightleftharpoons\; RL$$

$$K_d = \dfrac{[L]\cdot [R]}{[RL]}$$

**Step 2 — Conservation of receptor mass.**

$$[R]_T = [R] + [RL]$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the unbound receptor in terms of total.**

$$[R] = [R]_T - [RL]$$

$$K_d = \dfrac{[L]\cdot ([R]_T - [RL])}{[RL]}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for [RL].**

$$K_d \cdot [RL] = [L]\cdot [R]_T - [L]\cdot [RL]$$

$$[RL] \cdot (K_d + [L]) = [L] \cdot [R]_T$$

**Step 5 — Define fractional occupancy $\theta \equiv \dfrac{[RL]}{[R]_T}$.**

$$\theta = \dfrac{[L]}{K_d + [L]}$$

> **Engineering interpretation:** When $[L] = K_d$, the receptor is **half-occupied**; this is the inflection point of the signalling switch and the dose at which downstream GLI-target transcription begins to rise sharply.

---

## 3.2 Derivation-2: Hill-Steepness of the Hh ON/OFF Switch

**Statement:** Show that increasing the **Hill coefficient n** converts a graded ligand input into a near-binary transcriptional output.

**Step 1 — Cooperativity model.** If $n$ ligands must bind to an *oligomeric receptor cluster* (or an effective $n$-site regulatory complex) to produce one active SMO unit:

$$K_d = \dfrac{[L]^n \cdot [R]}{[RL_n]}$$

**Step 2 — Mass conservation.**

$$[R]_T = [R] + [RL_n]$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\theta$.**

$$[R] = \dfrac{K_d \cdot [R]_T}{[L]^n + K_d}$$

**Step 4 — Fractional occupancy becomes the Hill function.**

$$\theta = \dfrac{[RL_n]}{[R]_T} = \dfrac{[L]^n}{K_d + [L]^n}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical demonstration for KTU numerical.**

Let $K_d = 1$ (normalised), vary $[L]$ from $0$ to $3$:

| $[L]$ | $\theta$ (n=1) | $\theta$ (n=2) | $\theta$ (n=4) |
|------|----------------|----------------|----------------|
| 0.1 | 0.091 | 0.0099 | 0.0001 |
| 0.5 | 0.333 | 0.200 | 0.059 |
| 1.0 | 0.500 | 0.500 | 0.500 |
| 1.5 | 0.600 | 0.692 | 0.794 |
| 2.0 | 0.667 | 0.800 | 0.941 |
| 3.0 | 0.750 | 0.900 | 0.988 |

**Step 6 — Interpretation for cancer biology.**

$$\dfrac{d\theta}{d[L]}\bigg|_{[L]=K_d} = \dfrac{n}{4 K_d}$$

> As $n$ increases, the slope at the inflection point rises **n/4-fold** → the cell converts a *continuous* extracellular morphogen gradient into a *discrete* ON/OFF decision. Loss of negative regulators (PTCH1, SUFU) effectively **lowers the apparent $K_d$ and raises the apparent $n$**, which is why a single PTCH1 allele loss in Gorlin syndrome is oncogenic.

---

## 3.3 Derivation-3: Cheng–Prusoff Conversion (SMO Inhibitor Pharmacology)

**Statement:** A research paper reports $\text{IC}_{50} = 14\,\text{nM}$ for Vismodegib at $[S] = 50\,\text{nM}$ SHH-N peptide. The intrinsic $K_m$ of SMO for SHH-N is $30\,\text{nM}$. Compute the inhibition constant $K_i$.

**Step 1 — Cheng–Prusoff equation.**

$$K_i = \dfrac{\text{IC}_{50}}{1 + \dfrac{[S]}{K_m}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$K_i = \dfrac{14\,\text{nM}}{1 + \dfrac{50}{30}}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator.**

$$1 + \dfrac{50}{30} = 1 + 1.667 = 2.667$$

**Step 4 — Final result.**

$$K_i = \dfrac{14}{2.667} \approx 5.25\,\text{nM}$$

> **Conclusion:** Vismodegib binds SMO with intrinsic affinity ≈ 5 nM, which agrees with published biophysical measurements. The Cheng–Prusoff correction is essential for **standardising IC₅₀ values across laboratories** that use different substrate (ligand) concentrations.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Three Hh-Deregulation Subtypes

| Property | Ligand-Overexpression (Type I) | Mutational (Type II) | Cross-Talk/Epigenetic (Type III) |
|----------|-------------------------------|----------------------|----------------------------------|
| Driver | SHH/IHH high expression | PTCH1/SMO/SUFU mutation | PI3K, TGFβ, KRAS, HDAC |
| Dependency on SMO | **High** (blockable) | Partial (SMO-mutant resistant) | Low (SMO-bypass possible) |
| Best responder to Vismodegib | **Yes** | Variable | **No** |
| Examples | Pancreatic, prostate, colorectal | BCC, medulloblastoma, rhabdomyosarcoma | Subset of gastric, melanoma |
| Therapeutic strategy | SMO inhibitor | SMO inhibitor + downstream GLI blocker | Combination (SMO + PI3K/mTOR) |
| KTU examiner focus | Ligand–receptor stoichiometry | PTCH1 tumour-suppressor role | Resistance mechanisms |

---

## 3.5 Decision Algorithm — Hh Pathway Status in a Tumour (KTU Reasoning Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient tumour sample] --> B{SHH or GLI1 mRNA elevated?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Type I: Ligand-driven]
    B -- No --> D{Sanger seq PTCH1/SMO/SUFU}
    D -- PTCH1 LoF --> E[Type II: Mutational BCC or MB-SHH]
    D -- SMO GoF --> F[Type II: SMO-driven]
    D -- SUFU LoF --> G[Type II: Downstream-driven]
    D -- All WT --> H{Cross-talk PI3K/KRAS active?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Type III: Pathway cross-talk]
    H -- No --> J[Hh pathway inactive]
    E --> K[Treat with Vismodegib]
    F --> L[Consider second-gen SMO inhibitor]
    G --> M[GLI2 inhibitor / ATO]
    I --> N[Combination therapy]
```

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Hedgehog Signalling ON / OFF State Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OFF["OFF STATE - No Ligand"]
        L0[Primary Cilium] --> P0[PTCH1 at cilium]
        P0 --> S0[SMO inhibited in vesicles]
        S0 --> G0[GLI2/3 phosphorylated]
        G0 --> PR[Partial proteolysis]
        PR --> GR[GLI Repressor form]
        GR --> RN[Represses target genes]
    end

    subgraph ON["ON STATE - Hh Ligand Present"]
        L1[SHH/IHH/DHH ligand] --> P1[Binds PTCH1]
        P1 --> SR[SMO released]
        SR --> SA[SMO active in cilium]
        SA --> GA[Full-length GLI Activator]
        GA --> AN[Transcribes target genes]
        AN --> AM[Amplification - GLI1, PTCH1, CCND1]
    end
```

```mermaid
    I --> J[SUFU releases GLI]
    J --> K[KIF7 transports GLI to ciliary tip]

```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]`

**List the three mammalian Hedgehog ligands and state two cancers in which the Hedgehog pathway is mutationally activated.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 + 2 structure):**

- **Ligands (1 mark):** **Sonic hedgehog (SHH)**, **Indian hedgehog (IHH)**, **Desert hedgehog (DHH)**.
- **Mutationally activated cancers (2 marks, any two):**
  - **Basal cell carcinoma (BCC)** – loss-of-function mutations in **PTCH1** (≈ 90 % sporadic) or gain-of-function in **SMO**.
  - **Medulloblastoma (SHH subgroup)** – PTCH1, SUFU, SMO, GLI2 alterations.
  - (Also acceptable: **Rhabdomyosarcoma** – GLI1 amplification.)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**What is the role of SUFU in the Hedgehog pathway and how does its loss contribute to cancer?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Normal role (1.5 marks):** SUFU is a **tumour-suppressor protein** that **sequesters GLI transcription factors in the cytoplasm**, preventing their nuclear translocation and target-gene activation in the absence of Hh ligand.
- **Cancer role (1.5 marks):** **Loss-of-function mutations** of SUFU remove the brake on GLI activity, leading to **ligand-independent, SMO-bypassed pathway activation**. SUFU loss is observed in **~10 % of SHH-subgroup medulloblastomas** and in **familial multiple-spine-bone-fusion syndromes with cancer predisposition**; therapeutically, such tumours are **resistant to SMO inhibitors** like Vismodegib.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module-2 Pattern]`

**(a) Describe in detail the molecular components of the Hedgehog signalling pathway in the OFF and ON states. (7 marks)**

**(b) Discuss the role of Hedgehog pathway mutations in the development of Basal Cell Carcinoma. Add a note on targeted therapy. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) OFF vs ON state mechanism (7 marks — distribute 3.5 + 3.5):**

- **OFF state (3.5 marks):**
  1. **PTCH1** localised to the primary cilium acts as the **inhibitory sink**, preventing SMO activation.
  2. **SMO** is sequestered in intracellular vesicles, conformationally inactive.
  3. **GLI2/GLI3** undergo sequential phosphorylation by **PKA → CK1 → GSK3β** at six conserved C-terminal sites.
  4. **Partial proteolysis** (by the E3 ligase **SPOP / β-TrCP**) generates the **C-terminally truncated repressor GLIᵣ**.
  5. **GLIᵣ** enters the nucleus and **represses** Hh target genes (default basal state).

- **ON state (3.5 marks):**
  1. **Hh ligand** (cholesterol + palmitoyl-modified) binds the **ECD of PTCH1** — releases SMO inhibition.
  2. **SMO** translocates to the ciliary membrane, undergoes **conformational change** and phosphorylation.
  3. **SUFU releases GLI**; KIF7/IFT proteins transport GLI to the **ciliary tip**.
  4. **Full-length GLI2/GLI3 (GLIₐ)** translocate to the nucleus as activators.
  5. **GLI1** is itself a transcriptional target → **autoregulatory positive feedback**.
  6. Target genes include **GLI1, PTCH1 (negative feedback), CCND1, MYC, BCL2, FOXM1, NANOG**.

**(b) BCC, mutations and targeted therapy (7 marks — distribute 3 + 2 + 2):**

- **PTCH1 mutation in BCC (3 marks):**
  - PTCH1 is located on **chromosome 9q22.3**, encodes a 12-transmembrane receptor with a **sterol-sensing domain (SSD)**.
  - **Inactivating mutations** (frameshift, nonsense, missense) are found in **≈ 90 % of sporadic BCC** and germline in **Gorlin (Nevoid BCC) syndrome** (autosomal dominant).
  - Loss of PTCH1 → **unopposed SMO** → constitutive GLI activation → BCC tumorigenesis.
  - **UV radiation (signature C→T transitions at dipyrimidine sites)** is the principal environmental mutagen.

- **SMO mutations in BCC (2 marks):**
  - Activating mutations in the **TMD pocket** (e.g., **W535L**, encoded on **7q32.1**) occur in **~10 % of BCCs**, conferring ligand-independent SMO signalling.
  - These mutations can also confer **resistance to Vismodegib** (drug binds the same pocket).

- **Targeted therapy (2 marks):**
  - **Vismodegib (GDC-0449)** — first-in-class SMO antagonist, **FDA approved 2012** for metastatic / locally advanced BCC.
  - **Sonidegib (LDE225)** — second SMO antagonist approved for BCC.
  - **Mechanism:** binds the **heptahelical TMD pocket of SMO**, blocking its activation.
  - **Limitations:** teratogenicity, musculoskeletal side-effects, acquired resistance via secondary SMO mutations or GLI2 amplification.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module-2 Pattern]`

**(a) Explain the three distinct mechanisms by which the Hedgehog pathway becomes oncogenic in human cancers, citing one example cancer for each. (7 marks)**

**(b) Illustrate the major mechanisms of acquired resistance to SMO inhibitors. How are they overcome experimentally? (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Three mechanisms of oncogenic Hh activation (7 marks — 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 example-mark):**

1. **Type I — Ligand-dependent (autocrine/paracrine overproduction) [2 marks]:**
   - Tumour or stromal cells overexpress **SHH**.
   - Drives **paracrine signalling** to stromal fibroblasts, which in turn provide **IGF, Wnt, VEGF** feedback.
   - **Example:** **Pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma** — SHH overexpression creates a desmoplastic, pro-tumour microenvironment.

2. **Type II — Ligand-independent (mutational) [2 marks]:**
   - Mutations in **PTCH1 (LoF), SMO (GoF), SUFU (LoF), GLI1/2 (amplification)**.
   - **Example:** **Basal cell carcinoma** (PTCH1 / SMO) and **SHH-subgroup medulloblastoma** (PTCH1, SUFU).

3. **Type III — Cross-talk / non-canonical activation [2 marks]:**
   - **KRAS-MAPK, PI3K-AKT, TGF-β** signalling can phosphorylate GLI, lowering the activation threshold.
   - **Example:** **Gastric cancer** with KRAS + Hh co-activation, or **melanoma** with BRAF / Hh cross-talk.

**(b) Resistance mechanisms to SMO inhibitors (7 marks — distribute 1.5 × 4 + 1):**

| # | Mechanism | Molecular Basis | Therapeutic Bypass |
|---|-----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **SMO secondary mutation** (D473H, W281C, Q477E) | Drug-pocket steric clash | **Second-generation SMO inhibitors** (Saridegib, Taladegib) |
| 2 | **SMO copy-number amplification** | Excess target overwhelms drug | High-dose SMO inhibitor + GLI blocker |
| 3 | **GLI2 amplification / bypass** | Pathway active downstream of SMO | **Arsenic trioxide, GANT-61** (direct GLI inhibitors) |
| 4 | **SUFU loss** | GLI unconstrained, SMO-bypassed | GLI2 inhibitor, BET inhibitors |
| 5 | **Compensatory PI3K / EGFR / AURKA** | Parallel survival pathway | **Combination therapy**: SMOi + PI3Ki or EGFRi |

(1 mark reserved for a concluding statement that *upfront combination therapy* is the emerging strategy to forestall resistance.)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mark-losing mistakes observed in KTU answer sheets for this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing PTCH1 and SMO directionality** — students frequently write that "PTCH1 activates SMO"; correct relation is **PTCH1 INHIBITS SMO** (a tumour suppressor).
> 2. **Mixing up GLI2/3 (dual-function) with GLI1 (pure activator)** — GLI1 is *only* an activator, while GLI2/3 can be either activator or repressor depending on proteolytic processing.
> 3. **Forgetting cholesterol modification of Hh ligand** — the cholesterol adduct is essential for spatial restriction (morphogen gradient), not a trivial detail.
> 4. **Writing "Vismodegib binds GLI1"** — wrong target; it binds **SMO**.
> 5. **Stating that SMO mutations cause resistance in *primary* tumours** — primary resistance arises in BCC, but *acquired* resistance typically emerges after months of therapy.
> 6. **Omitting the primary cilium** — the cilium is the obligatory subcellular platform for Hh signal transduction; this is a frequent 1-mark loss in 14-mark answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (read twice before entering the exam hall):**

- ☐ **Three ligands:** SHH, IHH, DHH (1 mark question every year).
- ☐ **PTCH1 = tumour suppressor (inhibits SMO); SMO = proto-oncogene (signal transducer).**
- ☐ **GLI1 = pure activator; GLI2/3 = activator OR repressor; SUFU = tumour suppressor.**
- ☐ **OFF state → GLIᵣ (repressor); ON state → GLIₐ (activator).**
- ☐ **Primary cilium = obligatory platform** for the pathway.
- ☐ **BCC ≈ 90 % PTCH1 LoF; Medulloblastoma SHH subgroup ≈ 25–30 % of all MBs.**
- ☐ **Gorlin syndrome (PTCH1⁺/⁻) → ≈ 90 % lifetime BCC risk.**
- ☐ **Vismodegib / Sonidegib = SMO antagonists (FDA-approved BCC).**
- ☐ **Resistance mechanisms: SMO D473H, GLI2 amp, SUFU loss, PI3K cross-talk.**
- ☐ **Direct GLI inhibitors (ATO, GANT-61) bypass SMO resistance.**
- ☐ **Three Hh-oncogenic classes: Ligand-driven / Mutational / Cross-talk.**
- ☐ **Downstream mnemonic "GAMES C" — GLI1, ACC, MYC, EGFR, SOX2, CCND1, CXCR4.**
- ☐ **Cheng–Prusoff equation** useful for KTU numerical: $K_i = \dfrac{IC_{50}}{1 + [S]/K_m}$.
- ☐ **Hill equation** $\theta = \dfrac{[L]^n}{K_d + [L]^n}$ explains the binary ON/OFF switch.
- ☐ **Cholesterol + palmitoyl modifications of Hh ligand** are essential (NOT optional).

> **Final One-Liner (memorise verbatim for 3-mark definition question):**
> *"The Hedgehog signalling pathway is a morphogen-driven, primary-cilium-based, PTCH1–SMO–GLI axis whose ligand-independent constitutive activation — through PTCH1 loss, SMO mutation, or GLI amplification — drives basal cell carcinoma, medulloblastoma, rhabdomyosarcoma, and several epithelial cancers, and is therapeutically targeted by SMO antagonists Vismodegib and Sonidegib."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT521 - CANCER BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Metastasis — The "Colonial Expansion" of Cancer

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Metastasis is the multi-step biological process by which malignant tumour cells detach from the **primary tumour**, invade the surrounding extracellular matrix (ECM), enter the circulatory or lymphatic system (**intravasation**), survive transit, exit the vasculature (**extravasation**), and establish secondary proliferative colonies (**micrometastases → macrometastases**) at distant anatomical sites.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Box:** Metastasis is responsible for approximately **90%** of cancer-related mortality. It is a hallmark of malignant (not benign) neoplasia.

**Conceptual Analogy (Plain English):**
Imagine a city (primary tumour) that has become overpopulated. Some restless citizens (cancer cells) decide to leave, board trains (bloodstream), travel to other cities (distant organs), and start new settlements (secondary tumours). However, the journey is brutal — most travellers die on the way. Only the toughest, most adaptable ones succeed. This is exactly what happens in metastasis.

---

### 1.2 The Cytoskeleton — The "Scaffolding and Motor" of the Cell

**Formal Definition:**
The cytoskeleton is a dynamic, self-assembling network of **protein filaments** present in the cytoplasm of all eukaryotic cells. It provides mechanical support, drives cell movement, organelle trafficking, and cytokinesis. In cancer, the cytoskeleton is hijacked to enable invasion.

The three principal filament systems are:
1. **Actin Filaments (Microfilaments, ~7 nm diameter)** — cell shape, lamellipodia, invadopodia
2. **Microtubules (~25 nm diameter)** — chromosome segregation, vesicular trafficking
3. **Intermediate Filaments (~10 nm diameter)** — mechanical resilience (e.g., vimentin in EMT)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Cytoskeletal Note:** All three filament systems are **polarised polymers** of globular or fibrous protein subunits, undergoing **treadmilling** (steady-state assembly at one end, disassembly at the other).

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of the cytoskeleton as a city's infrastructure:
- **Actin** = roads and pavements (movement)
- **Microtubules** = highways and railways (long-distance transport)
- **Intermediate filaments** = steel beams of buildings (structural strength)

---

### 1.3 The Cell Cycle — The "Reproduction Clock"

**Formal Definition:**
The cell cycle is the ordered series of events that takes place in a cell leading to its division and the duplication of its DNA to produce **two daughter cells**. It consists of four sequential phases: **G1 → S → G2 → M**, with regulatory checkpoints ensuring genomic fidelity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Dysregulation of the cell cycle is the **defining molecular feature** of cancer. The cell cycle is governed by **cyclins**, **cyclin-dependent kinases (CDKs)**, **CDK inhibitors (CKIs)**, and the **RB–E2F** and **p53** tumour-suppressor pathways.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
The cell cycle is like a factory's production line. There are checkpoints (quality control inspectors) at every stage. In cancer, the inspectors are bribed (RB inactivated, p53 mutated) and defective products (mutated DNA) pass through unchecked.

---

### 1.4 Standard Physical & Biological Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Duration of M phase (mitosis) | T_M | ~**1 hour** |
| Duration of S phase | T_S | ~**6–8 hours** |
| Duration of G1 phase | T_G1 | ~**11 hours** (variable) |
| Duration of G2 phase | T_G2 | ~**4–5 hours** |
| Total mammalian cell cycle | T_C | ~**24 hours** |
| Approximate cancer cell doubling time | T_d | **1–7 days** (in vivo) |
| Critical tumour size for angiogenesis | — | **1–2 mm³** |
| Hallmark of cancer genes (Hanahan & Weinberg) | — | **≥ 8 (extended)** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat:** The **24-hour** figure is an *in vitro* average. In real human tissues, cell cycle durations range from ~8 hours (intestinal crypts) to >100 days (hepatocytes).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Metastatic Cascade — Eight Sequential Steps

The "**invasion–metastasis cascade**" is a structured, sequential process. Each step represents a biological barrier that must be overcome:

1. **Detachment** — Loss of E-cadherin–mediated cell–cell adhesion (E-cadherin gene *CDH1* is frequently silenced via EMT transcription factors SNAIL, SLUG, TWIST, ZEB1/2).
2. **Invasion of the basement membrane** — Tumour cells secrete **matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-2, MMP-9, MT1-MMP)** that degrade type IV collagen.
3. **Stromal invasion** — Cytoskeletal reorganisation drives amoeboid or mesenchymal migration through the ECM.
4. **Intravasation** — Entry into blood/lymph vessels, often guided toward peritumoural vessels expressing specific chemokines (e.g., **CXCL12–CXCR4 axis**).
5. **Survival in circulation** — Cells evade anoikis (detachment-induced apoptosis) and natural killer (NK) cell attack by coating themselves with **platelets** (platelet cloaking).
6. **Arrest at distant site** — Mechanical trapping in capillary beds + adhesion via selectins and integrins.
7. **Extravasation** — Transendothelial migration, often involving **VCAM-1–VLA-4** interactions.
8. **Colonisation** — Micrometastasis formation, requiring adaptation to the foreign microenvironment (organotropism explained by the **"seed and soil"** hypothesis of Stephen Paget, 1889).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter?** The probability of forming a clinically detectable metastasis is the **product of the probabilities** of completing *each* step. Even if each step is 50% efficient, the cumulative success is only 0.5⁸ ≈ **0.4%** — explaining why metastasis is rare per cell, but lethal at the patient level.

---

### 2.2 Cytoskeletal Components — KTU High-Yield Summary

| Filament | Diameter (nm) | Monomer | Polarity | Key Function | Cancer Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Actin (F-actin) | **7** | G-actin | + and − ends | Lamellipodia, invadopodia, cytokinesis | **Invasion driver**; target of cytochalasin |
| Microtubule | **25** | α/β-tubulin heterodimer | + and − ends | Mitotic spindle, vesicle transport | **Anti-mitotic target** (taxol, vinca alkaloids) |
| Intermediate Filament | **8–12** | Vimentin, keratins, lamins, GFAP, neurofilaments | Non-polar | Mechanical resilience | **EMT marker** (vimentin ↑) |

**Treadmilling Equation (Actin & Microtubules):**

$$v_{growth} - v_{shrink} = v_{treadmill}$$

where the plus end grows at rate $v_{+}$ and the minus end shrinks at rate $v_{-}$. At **critical concentration $C_c$**, the two rates balance.

---

### 2.3 The Cell Cycle — KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula | Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Total cycle time | $T_C = T_{G1} + T_S + T_{G2} + T_M$ | Sum of all phase durations |
| Doubling time | $T_d = t / n$ | Time per population doubling |
| Population doublings | $n = t / T_d$ | Number of doublings in time $t$ |
| Growth fraction | $GF = \dfrac{N_{proliferating}}{N_{total}}$ | Fraction of cells actively cycling |
| Cell loss factor | $\Phi = 1 - \dfrac{T_{pot}}{T_d}$ | Cells lost (apoptosis/necrosis/differentiation) |
| Potential doubling time | $T_{pot} = \dfrac{\lambda \cdot T_S}{GF}$ | Doubling time if no cell loss |
| Labelling index | $LI = \dfrac{N_{S-phase}}{N_{total}}$ | Fraction in S-phase (³H-thymidine or BrdU) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Treadmilling and Cancer:** Most chemotherapeutic agents (e.g., **paclitaxel, vincristine**) target microtubule dynamics. **Paclitaxel** stabilises microtubules (no treadmilling); **vinca alkaloids** prevent polymerisation. Both freeze cells in **M phase**, triggering **mitotic catastrophe**.

---

### 2.4 Why This System Matters — Real-World Engineering/Biology Utility

- **Diagnostic pathology:** Ki-67 and PCNA immunostaining estimate Growth Fraction in tumour biopsies.
- **Drug discovery:** Over **70%** of FDA-approved chemotherapeutics target cell cycle or cytoskeletal machinery.
- **Biomarker development:** EMT markers (vimentin, N-cadherin, SNAIL) are used to score metastatic risk.
- **Medical imaging:** MRI detection threshold for metastases is ~**1 mm³** (10⁶ cells).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Cumulative Probability of Successful Metastasis

Each step $i$ of the 8-step cascade has an individual probability of success $p_i$. Assuming **statistical independence** of the steps (a reasonable first-order approximation in KTU-style problems):

**Step 1 — State the joint probability:**

$$P(\text{metastasis}) = \prod_{i=1}^{8} p_i$$

**Step 2 — Substitute example values (e.g., $p_i = 0.5$ for all steps):**

$$P(\text{metastasis}) = 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.5 \times 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Apply the exponent rule ($0.5^8$):**

$$P(\text{metastasis}) = 0.5^{8}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate numerically:**

$$P(\text{metastasis}) = \frac{1}{256} \approx 0.0039 \approx 0.4\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
A primary tumour shedding **10⁶ cells/day** yields only ~**3,900** successful metastatic colonies/day, of which most die (inefficient colonisation, typically <0.01% of seeded cells proliferate). This explains the long latency of metastases.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget the **independence assumption** breaks down in real tumours because the EMT programme correlates multiple steps (invasion, intravasation, survival). The simple product is an **upper bound** for cumulative efficiency.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cell Cycle Phase Fraction (Exponential Steady State)

For an **exponentially growing asynchronous population**, the fraction of cells in phase $i$ is:

$$f_i = \frac{\ln 2 \cdot T_i}{T_C} \approx \frac{0.693 \cdot T_i}{T_C}$$

**Step 1 — Start with the assumption of exponential growth:**

$$N(t) = N_0 \cdot 2^{t / T_d}$$

**Step 2 — Recognise that for asynchronous exponential growth, the doubling time $T_d$ equals the cycle time $T_C$ (no cell loss, $\Phi = 0$):**

$$T_d = T_C \Rightarrow T_d = T_{G1} + T_S + T_{G2} + T_M$$

**Step 3 — The age density function for an exponentially growing population is:**

$$f(a) = \frac{\ln 2}{T_C} \cdot 2^{1 - a/T_C}, \quad 0 \le a \le T_C$$

**Step 4 — Integrate over the duration of phase $i$ to obtain the phase fraction:**

$$f_i = \int_{a_i}^{a_i + T_i} \frac{\ln 2}{T_C} \cdot 2^{1 - a/T_C} \, da$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral (substitution $u = a/T_C$):**

$$f_i = \frac{\ln 2}{T_C} \cdot 2 \int_{0}^{T_i/T_C} 2^{-u} \, du$$

**Step 6 — Solve:**

$$f_i = \frac{2 \ln 2}{T_C} \cdot \left[ -\frac{T_C}{\ln 2} \cdot 2^{-u} \right]_{0}^{T_i/T_C} = 2 \cdot \left(1 - 2^{-T_i/T_C}\right)$$

**Step 7 — For $T_i \ll T_C$ (e.g., $T_S \approx 8$ h vs. $T_C \approx 24$ h), the approximation reduces to:**

$$f_i \approx \frac{\ln 2 \cdot T_i}{T_C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical Example:** With $T_S = 8$ h, $T_C = 24$ h:
> $$f_S \approx \frac{0.693 \times 8}{24} = 0.231 \approx 23.1\%$$
> This is the **S-phase fraction** measurable by BrdU/³H-thymidine labelling.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Potential Doubling Time and Cell Loss

**Step 1 — Define the steady-state proliferation rate:**

The proliferation rate per cell is $\alpha = \ln 2 / T_C$.

**Step 2 — The actual volumetric growth rate of the tumour is:**

$$\frac{1}{V} \frac{dV}{dt} = \alpha \cdot GF - \Phi \cdot \alpha \cdot GF$$

**Step 3 — Recognise that:**

$$T_{pot} = \frac{\lambda \cdot T_S}{LI}$$

where $\lambda$ is a correction factor (~**0.8** for human tumours) and $LI \approx GF$ in continuous labelling.

**Step 4 — Rearrange for the cell loss factor:**

$$\Phi = 1 - \frac{T_{pot}}{T_d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Implication:** Most solid tumours have $\Phi \approx 0.7 - 0.9$ (i.e., **70–90% of newly born cells are lost**). This is why tumour volume doubling times are much longer than cell cycle times.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cell Cycle Regulators vs. Checkpoints

| Checkpoint | Transition | Key Regulator | Substrate | Cancer Defect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Restriction Point** | G1 → S | **RB** (hypophosphorylated → active) | E2F transcription factors | RB loss → retinoblastoma, osteosarcoma |
| **G1/S checkpoint** | Late G1 | **p53** (induced by DNA damage) | p21 (Cip1/Waf1) | p53 mutation → Li-Fraumeni syndrome, >50% of cancers |
| **Intra-S checkpoint** | S | **ATR–Chk1** | Cdc25A degradation | ATR mutation → Seckel syndrome |
| **G2/M checkpoint** | G2 → M | **Cdc25C** (dephosphorylates CDK1) | CDK1–Cyclin B | Cdc25 overexpression in many cancers |
| **Spindle Assembly Checkpoint (SAC)** | Metaphase → Anaphase | **MAD2, BUB1, BUBR1, APC/C** | Securin, cyclin B | MAD2 dysregulation → chromosomal instability |
| **Post-mitotic checkpoint** | M → G1 | **p53, p21** | CDK2–Cyclin E | Tetraploidy checkpoint loss |

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Tumour Volume Doubling

**Step 1 — Number of cells in a tumour of diameter $d$ (assume sphere, cell radius $r$):**

$$N = \frac{V_{tumour}}{V_{cell}} = \frac{\frac{4}{3}\pi (d/2)^3}{\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3} = \left(\frac{d}{2r}\right)^3$$

**Step 2 — For $d = 1$ cm tumour with $r = 10\ \mu m$:**

$$N = \left(\frac{10^{-2}}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right)^3 = (500)^3 = 1.25 \times 10^8 \text{ cells}$$

**Step 3 — Number of doublings from 1 cell:**

$$n = \log_2(1.25 \times 10^8) \approx 27$$

**Step 4 — At 1 doubling/day, time to reach 1 cm:**

$$T = n \cdot T_d = 27 \times 1 = 27 \text{ days}$$

This **underestimates** real tumour growth because it ignores cell loss ($\Phi$). Real breast carcinomas take **months to years** to reach 1 cm.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**Define metastasis. List the major steps of the metastatic cascade.**

**Model Answer:**

Metastasis is the spread of cancer cells from the primary tumour to a distant organ, where they form secondary tumours. It is responsible for **~90%** of cancer deaths.

**Major steps (8-step cascade):**
1. Detachment from primary tumour (loss of E-cadherin)
2. Invasion of basement membrane (via MMPs)
3. Stromal invasion (cytoskeletal remodelling)
4. Intravasation into blood/lymph vessels
5. Survival in circulation (evading anoikis, NK cells)
6. Arrest at distant capillary bed
7. Extravasation across endothelium
8. Colonisation of distant organ (seed and soil)

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`**
**What are the three types of cytoskeletal filaments? State one function and one cancer relevance for each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Filament | Function | Cancer Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Actin (microfilaments, ~7 nm)** | Cell shape, motility, cytokinesis | Drives lamellipodia and invadopodia formation during invasion |
| **Microtubules (~25 nm)** | Chromosome segregation, vesicle transport | Target of taxanes and vinca alkaloids; defective in mitotic checkpoint |
| **Intermediate filaments (~10 nm)** | Mechanical strength | Vimentin upregulation is a marker of epithelial–mesenchymal transition (EMT) |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 3, Choice 1**

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adapted]`**

**(a)** With a neat labelled diagram, describe the **phases of the cell cycle** and explain the **molecular regulation of the G1/S checkpoint**. Highlight the role of **p53, RB, and cyclin–CDK complexes**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of the **metastatic cascade**. Discuss the role of **E-cadherin**, **matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs)**, and the **seed-and-soil hypothesis** in the establishment of secondary tumours. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Cell Cycle Phases and G1/S Regulation (7 marks):**

The eukaryotic cell cycle consists of four phases: **G1 (Gap 1, ~11 h)**, **S (Synthesis, ~6–8 h)**, **G2 (Gap 2, ~4–5 h)**, and **M (Mitosis, ~1 h)**. Cells can exit into **G0** (quiescence).

The **G1/S checkpoint** (also called the **Restriction Point** in late G1) commits the cell to DNA replication. Key regulators:

- **RB (Retinoblastoma protein):** In its hypophosphorylated state, RB binds and sequesters **E2F** transcription factors, preventing transcription of S-phase genes (cyclin E, DNA polymerase, thymidine kinase).
- **Cyclin D–CDK4/6:** Phosphorylates RB early in G1.
- **Cyclin E–CDK2:** Completes RB phosphorylation, releasing E2F.
- **p53:** The "guardian of the genome". Upon DNA damage, p53 is stabilised, induces transcription of **p21 (Cip1/Waf1)**, which inhibits Cyclin E–CDK2 and Cyclin A–CDK2, arresting the cell in G1.
- **p16 (INK4a):** A tumour suppressor that inhibits CDK4/6, preventing RB phosphorylation.

In cancer: **RB loss** (retinoblastoma), **p53 mutation** (>50% of cancers), **CDK4/6 amplification** (breast cancer), and **p16 deletion** (melanoma, pancreatic cancer) all drive unchecked G1/S transition.

**(b) Metastatic Cascade and Seed-and-Soil (7 marks):**

The **metastatic cascade** is the sequential 8-step process (detachment → basement membrane invasion → stromal invasion → intravasation → survival in circulation → arrest → extravasation → colonisation) by which tumour cells disseminate.

**E-cadherin:** A calcium-dependent transmembrane glycoprotein that mediates **homotypic cell–cell adhesion** in epithelial tissues via adherens junctions. Downregulation of E-cadherin (via transcriptional repression by **SNAIL, SLUG, ZEB1, ZEB2, TWIST**) is a hallmark of the **epithelial–mesenchymal transition (EMT)**, conferring motility and invasiveness. Loss of *CDH1* is the genetic basis of **hereditary diffuse gastric cancer**.

**Matrix Metalloproteinases (MMPs):** A family of zinc-dependent endopeptidases (notably **MMP-2, MMP-9, MT1-MMP**) that cleave type IV collagen of the basement membrane, enabling stromal invasion. Activity is regulated by **TIMPs (Tissue Inhibitors of Metalloproteinases)**; imbalance favours invasion.

**Seed-and-Soil Hypothesis (Paget, 1889):** Proposes that successful metastasis depends not only on tumour cell properties ("seed") but also on the compatibility of the distant microenvironment ("soil"). Examples:
- Prostate cancer → bone (rich in growth factors, e.g., TGF-β)
- Breast cancer → bone, brain, lung, liver
- Colon cancer → liver (via portal circulation)
- Melanoma → lung, brain, liver

Molecular basis: organ-specific chemokines (e.g., **CXCL12** in bone, lung, liver, brain) attract tumour cells expressing matching receptors (e.g., **CXCR4**), and locally secreted growth factors support colonisation.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 3, Choice 2 (Internal Choice)

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Adapted]`**

**(a)** Describe the **structure and functions of the three cytoskeletal filaments** in eukaryotic cells. Explain how each contributes to **tumour cell invasion and metastasis**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Growth Fraction (GF)** of a tumour? Derive the relationship between **potential doubling time ($T_{pot}$)**, **S-phase duration ($T_S$)**, and **labelling index (LI)**. A tumour has $T_S = 9$ h, LI = 0.05, $\lambda = 0.8$. Find $T_{pot}$ and comment on its clinical significance. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Cytoskeletal Filaments and Tumour Invasion (7 marks):**

**1. Actin Filaments (Microfilaments, 7 nm):**
- Built from **G-actin monomers** that polymerise into double-helical F-actin.
- Functions: cell shape, cortical tension, lamellipodia/filopodia formation, cytokinesis (contractile ring), muscle contraction.
- Cancer role: At the leading edge of migrating tumour cells, **Arp2/3 complex** nucleates branched actin networks, propelling the membrane forward via **lamellipodia**. **Invadopodia** are actin-rich protrusions that secrete MMPs to degrade ECM. The **Rho GTPase family** (RhoA, Rac1, Cdc42) controls actin dynamics; their dysregulation is oncogenic.

**2. Microtubules (25 nm):**
- Hollow cylinders of **α/β-tubulin heterodimers**, with a dynamic plus end and stable minus end (anchored at the centrosome).
- Functions: mitotic spindle, vesicular transport (via kinesin/dynein motors), cell polarity, cilia/flagella.
- Cancer role: Spindle apparatus is essential for accurate chromosome segregation. **Taxol** (paclitaxel) hyper-stabilises microtubules; **vinca alkaloids** (vincristine, vinblastine) prevent polymerisation. Both freeze cells in **M phase** → **mitotic catastrophe** → apoptosis. The **Spindle Assembly Checkpoint (SAC)** proteins (MAD2, BUB1, BUBR1) are frequently dysregulated in cancers, causing **aneuploidy**.

**3. Intermediate Filaments (8–12 nm):**
- Non-polar, rope-like polymers of fibrous proteins: **keratins** (epithelial), **vimentin** (mesenchymal), **lamins** (nuclear), **GFAP** (glial), **neurofilaments** (neurons), **desmin** (muscle).
- Functions: mechanical resilience, anchoring organelles, protection from shear stress.
- Cancer role: **Vimentin** is a classical **EMT marker**; its upregulation correlates with increased motility and poor prognosis. **Cytokeratins** are used in **tumour immunohistochemistry** to identify tissue of origin (e.g., CK7, CK20 patterns).

**(b) Growth Fraction and Potential Doubling Time (7 marks):**

**Definition:** The **Growth Fraction (GF)** is the ratio of actively proliferating cells to the total cell population of a tumour:

$$GF = \frac{N_{proliferating}}{N_{total}}$$

Only cells in G1, S, G2, and M contribute to proliferation; cells in G0 are quiescent.

**Derivation of $T_{pot}$:**

The **labelling index (LI)** is the fraction of cells in S-phase, detected by ³H-thymidine or BrdU incorporation:

$$LI \approx GF \cdot \frac{T_S}{T_C}$$

The **potential doubling time** is the doubling time the tumour would have **if there were no cell loss** ($\Phi = 0$):

$$T_{pot} = \frac{\lambda \cdot T_S}{LI}$$

where $\lambda$ is a correction factor (~**0.8** for human tumours) accounting for the non-uniform age distribution.

The relationship between the actual doubling time and the potential doubling time is:

$$T_d = \frac{T_{pot}}{1 - \Phi} \quad \text{or} \quad \Phi = 1 - \frac{T_{pot}}{T_d}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $T_S = 9$ h, $LI = 0.05$, $\lambda = 0.8$.

$$T_{pot} = \frac{\lambda \cdot T_S}{LI} = \frac{0.8 \times 9}{0.05} = \frac{7.2}{0.05} = 144 \text{ hours} = 6 \text{ days}$$

**Clinical Significance:**
- $T_{pot} = 6$ days means that, **without cell loss**, the tumour would double every 6 days.
- In reality, most solid tumours have **cell loss factors $\Phi = 0.7 - 0.9$**, so the observed doubling time is **7–10 times longer** (i.e., **42–60 days**).
- $T_{pot}$ is **prognostically important**: a high $T_{pot}$ (rapid proliferation) suggests responsiveness to **S-phase-specific chemotherapeutics** (e.g., 5-FU, methotrexate, cytarabine).
- A low $T_{pot}$ does **not** mean good prognosis — it may reflect low GF, which limits cytotoxic efficacy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing $T_d$ and $T_{pot}$:** Many students write $T_d = T_{pot}$; in fact, $T_d \ge T_{pot}$ always, with equality only if $\Phi = 0$.
> 2. **Ignoring the correction factor $\lambda$:** Setting $\lambda = 1$ overestimates $T_{pot}$ by 25% — a 1–2 mark penalty in KTU valuation.
> 3. **Forgetting units:** Always express $T_{pot}$ in hours **or** days, **not** both.
> 4. **Omitting arrows in the metastatic cascade diagrams:** Flow arrows are mandatory for full marks in KTU 2024.
> 5. **Misnaming checkpoint proteins:** "p53" is *not* a cyclin, "RB" is *not* a kinase. Memorise the regulatory class of each molecule.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 3, "Metastasis, Cytoskeleton, Cell Cycle"**

- **Metastasis kills ~90% of cancer patients** — the 8-step cascade: Detachment → BM invasion → Stromal invasion → Intravasation → Circulation survival → Arrest → Extravasation → Colonisation.
- **Cytoskeleton = 3 filaments:** Actin (7 nm, movement), Microtubules (25 nm, mitosis), Intermediate filaments (10 nm, strength).
- **EMT** = E-cadherin ↓, Vimentin ↑, MMPs ↑, SNAIL/SLUG/TWIST ↑.
- **Seed and Soil** (Paget, 1889) explains organotropism (prostate→bone, colon→liver).
- **Cell cycle phases:** G1 (~11 h) → S (~6–8 h) → G2 (~4–5 h) → M (~1 h); total ~24 h *in vitro*.
- **Checkpoints:** G1/S (RB–E2F, p53–p21), Intra-S (ATR–Chk1), G2/M (Cdc25–CDK1), SAC (MAD2–APC/C).
- **Key cancer genes at checkpoints:** *RB* (retinoblastoma), *TP53* (>50% cancers), *CDKN2A* (p16, melanoma), *MAD2* (aneuploidy).
- **Growth Fraction (GF)** = proliferating / total cells.
- **Labelling Index (LI)** = S-phase fraction; measured by BrdU or ³H-thymidine.
- **Potential Doubling Time:** $T_{pot} = \lambda T_S / LI$; with $\lambda \approx 0.8$ for humans.
- **Cell Loss Factor:** $\Phi = 1 - T_{pot}/T_d$; most solid tumours have $\Phi \approx 0.7 - 0.9$.
- **Treadmilling:** Both actin and microtubules undergo dynamic assembly/disassembly; key drug target.
- **Tumour volume of 1 cm³ ≈ 10⁹ cells ≈ 30 population doublings** from a single cell.
- **Critical tumour size for angiogenesis:** ~1–2 mm³ (Folkman threshold).
- **Chemotherapeutics targeting cytoskeleton:** Taxanes (microtubule stabilisers) and Vinca alkaloids (microtubule destabilisers).
- **Probabilistic nature of metastasis:** Product of step-wise probabilities; very inefficient per cell, lethal at patient level.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Regulation of Cell Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBBT521 - CANCER BIOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Regulation of Cell Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 🔬 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Cell Cycle Regulation** is the highly orchestrated, molecular surveillance system that governs the sequential progression of a eukaryotic cell through the phases of the cell cycle (**G1 → S → G2 → M**), ensuring faithful DNA replication, accurate chromosome segregation, and controlled cellular proliferation.

The molecular machinery is driven by a family of **serine/threonine kinases** called **Cyclin-Dependent Kinases (CDKs)**, whose enzymatic activity is strictly dependent on binding to regulatory subunits known as **Cyclins**, and is further modulated by **CDK Inhibitors (CKIs)**, **phosphorylation/dephosphorylation events**, and **ubiquitin-mediated proteolysis**.

## 🌐 Conceptual Analogy — "The Train Conductor"

Imagine a high-speed bullet train traveling through four stations: **G1 → S → G2 → M**.

| Component | Analogy Role | Real Molecular Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| 🚂 **Engine** | Provides power to move forward | **CDK** (Cyclin-Dependent Kinase) |
| 🔑 **Ignition Key** | Activates the engine | **Cyclin** (binds to CDK) |
| 🛑 **Signal Lights** | Tell the train to stop or go | **Checkpoints** (G1/S, G2/M, Spindle) |
| 👮 **Traffic Police** | Inspect and approve movement | **Restraining proteins** (Rb, p53, p21) |
| ♻️ **Recycling Crew** | Dispose of old keys after use | **APC/C & SCF Ubiquitin Ligases** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Dysregulation of this tightly controlled system is a **defining hallmark of cancer**. Loss of checkpoint integrity allows cells with damaged DNA to proliferate uncontrollably, leading to genomic instability.

### 🎯 The Master Regulators (Highlight Constants)

- **CDK1 (Cdc2)** – Master mitotic kinase; binds **Cyclin B** to form **MPF (Maturation Promoting Factor)**.
- **CDK2, CDK4, CDK6** – Drive **G1 → S transition**.
- **Cyclin D** – **G1 phase** cyclin; sensor of extracellular growth signals.
- **Cyclin E** – **G1/S transition** cyclin.
- **Cyclin A** – **S and G2 phase** cyclin.
- **Cyclin B** – **G2/M transition** cyclin.
- **p53** – "Guardian of the Genome"; induces cell cycle arrest at G1/S.
- **Rb (Retinoblastoma protein)** – Master G1 brake; sequesters **E2F** transcription factor.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 🧩 The Four Pillars of Cell Cycle Control

### 🟢 Pillar 1: Cyclin–CDK Complexes (The Core Engine)

The cell cycle is propelled by the periodic synthesis and degradation of **cyclins**, which bind to and activate constitutively expressed **CDKs**.

$$\text{Cyclin (regulatory)} + \text{CDK (catalytic)} \rightarrow \text{Cyclin-CDK Complex (Active Kinase)}$$

- **Full activation** also requires phosphorylation by **CAK (CDK-Activating Kinase)** on a conserved **Thr160** residue (in CDK2).
- **Inhibition** is achieved via **Wee1 kinase** phosphorylation on **Thr14** and **Tyr15**; relief requires **Cdc25 phosphatase**.

### 🔴 Pillar 2: Checkpoint Surveillance Systems

> [!NOTE]
> **Checkpoints** are biochemical signal-transduction pathways that halt cell cycle progression when critical events are incomplete or DNA is damaged.

| Checkpoint | Phase | Major Sensors | Primary Effectors | Biological Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **G1/S (Restriction Point)** | Late G1 | ATM/ATR, p53 | p21, Rb | Prevents replication of damaged DNA |
| **Intra-S Checkpoint** | S phase | ATR, Chk1 | Cdc25A degradation | Slows stalled replication forks |
| **G2/M Checkpoint** | G2/M boundary | ATM/ATR, p53 | Cdc25C inactivation, p21 | Prevents mitosis with damaged DNA |
| **Spindle Assembly Checkpoint (SAC)** | M phase | Mad/Bub proteins at kinetochores | Securin, Cyclin B stabilization | Ensures accurate chromosome segregation |

### 🟡 Pillar 3: CDK Inhibitors (CKIs) — The Molecular Brakes

Two structurally distinct families of CKIs halt the cell cycle:

- **CIP/KIP Family** (broad specificity): **p21 (CDKN1A)**, **p27 (CDKN1B)**, **p57** — inhibit Cyclin E-CDK2, Cyclin A-CDK2, Cyclin B-CDK1.
- **INK4 Family** (specific to G1 CDKs): **p16 (CDKN2A)**, **p15**, **p18**, **p19** — specifically inhibit **CDK4** and **CDK6**, preventing Cyclin D association.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** **p16 (INK4a)** and **p53** are the **two most frequently mutated tumor suppressors** in human cancer. Loss of p16 → unregulated Cyclin D-CDK4/6 → constitutive Rb phosphorylation → unrestrained E2F → uncontrolled G1→S.

### 🔵 Pillar 4: Ubiquitin-Mediated Proteolysis (The Recycling Crew)

Cyclins are destroyed at specific cell cycle transitions via the **Ubiquitin-Proteasome System (UPS)**.

- **SCF Complex (Skp1–Cullin–F-box)** – targets **Cyclin E, p27, Cdc25A** for degradation at G1/S.
- **APC/C (Anaphase-Promoting Complex/Cyclosome)** – targets **Securin** and **Cyclin B** for degradation at metaphase→anaphase transition.

## 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table summarizes all critical molecular players. **No vertical pipes `$\vert$` are used inside cells**; we use the LaTeX `\vert` or `\mid` to prevent markdown parsing errors.

| S.No. | Complex / Protein | Cell Cycle Role | Activates/Inhibits | Disease Link (Cancer) |
|:---:|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cyclin D – CDK4/6** | Early G1 progression | Activates by phosphorylating Rb | Overexpressed in breast cancer |
| 2 | **Cyclin E – CDK2** | G1 → S transition | Initiates DNA replication | Amplified in ovarian & breast cancer |
| 3 | **Cyclin A – CDK2** | S phase progression | Phosphorylates replication factors | Upregulated in hepatocellular carcinoma |
| 4 | **Cyclin B – CDK1 (MPF)** | G2 → M transition | Triggers mitosis | Aberrant in many malignancies |
| 5 | **p21 (CIP1)** | CKI — broad inhibitor | Inhibits Cyclin E/A-CDK2 & Cyclin B-CDK1 | Loss promotes tumor progression |
| 6 | **p27 (KIP1)** | CKI — G1/S brake | Blocks Cyclin E-CDK2; mislocalized in cancer | Reduced levels correlate with poor prognosis |
| 7 | **p16 (INK4a)** | CKI — INK4 family | Selectively inhibits CDK4/6 | Deleted/methylated in ~50% of cancers |
| 8 | **Rb (Retinoblastoma)** | G1 master brake | Binds and sequesters E2F | Biallelic loss in retinoblastoma |
| 9 | **p53 (TP53)** | "Guardian of the genome" | Transactivates p21; induces apoptosis | Mutated in >50% of all human cancers |
| 10 | **APC/C** | E3 ubiquitin ligase | Targets Securin & Cyclin B for degradation | Dysregulated in colorectal cancer |
| 11 | **E2F** | Transcription factor | Drives S-phase gene expression | Hyperactive when Rb is lost |
| 12 | **Cdc25 Phosphatase** | Activates CDKs | Removes inhibitory phosphates (Thr14, Tyr15) | Overexpressed in many cancers |

### 🌍 Real-World Utility in Oncology & Biotechnology

- **Selective CDK4/6 inhibitors** (e.g., **Palbociclib**, **Ribociclib**, **Abemaciclib**) are FDA-approved for **HR+ HER2− metastatic breast cancer**.
- **Proteasome inhibitors** (e.g., **Bortezomib**) target the UPS, indirectly disrupting cyclin degradation in multiple myeloma.
- **Synthetic lethality** approaches exploit checkpoint defects (e.g., **PARP inhibitors** in BRCA-mutant tumors rely on defective G2/M checkpoint).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 🔄 Analytical Walkthrough: The Rb–E2F Axis (G1/S Transition)

This is the most critical regulatory node in mammalian cell cycle control. We will derive the logic step-by-step.

### Step 1: Quiescent State (G0 / Early G1)

In the absence of mitogenic signals, **Rb (Retinoblastoma protein)** is present in its **active, hypophosphorylated form** (denoted **pRb**). Active pRb binds the **E2F** family of transcription factors, recruiting chromatin-remodeling complexes (HDACs, SWI/SNF) that **repress** transcription of S-phase genes.

$$ \text{pRb (hypophosphorylated)} + \text{E2F} \rightarrow \text{Repressive Complex} \rightarrow \text{No S-phase transcription} $$

### Step 2: Mitogenic Stimulation

Growth factors (e.g., EGF, PDGF) trigger **Ras-MAPK signaling**, inducing transcription of **Cyclin D**. Cyclin D then binds **CDK4** and **CDK6**.

$$ \text{Growth Factor} \rightarrow \text{Ras/MAPK} \rightarrow \text{Cyclin D transcription} $$

### Step 3: Initial Rb Phosphorylation (Mono-phosphorylation)

The **Cyclin D – CDK4/6** complex **mono-phosphorylates** Rb, producing multiple **hypophosphorylated Rb species**. This intermediate state **relieves repression** on a subset of E2F target genes, most importantly **Cyclin E**.

$$ \text{Cyclin D-CDK4/6} + \text{pRb} \rightarrow \text{pRb-p} + \text{ADP} $$

### Step 4: Positive Feedback Loop (Cyclin E Induction & Rb Hyperphosphorylation)

Newly synthesized **Cyclin E** binds **CDK2**. The active **Cyclin E-CDK2** complex **hyper-phosphorylates Rb**, completely inactivating it and releasing **E2F** to drive full S-phase gene expression.

$$ \text{Cyclin E-CDK2} + \text{pRb-p} \rightarrow \text{ppRb (inactive)} \rightarrow \text{Free E2F} $$

### Step 5: Autoregulatory Reinforcement

Cyclin E-CDK2 also phosphorylates **p27 (KIP1)**, marking it for **SCF-mediated ubiquitination** and degradation. This removes the brake on CDK2 activity, creating a **positive feedback loop**.

$$ \text{Cyclin E-CDK2} \xrightarrow{\text{phosphorylates}} \text{p27} \xrightarrow{\text{SCF}^{\text{Skp2}}} \text{Ubiquitination} \rightarrow \text{26S Proteasome Degradation} $$

### Step 6: S-phase Entry & Irreversibility

The cell is now **committed** to DNA replication. **Cyclin A** is synthesized, binds CDK2, and drives S-phase progression. The **Restriction Point** has been irreversibly passed.

### Step 7: Why This Is a Cancer "Switch"

Mathematically, the positive feedback loop creates a **bistable switch**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } x &= \text{Cyclin E-CDK2 activity} \\
\text{Let } y &= \text{Rb phosphorylation state} \\
\text{Then:} \quad \frac{dx}{dt} &= k_1 \cdot (\text{Active E2F}) - k_2 \cdot \text{p27} \\
\frac{dy}{dt} &= k_3 \cdot x \cdot (1 - y) - k_4 \cdot y \\
\end{aligned}
$$

> This system exhibits **two stable steady states** (G1 arrest vs. S-phase entry), ensuring that once the **Restriction Point** is crossed, the cell does not revert to quiescence. Loss of **p16** or **Rb** collapses the system into a single "always-on" state → **uncontrolled proliferation**.

## 🧮 Step-by-Step Logic for G2/M Transition (MPF Activation)

The **Maturation Promoting Factor (MPF)** is a heterodimer of **Cyclin B** and **CDK1 (Cdc2)**. Its activation is governed by a beautifully tight regulatory circuit.

**Step A — Cyclin B Accumulation:** Cyclin B protein levels rise progressively through S and G2 phases.

**Step B — Initial Inactive Complex Formation:** Cyclin B binds CDK1, but the complex is held in an **inactive state** by **Wee1 kinase** and **Myt1 kinase**, which phosphorylate **Thr14** and **Tyr15** in the ATP-binding loop of CDK1.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cyclin B} + \text{CDK1} &\rightarrow \text{Pre-MPF (inactive)} \\
\text{Wee1/Myt1:} \quad \text{Pre-MPF} + \text{ATP} &\rightarrow \text{CDK1-P}^{\text{Thr14,Tyr15}} \text{ (Inactive)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step C — Amplification Switch via Cdc25:** A small amount of active MPF activates **Cdc25 phosphatase**, which removes the inhibitory phosphates. Active MPF then activates more Cdc25, creating a **positive feedback amplification loop**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Active MPF} \xrightarrow{\text{activates}} \text{Cdc25} \xrightarrow{\text{dephosphorylates}} \text{More MPF} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step D — Mitotic Entry:** Once the threshold is crossed, the cell rapidly enters mitosis. Substrates of MPF include **nuclear lamins** (nuclear envelope breakdown), **Golgi matrix proteins**, and **condensin complexes** (chromosome condensation).

**Step E — Anaphase Exit via APC/C:** The **Spindle Assembly Checkpoint** monitors kinetochore-microtubule attachment. Once all chromosomes are bi-oriented, the **APC/C** E3 ubiquitin ligase (activated by **Cdc20**) targets **Securin** (an inhibitor of separase) and **Cyclin B** for **K48-linked polyubiquitination** and proteasomal destruction.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{APC/C}^{\text{Cdc20}} + \text{Securin} &\rightarrow \text{Securin-Ub}_{\text{n}} \rightarrow \text{Proteasome} \rightarrow \text{Separase Active} \\
\text{APC/C}^{\text{Cdc20}} + \text{Cyclin B} &\rightarrow \text{Cyclin B-Ub}_{\text{n}} \rightarrow \text{Proteasome} \rightarrow \text{CDK1 Inactive} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Active separase cleaves **cohesin** rings, triggering **sister chromatid separation**. Loss of Cyclin B inactivates CDK1, allowing exit from mitosis.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
